D.C. What sparked your interest in code?
66E.v.B. My older brother, Petr, is twelve years older,
and he became interested in computers early on. In the
late 1970s, at the same time personal computers were
being developed in California, he and some friends started5
building small computers based on the [MOS Technology]
6502 processor. They started making kits, so I built one.
I didn’t fully understand how it worked, but I knew the
major components and how it was made.
67After that, <Apple><G>9, 50 released the <Macintosh>,10
and my <brother>10 bought one. Around 1988, I bought
my first Mac SE, while I began studying graphic design at
the Royal Academy of Art, where Gerrit Noordzij taught
type design. Just [van Rossum] was one year ahead of me,
and thanks to Noordzij, we quickly understood that we15
were both into computers. At the time, nobody else was,
there were no computers at school. We tried to create
simple graphics on the computers, and Just figured out
how to write <PostScript><G>66 . I was surrounded by people
that were much faster than me on computers, so it was20
a very stimulating environment for learning.
68Just and I ended up in Berlin, at Spiekermann’s
small studio. There were some computers and a laser

Interview with Erik van Blokland A.B 67
.B
ERIK VAN BLOKLAND
printer, and not much to do besides hanging out at
the office. We knew that fonts were code, so we opened
files in a text editor and looked at what was going on,
how it all worked. We started manipulating the shapes
with the code, experimenting with a font made of a 5
single rectangular character. We moved the anchor points
around, as a sort of proof of concept, and tried to apply
this knowledge and concept to an actual font, but at the
time it was really difficult to get font data, because it was
encrypted. The technical specs would not be published 10
as open source for another two years. The only font data
we had real access to was the typeface I had created
for the Academy graphic design exam, which was a not
very well adjusted text face. We made the first version
of Beowolf in this manner. 15
D.C. Can you tell us a bit more about that?
70 E.v.B. We were mainly driven by technical curiosity,
how cool it would be if we could get this to work. What
it looked like came out as a surprise. So we reflected on it,
and came up with some backstory, because we wanted to 20
show that it wasn’t just a technical invention. Still, it was
much less radical than some people thought at the time.
We then went to a conference in Oxford, showed some
slides and talked about fonts being able to move, which
was new at the time. We wrote an article for Emigre 25
Magazine that Donald Knuth read. He wrote us a letter,
saying he really liked the article and sent some examples
of font experiments he had done before: splitting type-
faces into sticks and bowls and moving them around, the
whole <Metafont> <G>54thing. To our embarrassment, we had 30
no idea who he was at the time. We were twenty-two, we
hadn’t heard of him, so we forgot about it.

Years later, as <Python><G>69 was developed, we
learned about <Metafont><G>54 , and we kind of remembered
the letter. After a few years, I finally met <Knuth>11 at
a conference. I gathered up my courage and went to tell
him the story, saying how young and stupid we had been5
at the time, and that we were sorry we didn’t answer.
He replied he had no idea what I was talking about, so I
thought I might have imagined everything, but I found
the letter a couple of years later, and finally realized
that it was indeed him. I think ideas float around, and10
more than one person can connect with them. I read that
<Knuth>11 got his idea for <Metafont><G>54 after meeting
with Marjan Unger at Stanford, and talking about how
fashion moves in a cycle. The modularity in his program
is inspired by punk concepts in graphic design.15

ERIK VAN BLOKLAND
the side of the people who built this technology, the ones

D.C. What was the idea behind variable fonts? 10
74 E.v.B. Basically, <Google > <G>34dusted off a method of font
compression used to make files fit on floppy disks. This
only makes sense if you’re <Google > <G>34, serving billions of
fonts a day from your servers and wanting to harvest
data in the process. It’s a huge investment for them to 15
```
make these files available at all times; in addition, if
```
they are a bit smaller, it has a huge impact on the cost
in the end.
75 Nevertheless, aside from these economic consid-
erations, I think there is a lot of freedom that needs to 20
be explored with variable fonts. The majority of reading
happens onscreen, and that will continue to increase. The
pixels that form the text on the screen can be controlled,
they can have a logic, and they can move. What that
logic and movement should be is an entirely fresh field, 25
because type has never moved before. It was unstable,
it was variable in a sense: lettering, calligraphy and ty-
pography have shown us that letter shapes could vary.
However, the fact that this type can now breathe on
a screen is new, and so there are new things to learn 30
from that.

A.B 70 Part A: Interview B
INTERVIEWS
A
76Our optical nerves are most effective at detect-
ing movement, even before being able to see color, or
even focus. Our ancestors developed this capacity as a
defense mechanism, when perceiving the movement of
a potential predator was crucial to our survival. This5
makes movement a powerful tool for us as designers, en-
abling us to direct the attention of the viewer in a very
effective way.
D.C. It seems that Adobe products are still are the
most commonly used in the profession. Do you think10
creative coding can help a graphic designer shape their
own tools? How do you think things will evolve
regarding specialized tools?
78E.v.B. I don’t think you have to create your own In-
Design or <Photoshop><G>6 . It’s all a matter of circum-15
```
stances: if these tools do what you need, are useful in
```
your workflow and make sense from an economical point
of view, you should use them. What is required is to
have a critical position with regard to those tools, their
use should be a conscious decision. The first thing to20
understand are the options available, as well as their
limitations.

B.C
People need to understand that computers can
be instructed to do different, unpredictable things. You
can use that in the design process: you code without
knowing exactly what the result might be, you generate
stuff that surprises you. That is something that amazes 20
me about a lot of generative work. When I look at the
outcome of a program I wrote, I’m seeing the thing
for the first time. Even though I made it line by line,
the result is almost always different from what I had
imagined. Again, that’s not the only way to make cool 25
images, but that works particularly well for me.
D.C. How would you define creative coding?
83 E.v.B. Creative coding is different from software engi-
neering. If you open <DrawBot> <G>21, you will probably start
out with a line of code in mind that will have almost 30
nothing to do with what the overall program outcome
will be. It’s very different from writing a framework or
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.B 72 Part A: Interview B
INTERVIEWS
A
```
a file format; that stuff has to work, be effective and
```
follow engineering practices, but you don’t have these
constraints with creative coding. You focus on the few
lines that caused that particular thing, but there are
things that you want to have work differently, so you5
try to modify some part, or structure it differently. Here,
writing the code and designing the thing are part of the
same process. You cannot imagine the shape it will take
when you look at the code, but you can imagine what
the code needs to do when you look at the shapes. That’s10
how <DrawBot><G>21, 68 works, that’s how <Processing> works.
I think that’s what creative coding is.

<GG>12 WHO·IS ·< PETR·VAN·BLOKLAND >?
You'll · find · a · man · who · is · at · once ·calm, · charismatic · and, · dare · we · say · it, · rather · Zen, · sitting · down ·
at · a · desk · with · a · few · items · placed · atop · a · low · table · that ·would · leave · an · ordinary · person · with · a · hernia. · The · items · sit · at ·
the · edge · of · a · computer · that · might · fit · in · the · hand · of · a ·three-year-old · child… · But · there · is · no · child · to · be · found. ·
Indeed, · the · man, · company · and · brand · sits · at · a · vast · desk · in ·the · middle · of · a · corporate · suite, · surrounded · by · the ·
world's · fastest · computers. · It · has · to · be · seen · to · be · believed.So · who · is · Petr · van · Blokland?
```
Perhaps · the · biggest · mystery · of · the · modern · car · industry · (where ·more · than · one · industry · has · been · known · as · the · "myth · of · the ·
```
```
perfect · race · car") · is · the · role · of · tire · development · and, · more ·importantly, · the · tire · designer. · The · best, · as · it · always · had · to ·
```
```
be · believed, · were · the · "invisible · heroes" · at · Michelin. · The · fact ·that · a · company · of · around · 24000 · (!) · people · with · a · rather · large ·
```
footprint · does · the · tire · testing · and · development · for · all · major ·brands · on · a · day · to · day · basis · certainly · has · its · own · mystique. ·
And · what · did · we · discover? · Quite · a · lot, · including · the · fact ·that · it · all · starts · and · ends · with · one · man.
At · the · risk · of · giving · the · game · away, · van · Blokland · has · played · a ·key · role · in · the · development · of · most · of · the · major · car · brands ·
on · the · world's · roads. · All · of · which · makes · him · possibly · the ·world's · most · famous · non-driving · automotive · engineer, · let ·
alone · tire · engineer. · When · you · think · about · it, · it · shouldn't · have ·been · possible. · Just · look · at · the · cars · these · tire · designers · have ·
come · from: · Citroen · DS, · Renault · 4XV, · Fiat · Uno, · Peugeot · 106, ·Mitsubishi · Largo, · Suzuki · Esteem, · Porsche · 944 · and · 924 · and ·
Mercedes-Benz · S-Class.But · somehow · they · made · it · and · were · the · first · to · the · finish · line: ·
from · the · first · tests · of · the · original · Formula · 1 · series · to · winning ·Pikes · Peak · 24 · times.
Even · when · that · competition · went · elsewhere · the · tires · stayed ·around. · In · fact, · almost · all · of · these · cars · were · developed · on ·
these · tires, · most · of · which · ended · up · as · road · cars, · before ·being · upgraded · to · other · brands.
And, · of · course, · these · tires · continued · to · win · everywhere, ·including · the · GT4 · class · championship · of · GT4 · in · Europe · – · where · it ·
proved · that · there · wasn't · anything · these · man-made · creations ·could · not · do…

Petr
van Blokland
Interviewed by
Demian Conrad
```
<Petr van Blokland>12 (b. 1956) works together with designer
```
Claudia Mens and others, depending on the type of project. He
graduated in 1980 cum laude with a degree in Graphic Design from
KABK/The Hague and went on to study industrial design in Delft.
His main focus is on typography, type design, designing the design
process, programming and design theory. His current work includes
international projects related to typography and type. He is a
cofounder of webtype.com, and typenetwork.com for which a range
of software design tools have been developed. He also created the
type foundry TYPETR, and has lectured at various academies at
both under- and postgraduate levels. Van Blokland has received
the Charles Peignot Award for typography from AtypI in 1988 and
was a board member of the organization for a number of years.
Interview with Petr van Blokland A.C 75
Demian Conrad Welcome, Petr van Blokland, I’m
very pleased to have you here. I would like to start
by asking you to introduce yourself and your practice.
86 Petr van Blokland Thank you for inviting me. My
name is Petr van Blokland and I do a lot of things in my 5
```
practice: type design, typography, coding and graphic
```
design. I started out in the late 1970s. I studied graphic
design at the Royal Academy of the Hague [KABK]
from 1975 to 1980. After I graduated, I did internships
at Studio Dumbar and Total Design Amsterdam. Since 10
1981, I have been running a studio with my wife, Claudia
Mens, who is an interior and environmental designer. We
each manage our own side of the studio, with a lot of
overlap. In the 1980s, I studied industrial design in Delft,
but I never completed the program. I’m not an engineer, 15
but it helped a lot in terms of my conception of design
as an industrial process and not so much as an art form.
I try to cover the entire spectrum between graphic design
and engineering, and I have been programming since
1980. I built my own computer five years before the first 20
<Macintosh> <G>50came out.
87 Over twenty-five years, we employed between
three to five people in our studio, mostly working on cor-
porate design projects. We must have had about twenty-
five people who worked with us since the studio opened. 25
Over the last decade, we have changed direction: now we
collaborate remotely with designers and former students
from all over the world on a variety of projects.

P.v.B. I have been teaching Graphic Design at the
undergraduate level at KABK since 1986, and also teach
through the TypeMedia master’s program in type design.5
I also taught in the Graphic Design master’s program
at the St. Joost School of Art and Design in Breda
for about ten years, but I quit in 2017. I still do some
workshops there during the academic year. We replaced
our teaching positions with an online study program that10
we also started in 2017 called DesignDesign.Space.
90The students who come from all over the world
to do a master’s program in the Netherlands are the lucky
ones. They are privileged. There are many people who
would study but cannot manage the finances required, or15
who cannot obtain a visa. Or they simply cannot leave a
studio they have built up and take a sabbatical.
91 ‡ B.D 137g

B.D
The idea of DesignDesign.Space is to offer a
chance to do master’s level studies without accreditation.
Since there is no diploma, it’s very flexible. We can adapt20
the curriculum to any length or subject that people want
to do. It has been working nicely so far, not to the extent
of being able to make a living out of it, but it helps.

P.v.B. Nowadays, most of my days are filled with type

```
library: that means my fonts were automatically dis-
```
tributed among 20 million <Adobe> <G>2software users. This
helps, financially speaking. In the past, you were paid
for a type license and people still think that’s the way

Fig.4
Detail, RoboFont plug-in, Allows you to design and proof typeface
kerning as it is drawn, Petr van Blokland.
95 To get back to your question, in general I’m
interested in shaping the design process through teaching 15
graphic and type design, as well as how to write software,
coding and programming. The common denominator is
that I don’t make any distinctions between these things.
Sketches and lines of code are all stored in the same
way in my sketchbooks. I can see lines of code as an 20
image. I can manipulate them in my head, run them and
visualize their outcome. Sometimes I’m in the middle of
coding when I realize that I actually already sketched
the idea. The transition from sketching to coding is a
blurry one, and, during the design process, I constantly 25
switch between the two.

A.C 78 Part A: Interview C

A
Fig.5
The TYPETR Responder gives the designer control over
pen shape and size, Petr van Blokland.
96I never sketch out an idea one day, and then
code it for the next few weeks—the process is a con-
stant to-and-fro. In the model we have with Claudia, we
state that if a design problem cannot be solved within
a day, there is no way it will be solved with three more5
months of work.
97In the performing arts, you can tell upon hearing
them if someone has just learned to play an instrument.
You don’t have to be an educated musician yourself in
order to recognize that. The problem with graphic design10
is that the moment of presentation is not the moment of
creation. This makes it difficult to tell whether a poster
was done in a day or three months—you need training
in order to see the difference.
98 ‡ B.D 127g
Create 19
gProject 16
gPractice 16
gStudent 17
B.D
What we try to do with students, and also in15
our own practice, is to scale long-term projects back into
a performing arts timescale. We give students a pencil
and a piece of paper and say: “You have one hour to
create a corporate identity, go ahead and I’ll just sit here
and keep watching.” They often reply that they cannot20
work like that.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Petr van Blokland A.C 79
.C
PETR VAN BLOKLAND
99 However, what is different with acting on stage
or running a marathon is the fact it is also a mental thing.
Why does your design get better if I’m not watching you
for the three weeks it takes you to make it? We’re trying
to understand what’s at stake here, and help people 5
overcome it. We have conducted over a hundred Design
Games over the years. We put around fifty people in one
space, divided between design studios on one side, and
customers on the other. In this competition, the design
studios are set a design problem to solve in one hour. 10
They may ask the clients questions, but clients only get
to answer with “yes” or “no”.
100B.C 104 †
gWay · of · Working 17
B.C
Many of the participants think it’s impossible to
work that way, but that would mean that all the projects
should be equally terrible at the end of the hour, but that 15
is never the case. Some people figure that it’s a matter of
process, and they structure their way of working within
these limitations, and quickly adapt to them.
101 That’s what happens with many students when
they work on their graduation project for around two 20
and a half months. They think about the subject for two
months, without designing, and then all the design work
has to be done in the last couple of weeks. However, the
subject is never judged, so splitting your work that way
is a waste of time. Our method forces the students to do 25
a whole thesis project in one hour, or perhaps one day.
102 This comparison with the performing arts shows
that you can’t perform a concert at your best if you only
start to work on it two days before. Why is it that some
graphic designers think they can do things this way? We 30
might ask why we have the luxury of not being watched
while designing, even though it’s part of the game in
other art forms to perform in front of audiences?

103To return to the design process, I’m not inter-
ested in knowing whether a project concerns typography,
type design or programming. Students often say they
do one part, and then let other specialists work out the
rest, but I think that’s like making a sketch of a car, and5
then giving it to someone else to build. The shape flows
from what should be within the car, they are not sepa-
rate things. Therefore, I try not to separate the things
that I do, and consider each project as a whole, both
conceptually and technically.10
104 ‡ B.B 69g
Range · of · Tools 11
B.B
There are also a whole range of tools. Some like
the major players, such as <InDesign><G>5 . They are the
common denominators, what everybody wants to have.
It’s like a crowded beach—everybody is doing the same
thing with the same tools. If you want to escape that,15
you have to create your own island. Nobody is going to
build that island for you.
105 ‡ B.B 85g
Lines · of · Code 28
gDesign · Process 26
gLot · of · Potential 25
B.B
For instance, someone comes to you to design
their PhD thesis: 200 pages with complex images, graph-
ics, etc. You are doing your basic layout by pasting20
content from a Word document into <InDesign><G>5 , and
you want to link the images and words in the text with
arrows. If anything changes in the text on page three,
you have to redo all your arrows manually. A lot of po-
tential design choices that might potentially be part of25
the design process are not even considered because they
are too tedious to do manually in <InDesign><G>5 , while
five lines of code could get the job done. Programming
has now become part of the toolkit. This means that
a whole different set of design solutions can come from30
that source.

Interview with Petr van Blokland A.C 81
.C
PETR VAN BLOKLAND
106B.A 24 †
gDesign · Work 8
B.A
In terms of project management, if you work
on a prototype showing most of the design and tech-
nical solutions, and the final product needs to be ten
times faster, or a more reliable version, there is no clear
boundary between designing and producing. You slowly 5
go from one aspect to the other. How should you define
your role, and know when to hand the project over? If
you want to do all the design work, it would mean hav-
ing to do all the production work as well. My way of
dealing with it is to write a program that does the pro- 10
duction, because I’m not going to spend 80% of my time
doing that.
107B.C 97 †
gDesign · Process 16
B.C
My definition of design is something that you
create that otherwise wouldn’t be there, something that
you can do over and over and improve each time. Pro- 15
gramming is a tool that is part of the design process. If
you do it over and over again, you get better at it. This
also applies to sports, music, raising children, writing
stories, making movies, drawing typefaces, and so on.
108 The programming process is a feedback loop. 20
Until you run out of time, you have a chance to make
another version and learn from it. Potentially, you can
streamline that, so that the next time you can start at a
halfway point, thus saving some time. This explains why a
lot of research being done on feedback loops originates in 25
totally disparate areas, such as organizations, electronic
circuitry, and artificial intelligence.
109 One time, for my literature study group at the
Royal Academy, I selected a text on agents, a robot in
a maze, for instance, which is a common science experi- 30
ment. You have an independent decision-making object,
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.C 82 Part A: Interview C
INTERVIEWS
A
it could be a human, but could also be a robot or any-
thing else. How does a robot find its way through a
maze? It can store previous errors. It can make decisions
and weigh options before actually deciding on a given
direction. I took the text and replaced “agents” with “de-5
signers,” and “choices” with “sketches.” I presented this
to my colleagues, and asked the question “Does this seem
like a definition of the design process?” It seemed very
reasonable to them. Simply by replacing those words,
you are talking about a different process, one very similar10
to the agents—it all boils down to feedback loops.
110Another definition of design would be detail
management. In my opinion, a lot of designers who start
sketching on <InDesign><G>5 start right away with the pro-
duction process. <InDesign><G>5 is a production tool, not15
a design tool. If <InDesign><G>5 were a design tool, you
could show it two pages of a book, and it would work out
the other fifty pages. That’s what a design tool would
do. <Photoshop><G>6 was never made for graphic designers
to work with. It’s simply that there’s nothing better,20
and many designers accept its limitations, but in essence
these are production tools, made for printers. Conse-
quently, we need to make our own tools. I hardly ever
use <InDesign><G>5 , because by the time I know what I’m
doing, I have already written a program that does it.25
D.C. Can you explain a bit more about how you got
into programming, and the kind of tools you use?

and I were looking for a source code to connect to

are. · That · confusion · is · understandable · since · we · often · equate ·design · with · art. · In · other · words, · we're · too · close · to · design ·
to · really · be · able · to · see · it · for · what · it · is.For · example, · we · often · find · ourselves · trying · out · new · software ·
design · programs, · such · as · Photoshop · Elements · to · see · how · well ·our · designs · turn · out. · We · are · proud · of · our · designs · and ·
think · they · would · look · great · when · we · make · them · into · paintings. ·However, · we'll · quickly · find · out · that · the · design · part · doesn't ·
look · great. · A · closer · look · reveals · that · the · design · quality · isn't ·so · good · after · all. · We · may · even · ask: · how · will · my · designs ·
look · when · printed · out?Let · me · explain · in · more · detail · what · design · really · is. · The · design ·

<DrawBot><G>21 is great: there is a lot of coding
going on, and it has the power of pencil and paper, but10
you have to know what you’re doing. It’s a nice scribbling
equivalent using code. You can easily make animated
GIFs of mathematical equations. That’s great, it’s a good
means of introduction to coding and algorithms. Still, we
intentionally made <DrawBot><G>21 with no built-in graphic15
design knowledge. It’s like designing a <magazine>16
with just a pencil and paper: it might be possible but
would take a lot of time. I don’t know many graphic
designers who use it in their daily practice, because the
disparity with common design software is huge. About20
five years ago we asked ourselves: “How can we bridge
that gap?” The world is really divided between web
and print, but that’s an artificial boundary. I should
be able to save a given document as a website, as a
ready-to-print <PDF><G>65 , or as an animated GIF. Why25
isn’t there a software that can do that? We started to
think about that huge gap, and it has become the open-
source project <PageBot><G>62 . We’re still beta-testing, but
we get more and more assignments we can work on with
it. Also, if someone buys a <PageBot><G>62 template, I give30
them a typeface for free. The moment you begin to build

bridges between usages and medias, it opens up a lot of

116B.D 137 †

B.D
There are currently three pillars on which our
work is based. The first is DesignDesign.Space, which
covers education. We don’t hold back there and we have 5
put several assignments online. Students come to us for
paid courses if they need specific knowledge with feedback
and routine discipline to finish their project.
117 The two remaining pillars are coding and type
design. We can switch between them, we can mix the 10
three or offer some parts for free. If someone pays for
a workshop, I give them my tools or some examples of
typefaces with which they can work. If people buy my
typefaces, I show them how I would use them, or I’ll
comment on their usage. Again, it’s a very flexible way 15
of working.
D.C. Can you tell us more about PageBot,
this tool that should bridge the gap between DrawBot
and graphic design?
119 P.v.B. Sure. We first presented it four years ago at 20
ATypI Montreal. It was originally set up as a library on
top of <DrawBot> <G>21, filling in all kinds of design knowl-
edge standards. It’s all downloadable on <GitHub> <G>31.

120Following that lecture, people showed interest,

121Since then, we have been trying to do two things
with <PageBot><G>62 . The first is to make as many classes5
as we can. The core element in <PageBot><G>62 can be put
```
on a page, it has a generic behavior; a position, a size, a
```
background color, etc. All the elements inside it inherit
from the top element class. However, on lower levels,
elements can be very specialized. These intelligent ele-10
ments know how to solve internal design problems: you
make them once, and afterwards you don’t have to deal
with those decisions again. You close the trunk on them,
unless something gets broken, or needs to be fixed.
122 ‡ B.A 54g

The second thing we began doing with <Page-15
Bot><G>62 was adding what we call contexts. For example,
<PageBot><G>62, 50 on a <<Macintosh>18> is only one context.
Step by step, we’re creating a context that runs on
<Linux><G>47 , with the functions we already have on Mac.
Still, we’re moving relatively slowly, it’s an unfunded20
project. It’s funny because the big companies are not
willing to invest in open-source projects. They might be
willing to provide some funding, but if it’s open-source,
they say “thank you, we’ll take it from here.” Getting
funded directly for open-source projects is a challenge,25

D.C. How do you see creative coding shaping the
way designers work in the future, keeping in mind
Adobe’s monopoly, open-source software and emerging
online design generators?
126P.v.B. I think the reason a lot of <Adobe><G>2 products5
still exist, especially in the design field, is because the peo-
ple using these softwares were educated to mainly work
on print-based media. However, if you look at volumes
of information, 95% of it is now being communicated
digitally, and I still see very little crossover between those10
two worlds.
127The graphic designer who is working with
Creative Cloud, and then using <InDesign><G>5 or <Photo-
shop><G>6 to make documents that go to a so-called devel-
oper who does the <HTML><G>37, 17 and <CSS>, thinks they15
made a website, but it’s just styling.
128 ‡ B.A 50g
Functional · Website 25
gGraphic · Design 18
gClients 23
B.A
Traditionally, customers ask a designer to do a
website. The graphic designer comes up with mockups,
<Photoshop><G>6 documents, etc. The whole thing is then
put on the developer’s table, and they start from scratch,20
because there is no information about responsive or con-
ditional behaviors, etc. The developer basically does all
the work, and clients now realize that. So next time,
they start with the developer, who can make a functional
website.25
129At that point, the developer asks the designer
for colors and typefaces, keeping the designer out of
the loop. In the past, the designer was designing the
whole process, but now they are just visually adjusting
something that was made without them.30

130 If you buy sixty pretty good WordPress tem-
plates for a hundred dollars, you don’t need to pay thou-
sands for a designer. Graphic designers have to get them-
selves back in the process, where they’re part of the team
again and dialoguing with developers, let’s say every ten 5
minutes, if you still want to draw a line between design
and development.
131 This linear thinking, with people only being part
of a production chain, assigned to a particular function,
where the creative works on preliminary sketches, pol- 10
ishes them, passes them on to a developer and then steps
out of the project, with a developer who only then starts
to work on their own, is the opposite of what design
should be like.
132B.D 138 †
gCourse 18
B.D
To answer your question about the future, 15
I think that the designers who will survive are ones
who are capable of putting themselves in a different state
of mind. Of course, there should be talent, and taste, and
one should have fun doing things. The hardest aspect to
inculcate is entrepreneurship: is a design assignment a 20
cost or an investment?
133 You have to find a way to have fun designing
```
something today; you don’t want to go through thou-
```
sands of hours of boring exercises before you can actu-
ally master something. Learning to play an instrument 25
should be fun every day, that is the way to survive the
first years and get really good at something, better than
someone else.
134 The hardest thing is selecting people and moti-
vating them. You can’t save them all. There are people 30
who never want to be entrepreneurs, they just want to
be told what to do.

A.C 92 Part A: Interview C
A
INTERVIEWS
.D
TED
DAVIS
www.teddavis.org
Ted
Davis
Interviewed by
Rob van Leijsen
<Ted Davis>20 is a media artist, designer and
educator originally from the United States,
currently based in Basel, Switzerland. Since 2010,
he has been teaching interaction design and
coordinates in the UIC/HGK International Master
of Design program, which is part of the Visual
Communication Institute at the Basel School of
Design HGK FHNW. His work and teachings
explore the volatility of digital media through glitch
and using innovative programming to reactivate
“older new media.” His open-source projects
```
(basil.js, XYscope, P5LIVE) enable designers
```
to program within Adobe InDesign, render vector
graphics on vector displays, and collaboratively
create live coded visuals. Through his international
exhibits, lectures and workshops, he empowers
students to take hold of the computer’s ability to
design possibilities that reach beyond the hand
or the mouse.
Interview with Ted Davis A.D 93
Rob van Leijsen Ted, thanks for inviting us to your
studio. Could you introduce yourself, and explain your
practice as a media artist and teacher?
140 Ted Davis Thanks for involving me in this project, I’m
happy to participate. Well, I came to Switzerland [from 5
the US] twelve years ago, initially to do a Master’s degree
in Visual Communication. While I was working on that,
I got into creative coding and programming.
141 I had done websites in the past, but it was while
I was working on my Master’s project, specifically learning 10
about how digital images work and how they could be
taken apart, that I really got into code, learned <PHP> <G>61,
and saw its possibilities for my practice. Since then,
I sort of split myself between being an artist, a designer,
and an educator in the realm of media. 15
142 Professionally, my initial core focuses were glitches
and embracing errors, and the question of digital volatil-
ity. Then I got more into generative design. For the last
eight or nine years, I have really been getting into what
I call new and newer media, which is a sort of dialogue 20
with older new media by way of today’s newer media.
143 Some friends had introduced me to a thermal
printer, and at the same time, I got an old pen plotter
from the 1980s. Over the last few years, I have been really
focused on vector displays, like oscilloscopes and lasers, 25
and these different technologies that were seen as innova-
tive in the past, but then got replaced by something else.
I realized that, with the computers and programming
tools available today, we can use those devices to create
new things, so I plunged into this area. 30

A · version · can · be · thought · of · as · a ·file · or · set · of · files, · but · with · the · important · extra · attribute, · it ·
represents · a · new · unique · entity · of · data. What · are · the · steps · that ·go · with · creating · a · new · version?
1. · A · new · version · is · generated · by · the · tool · (using · git), · which ·includes: a. · A · set · of · files. b. · An · empty · commit · log · message.
2. · This · same · tool · can · be · used · to · commit · the · same · file · (or ·entire · set · of · files) · while · editing · that · same · file · or · set · of · files · to ·
modify · the · version. · It · does · so · by · adding · the · new · file · to ·the · log, · and · the · file · is · then · committed.
3. · To · change · it · to, · say, · 2 · different · files, · you · would · then · simply ·add · the · 2 · different · files · and · then · commit · the · changes, · as · if · the ·
original · file · never · existed.When · you · see · a · "git · fsck" · command, · what · is · it · trying · to · tell ·

in Switzerland to introduce elementary school teachers

gramming, it’s been interesting to see waves of this.

and kids were learning code with this device. I think

rived, kids would be learning code, that everyone would

coding skills, and big studios coming out with projects
to show they have this generative identity or website

How · do · they · get · to · be ·headlines? · Or, · more · to · the · point: · why · do · they · stick?
“That's · just · a · headline," · I · heard · my · co-host, · Tim · Russert, · say, ·pointing · to · a · large · banner · that · his · news · team · had · added · to ·
one · of · our · computer · terminals. · That · might · sound · a · bit ·snobbish · to · a · typical · casual · browser · or · online · consumer, ·
but · "headline-itis" · has · long · been · a · professional · media · cliché, ·dating · back · at · least · to · the · first · half · of · the · twentieth · century. ·
That · was · a · time · when · newspapers · were · struggling · to · find · their ·footing · in · a · new · medium. · By · the · 1930s, · for · instance, · the · New ·
York · Times · was · telling · its · reporters, · in · the · words · of · one ·Timesman, · "Don't · use · a · headline.“
“You're · making · headlines · with · that · story," · Russert · told · us.“Are · you · kidding?" · I · said. · "That's · an · incredible · headline, · or ·
headline-itis—how · can · we · use · that?“

173One of the other important points, apart from
saving time, is the possibility of iteration. That’s perhaps
the most important aspect of creative coding for me,
actually. It would ordinarily take me an infinite amount
of time to try out an idea by hand, change my mind, etc.5
When the layout is based on parameters and variables,
you have the chance to try things, to modify a value and
immediately see how it affects the design. This iterative
process, creating, reflecting, changing, creating, and so
on, is very quick, and can be endless. The downside is10
that there’s no reason to stop, when you’re coding a
project. The plus side is that you can always reflect,
change and update.

Animal Farm, Publication designed by students
Martina Dolezalov and Annalisa Savin using
basil.js, Ted Davis.

A
R.v.L. Do you notice your students having problems
being selective in their output, and arguing about their
selection? How do they deal with that?
180T.D. I don’t think that is limited to creative coding
and programming. I think students who focus on photo-5
graphy, or on type, also have to create lots of iterations,
lay them out, and evaluate why one is working better
than the other. Maybe the difference lies in the way more
possibilities can be created within a coded environment,
but I think that’s a general skill that’s been instilled10
at school. Consequently, I don’t think it’s an especially
tricky problem in the realm of coding.
R.v.L. Are you also seeing some libraries that tend to
generate similar outputs? How do you avoid repetition
from an esthetic standpoint?15
182 ‡ B.B 76g
Own · tool 18
gLibraries · 20
gCreate 20
B.B
T.D. That is important, because whatever tool we
use, it influences the output. Even if you create your own
tools, they’re limited to what you know. In the realm of
coding, it’s possibly limited to the number of functions
one knows how to create, and then libraries boost those20
possibilities.
183However, we can also be limited by those pos-
sibilities. I might be using a library that’s adding fluid
dynamics to a letterform, if so, I will be limited to
what has been programmed for those physics and values.25
I might be able to influence some of them, but others
might be fixed. If I did it from scratch, it would proba-
bly come out quite differently, but that might require a
much greater understanding of programming. As a result,
I think the higher and the further one goes in coding,30
and customizing one’s tools, the more esthetically diverse
the output will be.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Ted Davis A.D 107
.D
TED DAVIS
R.v.L. How is your own practice evolving, both in
terms of creative coding, and from your experience with
teaching?
185 T.D. With creative coding, pretty early on I started
getting tired of seeing it on the computer screen. That’s 5
where this “new and newer media” enabled me to see
far more surprises from the code. The minute the code
is output from the computer onto some other display,
or printing device, surprises happen—they are part of
your code but unanticipated. Then you can modify your 10
code in terms of that specific environment. As a result,
things became possible using a pen plotter or a thermal
printer, such as over-printing, and seeing what happens
when something gets hit twice with that same line. You
can’t really see that on the computer screen. 15
186B.D 139 †g
Teaching · Processing
gWriting · Code
B.D
One year, after having fallen into vector dis-
plays, I brought that into the classroom while teaching
<Processing> <G>68to our Master’s students, in a course called
“Alt-Outputs”. In it they would write code, but it had
to be rendered on anything except the computer screen. 20
Some students went with projection, some took apart
a computer screen and set it on an overhead projec-
tor, to explore what that did. One group projection
mapped onto spheres, others inside one. Some used plot-
ting, either building their own pen-plotter, or using an 25
existing one, trying to interface with it in some new way.
The thing is that, when you use these other devices,
there are weird hiccups that happen, and that you are
not anticipating, but they add a really nice quality to
the design. 30
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

A
R.v.L. One thing I wanted to follow up on was the

188T.D. To my understanding, the tools we’re using were
created for both artists and designers. I think the design
field is more influenced by media arts output: they get
increasingly closer, and start to overlap.10
```
189In the US (at least where I studied) the depart-
```
```
ments of graphic design and media design (or broadcast),
```
were quite separate and distinct. When I arrived here,
visual communication was a far bigger umbrella term,
because it encompassed type, image, moving [kinetic]15
images, all of these things. I really like the way it left
things much more open.
R.v.L. Have you observed a difference between how
designers in the US and Europe use code?
191 ‡ B.A 56g
Self-initiated · Projects 31
B.A
T.D. It’s tough for me to say, having been in Europe20
for twelve years, but as a general observation from afar,
I have a feeling that, in the US, these technologies are
used more for commercial projects, making apps and
applications. In Europe, it has been used much more
in media arts, and less in applied experimentation, but25
that might just be because there are more media arts
museums and festivals in Europe. Perhaps, one might
say that the scope is wider and not uniquely client-based
in Europe. Part of that might also be due to funding
structures. There is so much more government funding30
for the arts, for festivals, for self-initiated projects in
Europe, whereas most of the funding in the US has to
come from a commercial enterprise or private company.

TED DAVIS

193B.B 93 †

T.D. I think on the one hand, right now, creative 5
coding offers us so much, and I’m a huge advocate for
everyone learning it. I’m really curious about what the
future holds in terms of creative coding becoming in-
creasingly easier to use. Perhaps at some point we may
not actually need to type in code, but just dictate the 10
concept we would have previously coded to the computer,
and it will just figure that out and do it for us. Maybe
some aspects of code will be offset, or replaced in the
creative coding process. In the end, what we’re coding
could be written out as human-readable instructions, for 15
the most part, reducing some people’s need to learn code.
194B.A 47 †
gBook · Design 19
B.A
On the other hand, I’m a firm believer that code
contributes to the evolution of the fields of design, ty-
pography, photography, motion, and book design. For
quite a few years, we were limited to the tools made avail- 20
able, packaged software that offers so many possibilities,
but also has so many limitations. The minute we intro-
duce code, we break those limitations and can go into
weird, uncharted territory. For me, code is really about
expanding the realm of possibilities and confronting the 25
limitations within the digital domain.
R.v.L. Thanks a lot, Ted.

DIA · Studio · is · a · Chicago, · IL-based · digital · design · and ·development · firm · servicing · North · America. · We · work · closely ·
with · our · client's · product · development · teams, · architects, · data ·miners, · product · owners, · strategists, · and · design · experts · to ·

Mitch Paone is a co-founder of <DIA Studio>28,
which specializes in corporate and cultural visual
identity systems, graphic design, and typography.
DIA’s signature use of motion and generative
tools has led to major collaborations with Apple,
Balenciaga, A-Trak, Nike, Saint Laurent,
Squarespace, among many others. In addition to
his work with DIA, Paone is a performing jazz
pianist, composer and type designer. Paone also
lectures and leads workshops at conferences and
universities worldwide, most notably, at the Royal
```
Academy of Art at The Hague (KABK), Zurich
```
```
University of the Arts (ZHdK), École Cantonale
```
```
d’Art de Lausanne (ECAL), the Pratt Institute,
```
and the School for Visual Arts. Recently Paone
has accepted a permanent professorship at
the Haute École d’Art et de Design in Geneva
```
(HEAD-Genève) in the Media Design Master
```
and Visual Communication Bachelor programs.
There, he will be implementing DIA’s creative
process into the academic curriculum, with a
specific focus on motion and generative tools in a
graphic design context. Paone and his DIA studio
partner Meg Donohoe were recently inducted
```
into the AGI (Alliance Graphique Internationale).
```

Mitch Paone
Interviewed by

and

Interview with Mitch Paone A.E 113
Demian Conrad Mitch, first of all, thank you for
participating in our project, we are delighted to have
you. Could you introduce us to the topic of creative
coding? For example, tell us about a project you did for

develop it and how did creative coding impact your

198 Mitch Paone All right, so to introduce myself, I’m
Mitch Paone, Creative Director and Cofounder of DIA
Studio. We are a Slack-enabled graphic design studio 10
located in Geneva [Switzerland], New York City… one
of our staff is in Germany, we’re all over the place. We
specialize in corporate identity for the most part, with
a lot of motion graphics and more production-oriented
stuff as well. My partner Meg [Donohoe] is the Managing 15
```
Director; she primarily handles the business functions of
```
the studio, but also runs creative direction on projects.
And then there’s Daniel Wenzel, who is a designer and a
creative coder as well. We also have Deanna Sperrazza
as a graphic designer: she’s holding down the New York 20
office at the moment. They have all been part of the
team for some time.

there are a lot of cases in which we’ve used coding to

Sometimes creative coding is a very expressive part of

visual system. Then there was also an exhibition piece

Zach Lieberman and
Squarespace, 2018.
200Secret 7" is a music/art event which takes place
in London, and they asked Squarespace to sponsor the5
event. As part of the sponsorship, they helped fund
it. We decided that this project would provide a great
opportunity to create some very interesting artistic tools.
We ended up creating a tool, based on seven different
pieces of music, that typographically visualized each10
track in a certain way. Ultimately, the final product was
actually a design application that any member of the
public could use to create very expressive typographic
posters and animations, one which receives input text,
generates effects through it, and prints out this stuff at15
high resolution. In this case, the tool or the code was
the final product of the piece. That was quite interesting
for us, because we were able to create a prototype and
develop it over a period of time. In the end, you just
give it to other people for them to use, to enable them20
to create designs very easily. The end user didn’t need to
have any background in design, the tool itself maintained
all these principles of design that we set up and then
placed them at the disposal of someone else for their use.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

Visitors creating posters at Secret 7".

Initially, we did a lot of research on our end in
terms of creative and art direction, asking questions like
“Which effects made sense with each music track?” We
were trying to convey that visually, in the same way the
designer of a record cover creates a piece of artwork. We 5
had to do that times seven, only using typography, in
black and white, with limitations. Each one had a very
different style, a different effect, a different approach, all
conveyed just through the use of text.

202How were we able to make that stuff? We pro-
totyped everything in 3D with Cinema4D or <After
Effects><G>3 , and created looping animations to show the
way we wanted the text to animate. Once we had some
sign-off for these directions, we began working with Zach5
Lieberman to bring these elements into a coded environ-
ment.
203All of a sudden, what we had could become
interactive and expressive, with more range and modules
and tools. You could change camera angles, you could10
scale it, you could instance the text, you could do a lot
more stuff based on the prototypes that we built. Finally,
there was a third wave to this, because Squarespace
makes websites for people, so they were like “We need
to make sure this is a web-based application,” because15
that would be in line with the company’s needs.

204Using Zach’s openFrameworks we then worked
with the in-house team at Squarespace. They have a huge
team of developers to convert it all into <JavaScript><G>41 .
Then we made a web app that was publicly available.20
Unfortunately, the link isn’t up anymore.

205 It was a well-funded experimental project that
really changed the studio and how we approach some
stuff, and we’re seeing this becoming increasingly
common.
206 In tandem, we worked on the rebrand for Square- 5
space. It was a pretty traditional rebrand, but what was
uniquely interesting about this project was the behavior
of this animation: we played with the idea of a square
and space as some sort of visual trick that became the
identifying element of how the work was actually dis- 10
played visually. So you perceive this sense of dimension
using this square in space through both animated and
still formats.

207 In a way, the motion and the actual behavior
that we developed in motion was the way we gener- 15
ated the entire work. In this case, we were using <After
Effects> <G>3to do 90% of the design tasks. It became prob-
lematic at a certain point that we couldn’t necessarily
convert this into coded stuff, or generate huge-scale prints
with <After Effects> <G>3. You simply just couldn’t do that. 20

208So what we needed to do was to take the same
behaviors we were using for animation and convert them
into an interactive environment for the in-house team to
use. We set up a whole range of different toolkits and
toolsets, where if you just enter data, put images in,5
and then text, and you deal with some sliders, it gener-
ates different possibilities of layouts, especially for larger
print stuff.
209What was so paramount about a tool like this
—and I think in this process—is that the brand did not10
have fixed formats for communication. So we could go
square, rectangle, tall, wide, and everything in-between.
The only way to generate consistency of composition
rules was that it needed to be coded into something
and not have to be interpreted by a brand designer15
every time.
210Furthermore, since the in-house brand teams
were not super proficient in, say Cinema 4D or <After
Effects><G>3 to do this work, this made it very easy for
them to take over these assets to create stuff based on20
these tools. They didn’t have to really think about the
technical side. They could just play around with it, find
the composition that worked for them, and then export
it.
211I feel pretty strongly that this is how things25
will be happening more and more frequently. I think
as a studio, we are not actually interested in making
<PDF><G>65 documents and brand guidelines. It is way more
interesting to create a series of tools that you hand over
to these companies so they can take over the execution30
of the work internally.

I believe this is the future for several reasons:
one, you are guaranteeing consistency because you have

a good discussion with Meg about this. From a business

about this more the way you would for music licensing

a design problem.

We did a bunch of R&D on tools for in-app
effects for Instagram, and that was the same thing. They
bought licenses for these <JavaScript><G>41 things that we
sent to them. So they were bank-buying IP, basically,20
outright or in perpetuity, versus just giving us a budget.
So I thought, “This is a new way, a totally new way
of working!” I don’t want to be dealing with project to
project work for the next twenty years of my life, because
that’s just not sustainable for me. Who knows what’s25
going to happen in twenty years? I take the position
that you’ve got to jump on the train, otherwise you’re
going to be gone. You’re going to be in the dust if you
don’t. You don’t have to learn how to make it, you’ve
got to learn how to work with it and work with people to30
create things. You don’t necessarily have to learn code,
but you have to understand the process.

to becoming a designer of systems. So can you develop

218 M.P. I really do think hardcore typesetting and that 5
sort of stuff will be automated within the next ten years.
Good rags, good leading. You’ll just look at the page,
figure out the dimensions, boom, you’re going to get the
right typeface size, no variable fonts, who knows. It’s
going to be able to give you the exact, perfect thing. We 10
won’t have to think about this anymore, which is good,
I think. Maybe people will get really upset with me for
saying that, but I do think it’s a good thing, because
that way we can set our minds to thinking about the
concept of the work, maybe pushing the work further, 15
and automating these things that we would have wasted
hours on doing, fundamentally making it much quicker.
I had a lot of experience with that, especially with the
A-Trak job. A lot of the covers were done based on
exports from tools that were made. Basically, I could 20
generate effects or practice some very interesting layout
techniques that we could not do by hand. Then we would
just pick the one we liked best and that went to print. So
the process, the design was not crafted, it was curated
from something else. 25

A.E 122 Part A: Interview E

A
right direction. Time spent on it is bullshit. It’s like when
people say, “I worked on this font for ten years.” It doesn’t
mean it’s good. It might be well crafted, but the esthetic
could be off, so it doesn’t mean anything. Consequently,
when thinking about system designs more specifically, or5
systematic design, I have always seen brand guidelines as
essentially a formula. When I work with students and we
talk about composition, it’s the limitations you put on
it that makes it feel the way it does. If you can use only
Helvetica 18-point type, and then the rest is open, the10
fact that you have just the consistency of the font chosen,
the size, means that there will be a relative consistency
in everything, because those limitations are always there.
That’s just from an esthetic point of view. When you
start thinking about branding, you have a wide range15
of elements with different outputs or whatever it’s going
to be on the format. Ultimately, you are baking in all
these very strict parameters for the work to maintain a
consistent esthetic.

222 What’s good about things like Cinema 4D or

there, then you know exactly what you need to code,
because you built this thing.

230 M.P. It depends. The Squarespace thing was so tight

the tool, you should actually be happy to set it free. It
should be important for us to know that our babies have
left the nest and then to allow them to be free. That’s not 10
```
only just from a design standpoint; if you have created a
```
good enough tool, then it should just work. There will
be times where something breaks or a bug comes up and
needs to get fixed, but I think before it gets handed over,
we were pretty happy with the result. What’s actually 15
more interesting in this case, is that you’re going to
be surprised by what you see. You actually see more
interesting stuff generated because you have different
people working with these tools. Sometimes it actually
ends up being better, or more interesting. Or sometimes 20
it goes to shit, but we don’t have control over that. What
I realize is that if you take a lot of basic typographic
stuff out of the team’s hands, the things that would fall
apart and really ruin the thing are actually being taken
away. Then you should allow people to have more fun 25
with how things are being animated or composed, and
maybe let elements get more expressive.
231 It’s important for the tool to have enough flexi-
bility, and not be annoying or cumbersome for the end

I think there needs to be some enabled freedom in there

M.P. I think it’s very disconnected, and I think it25

hurdle right now is that the people that are part of

<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Mitch Paone A.E 129
.E
DIA STUDIO
don’t even understand the basics of animation, you have
to think about how we are going to jumpstart this at
a higher level. There’s no way, because the curriculum
has to spring from a working understanding of these
concepts. That’s why this is going to take some time. 5
Luckily, you have people like Zach Lieberman in the US
focusing on it. However, the issue with that is that it’s
not graphic design—it isn’t. It’s media art and there’s
a big delineation between media art and graphic design.
The way this is going to have to happen, is that these 10
media interaction departments need to merge with the
graphic design departments. I think it’s absurd that
they’re still separate. They’re closely linked in the US.
Interaction design and graphic design are much more
interrelated in the US, but the problem in the US is 15
that we have a design education system that is oriented
towards marketing, so we’re not teaching designers to
think systematically.
245 As time goes on and these lines start to blur
more, and this begins to infiltrate into the academic 20
environment, then it won’t just be a movement. I would
go as far as to say that it is the way design is going to be
in the very near future, maybe even just five years from
now. There’s going to be this fluidity between technology
and design. Design has to catch up with the formats and 25
the technology, and they have to be taught together. You
need to mix your media interaction design classes with
your graphic design classes. There is no other way: in
my opinion, they should not be separated. They are not
separate paths. 30

A.E 130 Part A: Interview E
INTERVIEWS
A
R.v.L. I have one last question. What would be your
definition of creative coding?
247M.P. I think of creative coding as more like a simpli-
fication of repetitive tasks, in a way. Essentially, it just
allows you to be far more creative because it’s simplifying5
things that you don’t necessarily want to have to deal
with, enabling new potentials in some sort of esthetic
output that was simply just not possible with the tools
and means we had before.
248It doesn’t need to be code as a matter of course,10
but I think the key is thinking about how code works,
thinking how an engineer works. It opens up a different
way of thinking about how work can be produced. That
is one of the most important things, it’s not necessarily
about the need to be a master, but rather of putting15
yourself in a position of empathy with an engineer and
knowing what can actually come of that. It becomes
more about using tools to allow you to curate results,
rather than sitting around crafting them for two months.
I think it’s more of a philosophy than a technological20
thing, but in the end, it is both.

it · means · to · you · and · your · favourite · fan.If · the · answer · is · Dynamo · is · home, · then · you · clearly · know · all ·
```
about · us; · if · it's · a · club · from · your · country, · you're · a · Dinamo · fan ·no · matter · what. · If · the · answer · was · neither · or · both, · you're · part ·
```
of · a · different · kind · of · club. · You're · the · kind · of · support · who ·gives · us · both · respect · and · the · motivation · to · be · better. · As · a ·
```
word: · "Chameleon."Chameleons · are · born · with · a · green · hue · that · can · quickly · change ·
```
to · whatever · colour · suits · them · best · at · the · time. · We · are · the ·greenest · supporters · in · every · league · – · we · can · change · as · often · as ·
we · can · make · a · profit. · We · have · all · the · colours · in · the · rainbow: ·blue, · white · and · red. · For · some · of · us, · yellow · can · become ·
green. · But · no · matter · what · they · are, · we · always · have · our ·trademark · colours · of · three · horizontal · stripes. · And · when · we ·
need · a · matchday · identity · for · a · new · country, · we · know · who ·to · blame.
The · Green · Legion · always · leaves · its · mark, · whether · it's · the ·legendary · "Chameleon" · chant · or · our · "Ekstra · Ekstra" · flag · that · was ·
designed · a · year · before · the · club · entered · the · top · flight. · These ·are · the · reasons · we · are · always · referred · to · as · Dinamo, · our · name ·
in · Ukrainian.Why · are · we · the · first · football · club · in · the · world · to · create · an ·
alternative · support · group?We're · the · greenest · supporters. · Our · team · in · Kiev · was · founded · in ·
1997 · and · we · joined · the · Ukrainian · league · in · 2001. · After · six · years ·in · the · second · league, · we've · just · clinched · promotion · to · the ·
top · flight.Back · then · we · were · known · as · Chornomorets, · then · the · "Green ·
Legion", · and · finally · as · Chornomorets-2. · When · we · joined · the ·Ukrainian · league · in · 2001, · we · changed · our · name · to · "Dynamo", ·
hoping · for · success · from · the · first · club, · the · "first · star", · in · Kiev. · It ·was · never · an · official · name · of · a · fan · movement.
The · club · wanted · to · become · Dynamo · Kiev. · The · fans · wanted ·to · become · Chornomorets, · not · to · be · seen · as · opposition ·
but · to · have · respect · for · the · previous · team. · We · started · to ·spread · around · Ukraine · with · the · Chornomorets · name. · I · had ·
my · own · team · and · was · very · active · because · other · fans · said: · "Our ·team · is · called · Chornomorets. · We · want · to · wear · the · green · and ·
orange · shirts."We · were · against · Dynamo · because · we · thought · that · the · fans ·
would · say: · "We · are · in · Kiev! · Dynamo · is · in · Moscow." · In · those ·days, · people · did · not · understand · what · fans · were.

Dinamo
Fabian

Interviewed by
