www.abcdinamo.com
Fabian Harb is a typeface designer based in
Basel, Switzerland and cofounder, with
Johannes Breyer, of Dinamo. After studying at
```
the Basel School of Design (CH) he began
```
working with Claudiabasel and Laurenz Brunner.
He has received awards in the Most Beautiful
Swiss Books and the Most Beautiful Czech Books
competitions, and is also a recipient of a Swiss
Design Award, as well as a member of the Alliance
```
Graphique Internationale (AGI).
```
Fabiola Mejía is an independent type designer
from San José, Costa Rica. After graduating from
the TypeMedia program in The Netherlands,
she joined Fabian Harb at the Dinamo studio in
Basel to help with an expansion of the Monument
Grotesk type family. She is now back in her
hometown, and collaborates remotely with Dinamo
and works on her own type-related projects.
<Dinamo>29 is a Swiss type design agency which operates
through a network of satellite members across the globe, offering
retail and bespoke typefaces, design software, research, and
consultancy. Dinamo members are visiting professors at various art
schools and have been invited to give workshops and lectures at
an international roster of educational institutions. To date, Dinamo
has released over twenty typeface families, and continues to
take on both commercial and cultural projects. They have designed
```
typefaces for clients such as Warp Records (UK), Kunsthalle
```
```
Zurich (CH), Acqua di Parma Perfumes (IT), RIMOWA (DE),
```
```
and Tumblr.com (US).
```
Interview with Fabian Harb + Fabiola Mejía A.F 133
Rob van Leijsen Today I’m in Basel with Fabiola
and Fabian from the Dinamo type foundry.
Can you introduce yourselves and tell us about the way
your studio is set up?
251 Fabian Harb Welcome to Dinamo, my name is Fabian… 5
252252 Fabiola Mejía …and I’m Fabiola. I’m a recent grad-
uate of the TypeMedia Master’s program at the Royal
Academy of Art in The Hague [KABK]. I am currently
a type designer and have been working on type design 10
projects at Dinamo since I left university.
Fig.15
Fabiola Mejía and Fabian Harb
at the Dinamo office, Basel.
253253 F.H. Johannes Breyer and I started Dinamo up after
we graduated from design school. At first, we mostly
designed typefaces together, then after some time passed
and we began getting commissions for an increasing 15
amount of projects, we gradually started sharing them
with friends and colleagues. Then we formalized it by
creating a type foundry. In the beginning it was just
our own designs, but now we also collaborate with and
publish the work of other designers. We also create cus- 20
tom typefaces for our clients, which include institutions
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.F 134 Part A: Interview F
INTERVIEWS
A
and brands. We try to make use of elements that lie off
the beaten track of mainstream type design. Sometimes
we design tools and other products that range from the
useful to the nonsensical.
R.v.L. Can you tell us more about the importance of5
creative coding in your practice?
255F.H. I am not a very tech-savvy person myself, but
I am very interested in what can be done, what the
limits are, alternative ways of using technology, and so
on. Johannes and I both studied graphic design and did10
not learn how to design type at school, but picked it up
on our own, learning by working with people in the field.
256As we pursued this course, we developed the
ability to collaborate with specialists on projects and
discovered that this would often enable us to find ways15
to accomplish things that at first had seemed impossible.
Consequently, even though coding is an important part
of what goes on at Dinamo, many of us here don’t do
much coding.
257Whenever we do our own developing, it is gener-20
ally handled by our own developer, Rob[ert Janes] who
has a range of skills reminiscent of a Swiss army knife.
Together we developed the Font Gauntlet, our own plat-
form for proofing variable fonts, over the past few years.
25
R.v.L. Did your understanding of the implications of
programming also increase your practical coding skills?
259F.H. As far as understanding goes, I think so. Still,
Johannes and I don’t do any, or almost none, of the
coding ourselves. Rather, we determine what should be30
developed, and who will work with us to develop it. Then
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Fabian Harb + Fabiola Mejía A.F 135
.F
DINAMO
Fig.16
Screenshot of the Dinamo Font Gauntlet integrated development
environment created to visualize Variable Font interpolation.
we follow the development, and provide feedback, which
in turn generates new questions and thoughts.
260260 F.M. It is very valuable to be able to mentally switch
between the creative and technical parts of the job, and to
know if there is a possibility of realizing an idea through 5
technological means. You can then identify what can be
done with coding.
261261 F.H. It’s great when it’s a dialogue. When we start
out on a project, we rarely start out knowing everything
you need to produce it at the outset—that would be quite 10
boring. Over the years, we have gained great insight into
technology and developments through our exchanges
with people at the forefront of typeface technology.
R.v.L. If you had to outline your collaboration, where
does the type designer stop and the programmer step 15
in?
263B.C 98 †
gDesign · Process 1
B.C
F.H. That’s hard to say and specific to each project.
There are projects where the separation is really clear, for
example the creation of the Galapagos typeface. We drew
the modules, and we handed over to Gustavo [Ferreira], 20
and he made it work technically, but that process is quite
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.F 136 Part A: Interview F
INTERVIEWS
A
rare. Most of the time the design process is an iterative
one, where you implement what you have learned, and,
in order to move along at each step, you talk to the
person who can help you with that. It’s a bit like ping
pong… there’s not really a set recipe.
Fig.17
Galapagos application,
Dinamo, 2016.
5
R.v.L. Is there a set of collaboration guidelines that
can be applied to all projects?
265F.H. The only consistent requirement is that your
team has to be made up of people who are nice to work
with.10
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Fabian Harb + Fabiola Mejía A.F 137
.F
DINAMO
R.v.L. So are the programmers involved in projects
right from the first sketches?
267 F.H. Sometimes that happens with Robert, because
he is part of Dinamo. However, when we collaborate with
outside people, we usually try to have an idea that is 5
already clear enough to make us feel confident about
involving someone else.
R.v.L. What are the specific tasks a programmer
executes when involved in type design?
269 F.H. There are so many different ones. The most 10
frequent is the creation of OpenType features, dealing
with all the technical aspects to make sure the font files
render properly on all output devices, and generating
font files. There also may be special requirements for
specific projects. 15
R.v.L. How do you see typefaces evolving? Are there
specific future trends or uses you see on the horizon?
What are you working on yourselves to enhance your
type productions?
271 F.H. At the moment, we are really interested in vari- 20
able fonts, a relatively new font format that allows for
incorporating multiple styles into just one file, and pro-
vides seamless access to the full design spectrum. We
also develop tools to proof design spaces for our variable
font projects, especially as regards the dynamics of shape 25
development, when you set a typeface in motion. The
potential to animate typefaces opens up a lot of oppor-
tunities for design and branding. Also, on a technical
level, it offers advantages in terms of file size and loading
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.F 138 Part A: Interview F
INTERVIEWS
A
speeds. It could well be that, in the near future, we will
completely shift to variable fonts.
R.v.L. If you had to look ahead, what might
a typeface become in the future?
273 ‡ B.A 43g
Variable · Fonts 5
gVariable · font · Technology
B.A
F.M. When you use variable font technology, you have5
a more interactive experience when you design typefaces,
making typography a more engaging and responsive
process.
R.v.L. To what extent does the user control
these features?10
275 ‡ B.A 43g
Variable · Fonts 15
gVariable · font · Technology
B.A
F.H. Currently, this depends a lot on the environment.
If you are a designer working with specific software, you
can have full control over all these features. However,
if you are “just” a reader of a news platform, at the
moment there isn’t much exploration of the variable font15
technology available for you yet. I am sure this will change
and things will open up, and that personalized settings
and reading preferences will eventually be available to
everybody.
276276F.M. There needs to be an awareness of how to engage20
with this new technology on the user side. The technology
is already there, but users still seem to be hesitant or
have trouble seeing the opportunities it presents. So
I hope we get to a common ground where the two can
come together and understand each other.25
R.v.L. If typefaces are adapting to the personal
behavior and preferences of the user, how can you, as
a type designer, create more awareness about all the
possibilities of these tools?
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Fabian Harb + Fabiola Mejía A.F 139
.F
DINAMO
278B.A 43 †
gVariable · Fonts 8
gClients 3
B.A
F.H. The question of how to make the public aware
of new features so they can learn about the possibilities
is a big topic. A lot of the time, clients are also hesitant
about implementing new technologies, because they look
at statistics that show that a share of their public is 5
still using old browsers, for example. This makes them
reluctant to move forward and risk using new technology.
A few big corporations already use variable fonts in their
systems, and on their websites, but in such a way that
the user does not really notice the technology. 10
R.v.L. Do you also see it as your mission to showcase
this potential and guide your clients? How do you
accomplish that?
280 F.H. Absolutely. In order to consider new technology,
you first have to learn about it and understand its poten- 15
tial, its advantages. Therefore, we try to showcase our
variable fonts as much as possible and provide as much
insight as we can into their possibilities.
281B.A 46 †
gVariable · Fonts 25
B.A
People also come to us with questions about
licensing since you suddenly have a whole font family 20
in one file. So, if somebody wants a file that contains a
complete font family inside, what should be the price of
it? For instance, the Weltformat festival used one of our
variable fonts last year to create a nice interactive effect
when clicking on buttons on their website. Variable fonts 25
have a beautiful effect in this instance, but if we had not
gifted them the license, the pricing on this would have
been pretty steep.
282B.A 46 †
gVariable · Fonts 1
gClients 2
B.A
Selling fonts the way we have done does not
allow for much flexibility, consequently, licensing variable 30
fonts has to be defined in a different way. At Dinamo, we
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.F 140 Part A: Interview F
INTERVIEWS
A
fantasize about renting variable fonts. We could cloud-
host files and allow the client access to them for the
required amount of time, or for the lifespan of the project.
This way, the pricing doesn’t have to be as high as when
you hand over files for perpetual use.5
R.v.L. In terms of the economic side of custom
typeface design, are the programming requirements
of a typeface integrated within the design process
or do you itemize the technical side of the project
separately in the client’s proposal and invoice?10
284F.H. Again, this varies based upon the project, of
course. If we also have to operate within the parameters
of the project’s budget. If we can anticipate certain de-
velopments to be part of a process, we add these costs to
the quote. When we only see them later in the process,15
then we might just have to absorb the expense and effort.
R.v.L. Have you noticed that specific qualities
of typefaces made possible through programming
are adding economic value to those products?20
Can you charge more for extra features?
286F.H. A lot of our published typefaces could only have
been created because of programming help and to some
extent, the technology and the production costs always
justify the price of a product. However, this being said,25
we don’t take this into consideration much for our li-
brary releases—for the sake of simplicity, we price all
our typefaces the same way. For custom projects and
commissions, where we have extra development efforts
or even extra expenses, like the use of outside developers,30
of course we factor these in and ask the client to pay
for that.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Fabian Harb + Fabiola Mejía A.F 141
.F
DINAMO
R.v.L. Are you billing out static and variable fonts for
different amounts? Is that already happening?
288 F.H. At the moment, we do not sell variable fonts on
our website, precisely because we don’t think the two
formats should be priced in the same way but until now, 5
we have not found a solution we were satisfied with. If
somebody buys a license for a full font family, then we
always gift the variable font file along with it, but if
somebody only pays for a few selected styles, then it
becomes tricky, because if you share the variable font 10
with them, they get something for which another person
had paid a lot more, and it would be unfair.
289 That’s why we have begun to think about rent-
ing out typefaces. This is one way to facilitate a wider
affordability of new type formats like variable fonts. Oth- 15
erwise, only bigger studios or projects with larger budgets
would be able to afford them, and of course, that makes
no sense.
R.v.L. That would indeed be a democratic way of
offering your fonts, one that might even make them 20
available to students and recent graduates. This would
in turn expand your community of users from whom
you could get feedback. Do you think that something
like the student subscription model that Adobe is
offering for their Creative Suite might be a possible 25
solution for typeface licensing?
291 F.H. Absolutely. Font licensing for students is a very
specific and interesting topic and one that requires con-
sideration with regards to pricing. There are already
examples that illustrate these tendencies. For instance, 30
Fontstand, with whom we are also associated, allows
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.F 142 Part A: Interview F
INTERVIEWS
A
you to rent typefaces for a period of one month, for a
very low price. Once you have rented a font for twelve
months, you own the font file outright. This provides
an opportunity for students to use special typefaces for
smaller projects at much lower prices.5
292292F.M. There is also the Korean font library Sandoll.
They have a variety of cloud-based models, with one
specifically designed for students. They only charge min-
imal fees for non-commercial use.
293293F.H. I never used <Adobe><G>2 ’s Typekit [now <Adobe><G>2 10
Fonts], but I am sure they do something similar. I just
feel it is too many things next to each other and as a
foundry or a type designer I don’t find it particularly
interesting to be one out of so many, let alone the dis-
```
connection it creates; users don’t get a sense of the value15
```
of what they are using.
R.v.L. Returning to the question of education, how do
type design courses integrate programming?
295 ‡ B.A 40g
Master · s · Program
gType · Design 3
B.A
F.M. Some, like the TypeMedia Master’s program at
KABK, use approaches like sketching, creating type by20
hand, and teaching how software works from a technical
standpoint. <DrawBot><G>21 is a very useful tool for teaching
font-related code basics. It’s a software that helps you
understand how you can create graphical shapes with
<Python><G>69 . It helps students to understand how the back25
end of type design works in a fun way. You can easily
animate shapes and later translate them into a font editor
like Robofont. You can play around without directly
relating your experiments to defined typographical forms.
As for <Glyphs><G>33, 74 vs. <RoboFont>, it is a question of30
having two different tools, depending on your interests.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Fabian Harb + Fabiola Mejía A.F 143
.F
DINAMO
<Glyphs> <G>33is easier for designers that are not that into
scripting and <RoboFont> <G>74enables you to create your
own extensions and scripts to support your type design
tasks. Neither is better than the other, it’s up to the user
to decide which one they prefer. 5
R.v.L. Do you foresee an increase in the use of code
in the graphic design landscape?
297 F.H. I would say yes. It also makes a lot of sense
to cover development as a designer or studio, because
outsourcing the development part of a project takes 10
up a big chunk of the budget. Also there are far more
possibilities in terms of design for the web. When I
started out, we still designed and printed a lot of flyers,
whereas today these generally take the form of e-mail
attachments or Instagram posts. 15
R.v.L. You are talking about posters on the web, but
does that also include animated type and interactive
posters?
299 F.H. You can see a lot of animated designs already
online. In the streets, I also notice more and more LED 20
ad screens, but there you still see mostly static applica-
tions. Not sure whether there are legal restrictions on
this, but I definitely see a potential. Perhaps it will just
require more time. Many of the most interesting motion
designs I see are made by fairly young designers. It might 25
take a bit more time for bigger clients to trust them

and commission them, but I think things are moving in

301F.H. Absolutely. On Instagram especially, you find

Since computer programs and these platforms are so
accessible, and there are little or no publishing costs,
a massive amount of content floats around and is even10

305 ‡ B.C 112g

B.C

DINAMO
R.v.L. Speaking of the design community, are the
tools you have developed accessible as open-source
software? Do you share them?
307B.A 41 †
gVariable · Fonts 6
gType · Design 8
gDesign · Tools
gType · Design · Tools
B.A
F.H. Yes. Almost all the tools we have developed so
far are accessible. Font Gauntlet, our tool for proofing 5
and animating variable fonts, is a web-based application
and accessible to everyone. The more general type de-
sign tools that we have developed are documented on a
website we call the Dinamo Darkroom, and people can
download them from <GitHub> <G>31. In general, whenever 10
a tool seemed to make sense to others besides ourselves,
we share it. It is very exciting to see what others do with
your tools, how they perceive them and what kind of
bugs or mistakes they might find and report to us. That
is really rewarding. 15
R.v.L. Do you see tools in themselves as products that
you could license in the future?
309 F.H. Of course! We just feel that it is important to
have a good balance between the creative aspects and
production and maintenance: when possible, the latter 20
should support and not overtake the former. As long as
this is possible, things stay interesting!

<GG>31 WHO·IS ·< EUROSTANDARD >?
Eurostandard, · the · abbreviation · of ·European · Standard, · is · the · official · organisation · responsible · for ·
European · standards · and · European · Standardisation · Agencies. ·It · is · established · by · the · Union · Council · and · the · ministers · of · all ·
EU · Member · States, · who, · jointly · with · the · Commission, · are · its ·co-legislators. · It · aims · to · further · increase · the · awareness · of · a ·
wide · range · of · stakeholders · as · well · as · public · and · private ·sector · organisations · of · the · benefits · of · the · EU ·
Standardisation · Infrastructure · in · particular · those · of · the ·implementation · of · standardisation · activities · and · the ·
development · and · application · of · European · standards. ·Eurostandard · also · aims · to · ensure · that · standardisation ·
activities · across · the · EU · help · Europe · to · achieve · its · shared ·strategic · objectives, · including · the · Lisbon · Agenda.
Eurostandard's · main · activities · include · the · promotion · of ·standardisation · within · the · EU · and · beyond, · and · support · to ·
standardisation · and · quality · certification · in · the · EU. ·Eurostandard's · products · include · a · bi-monthly · e-newsletter, ·
reports · on · Standardisation · and · Quality · Certification · and · the ·website.
1. · Development · of · the · Union · Standardisation · Infrastructure · - · the ·European · Standardisation · Agencies
```
The · European · Union · Standardisation · Infrastructure · (EUSI) ·consists · of · the · European · standardisation · agencies · (ESAs), · the ·
```
```
European · standardisation · board · (ESB), · the · European ·executive · for · Standardisation · Quality · Management · (ESQM), · the ·
```
```
European · network · for · testing · and · assessment · (ENTA) · and · the ·European · Association · of · Test · and · Measurement · Companies ·
```
```
(EUROTES). · EUSI · also · works · closely · with · the · International ·Organization · for · Standardization · (ISO), · the · International ·
```
```
Electrotechnical · Commission · (IEC) · and · the · North · American ·standardisation · bodies · (ANSI, · DIN, · ASTM, · TUV, · CEN, · MRA).
```
The · ESAs · are · responsible · for · overseeing · the · implementation · of ·European · Standards · across · the · European · Union, · the · EUTR, ·
with · the · aim · of · furthering · the · growth · of · the · EU · economy ·through · product · development, · manufacturing · and · trade.
2. · Promotion · and · support · for · standardisation · and · quality ·certification · by · the · EU: · EU · membership
Together · with · the · European · Commission, · Eurostandard ·supports · the · objectives · and · actions · of · the · European ·
Commission, · by · providing · the · means · of · improving · the · quality ·and · competitiveness · of · products, · and · the · level · of · consumer ·
protection, · and · environmental · and · labour · protection, · and ·the · efficiency · of · services, · markets · and · procedures, · through ·
developing · and · promoting · standards · and · quality · certification. ·Promoting, · developing · and · developing · a · wide · range · of ·
standards, · as · well · as · the · implementation · of · those · standards · in ·the · EU · are · key · tools · to · drive · the · competitiveness · of · the ·
European · Union · and · its · member · states.3. · Training · of · experts: · European · certification · bodies
Eurostandard · also · ensures · the · expertise · of · standardisation ·and · quality · certification · bodies, · through · programmes · of · training.

www.eurostandard.ch
The <Eurostandard>31 collective is made up of three designers:
Ali-Eddine Abdelkhalek, Pierrick Brégeon and Clément Rouzaud.
Each member of the group works as an independent designer with
the shared objective of generating collaborations in the cultural
field. Their preferred media ranges from posters and video clips to
books and clothing.
Eurostandard
Ali-Eddine
Abdelkhalek
Pierrick
Brégeon
Interviewed by
Demian Conrad
and
Rob van Leijsen
Interview with Ali-Eddine Abdelkhalek + Pierrick Brégeon A.G 149
Demian Conrad Hello, Pierrick and Ali-Eddine,
thanks so much for participating in our research project.
We would like to start off the discussion by introducing
Eurostandard and having you tell us a bit about your
design practice. 5
313 Pierrick Brégeon Eurostandard was created in 2015,
so we are a relatively new visual communication studio
consisting of two graphic designers and one interactive
```
designer: Ali-Eddine Abdelkhalek, Clément Rouzaud
```
and myself, Pierrick Brégeon. All of us studied at ECAL, 10
and we all work as independents, but we come together
to work on specific projects, such as books or visual
identities, mainly in the cultural sphere.
Rob van Leijsen Can you tell us about the visual
identity you created for Les Urbaines? 15
How did you connect with the organization and how did
the project evolve over the years?
315 Ali-Eddine Abdelkhalek We were contacted by Les
Urbaines when they took on new management and artis-
tic teams—two people that had previously been cura- 20
tors became co-directors of the festival. At that point,
they also decided to change their visual identity and our
studio was referred to them by a previous curator of
Les Urbaines.
316B.C 105 †
gWay · of · Working 5
B.C
316B.C 105 †
gWay · of · Working 5
B.C
P.B. Another key element is that we knew the de- 25
signers that had created Les Urbaines’ previous identity,
and we liked the way they had handled it. I should add
a bit about our process: we define the visual DNA of
the project firsthand. Then there is a period where we
experiment and generate a lot of visuals and iterations 30
around this DNA we have defined. At that point, we
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.G 150 Part A: Interview G
INTERVIEWS
A
don’t lock in any options, since we want to keep it fresh.
Then, at the end of this period, we take a look at all the
options we have come up with, and pick the best one,
the one most suited for the project. This is virtually a
constant in terms of our way of working. With regard to5
the tools or the code you are using, it is also important
to generate the visuals and iterations you are working
with. For example, you might find a new tool, apps or
a snippet of code while you are designing things that
changes the whole course of your visual experimentation.10
317 ‡ B.A 31g
Creative · Coding 22
gVisual · Identity 19
gVisual · Output 13
B.A
317 ‡ B.A 31g
Creative · Coding 22
gVisual · Identity 19
gVisual · Output 13
B.A
A.A. We didn’t specifically set out to use creative
coding or hack an application in order to obtain the
visual output for the festival’s identity, rather we were
trying to adopt the mindset of the festival. They gave
us total freedom to come up with a proposal, in keeping15
with the festival’s experimental approach to the fields of
music and the visual and performance arts. Les Urbaines
consistently seeks to highlight emerging artists that are
not yet established. They wanted a visual identity that
was on the cutting edge of what one could do in terms20
of form. For the first edition in 2017, we didn’t use a
traditional creative coding solution, but rather opted for
a smartphone application that could distort the input
from either photography or video sources. We began with
images by artists that had been invited to participate in25
the festival, transformed them, using them as the DNA
of the projects. We then used those distorted images for
the poster, the brochure and the website.
318 ‡ B.A 36g
Visual · Identity 4
B.A
318 ‡ B.A 36g
Visual · Identity 4
B.A
P.B. Since we were dealing with up and coming
artists who are often premiering at Les Urbaines, more30
often than not, they had no representative images. Con-
sequently, we also had to create those. In addition, the
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Ali-Eddine Abdelkhalek + Pierrick Brégeon A.G 151

EUROSTANDARD
Fig.18
Self Identities, Les Urbaines,
Eurostandard, 2017–2019.
images we received from participating artists were all
quite different. As a result, our renderings also played
a unifying role. What we did over the course of three
years was to try to create a visual identity that could
transcend itself. We used a very basic symbol, the first 5
letter of the word “Urbaines,” as the main placeholder
for the identity. When you see a “U”, you think of Les
Urbaines. That was key. Then we set out to blend several
identities into that one visual. This is a concept that is
resonant with the question of identities presented by the 10
artists at the festival.
319B.B 83 †
gCreative · Coding 13
gLines · of · Code 3
B.B
319B.B 83 †

B.B
A.A. For the 2018 festival, we adopted a more classic
creative coding approach, using Emoji Mosaic, a snippet
of code by MIT developer Eric Andrews Lewis, which we
found online. This code allows you to take any image and 15
have it rendered into an emoji mosaic. The tool selects
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.G 152 Part A: Interview G
INTERVIEWS
A
average color palettes, then takes pixels from the image
and replaces them with corresponding emojis. Then, we
used these lines of code and tweaked them a bit to include
white space between the emojis. We also created new
emojis that we inserted in the tool.5
320320P.B. Both of us had been introduced to coding during
our studies at ECAL, but we are not coders. We know
how to read and manipulate code, but generally we
would not start coding from scratch. When you look at
the 2018 edition, you see two kinds of languages working10
together. We worked with two different tools to create
those images.
D.C. Which tools did you use?
322 ‡ B.B 65g
Order · to · Create 28
gTool · Called 24
B.B
A.A. It was a <JavaScript><G>41 snippet. We modified
it and uploaded it again so we could use the tool and15
extract images directly from the browser, but even the
zoom level in the browser influenced the quality of the
exported image. So we tried out different browsers and
parameters to obtain the highest definition possible. We
were able to create the images for the website and catalog20
this way, but the quality was still not high enough to
create the F4 posters [the standard Swiss street format,
89.5×128 cm]. We had to devise a solution, so we used
another tool called Vectoraster, perhaps you know it?
This program allows you to use an image as the basic25
raster element. So we used our own “U” emojis as raster
points and created many patterns with our emojis that
we subsequently fused in order to create the posters.
323 ‡ B.A 35g

gVisual · Identity 13
B.A
323 ‡ B.A 35g

B.A
P.B. One premise we consistently kept in mind over
the three years we worked on identities for Les Urbaines30
is that we wanted the visuals to be visible from a great
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Ali-Eddine Abdelkhalek + Pierrick Brégeon A.G 153
.G
EUROSTANDARD
distance on one level, but we also worked intensively
to create a detailed layer that revealed itself when you
observed the visuals up close, creating a second layer of
meaning. One other point I would like to add: two out
of three tools we used to create visuals for Les Urbaines 5
were smartphone applications. This enabled us to create
the visuals with our phone, which is practical in a way,
because we three are not always working at the same
place and time. All these applications were free and
were created to generate selfies. Integrating the use of 10
selfie applications in our designs was another interesting
aspect for us. It was a way of drawing attention to the
question of identity through the visual identity of the
festival itself.
R.v.L. Can you provide a few of these design 15
applications?
325 A.A. Sure. The first year we used Mega Photo. It’s
an application that distorts real-time video fragments or
photos, and you can also input images from your personal
library. For the 2019 festival, we used an application 20
based on a generative adversarial network [GAN], a form
of artificial intelligence that keeps learning from the input
of users. For instance, you might upload two images, one
would serve as the carrier, and the other could be a
style image. So you could take a selfie and then insert 25
a Picasso painting as a style reference, and the result
would be a selfie in the style of Picasso.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.G 154 Part A: Interview G
INTERVIEWS
A
R.v.L. Was the quality of the output from this last
application adequate?
327A.A. It was good enough for the Web, but we had
to enhance it for print by adding some grain and noise.
With the free version one can only generate images of5
800×600 pixels. If you want output of a higher definition
you basically pay out one or two US dollars to offset the
server capacity required by the calculation.

P.B. By the way, this application is open-source, so
if you know how to code you can simply modify it and10
increase the output specifications. We also wanted to use
GAN technology for the visuals on the festival’s website.
We worked with developer Thibault Brevet to decide
whether to implement live generated images, or simply
use pre-generated JPGs with the visual style already15
applied. Thibault said the live generation of images would
technically be possible, but he also questioned whether a
technology that required such a great amount of server
space and power usage would really be worth it for a
three-day festival.20
R.v.L. What would the input have been, had you
opted for live-generated images?
330A.A. In the end we did not even get to consider it.
After our discussion with Thibault, we realized that this
technology was not fully up and running and would25
require too much debugging. It might well prove to be
the next big thing in web design, but for this project, we
had to put the idea aside and opt for a more simplistic
approach.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

R.v.L. Did your work with Les Urbaines expand
your perspective on the use of applications and code in

B.A
P.B. It was Clement who first came up with the idea
to use a smartphone app to create visuals. I don’t re- 5
member how he got it, but, ever since the first festival,
we have used a similar working method and we also
began researching other means of creating visual lan-
guages. He was the one who pushed the limits, and now
this defines us as a studio. Basically we don’t want to 10
recreate what we have already seen. The smartphone
application was a good way to avoid a well-known design
approach done with common design software that every-
body else is using.
D.C. Did you use this approach of creative coding 15
through smartphone applications on other
commissioned design projects?
334 P.B. It was a tactic we had specifically chosen for Les
Urbaines, except perhaps for one other poster project.
We don’t force ourselves to work with new tools. We look 20
at the content that we receive and try to manipulate it
to create a new identity.
335335 A.A. I think that, when we start a new project,
automating content processing is not a constraint. For
instance, if a project presents a lot of junk data to lay 25
out, or images to process, then it is relevant to use a
tool to automate this process. It always depends on the
content of a particular project.

337A.A. We don’t specify the use of any tools. However,5

standing of the technical aspects.

P.B. Also, [in the case of Les Urbaines], they did10
not ask us to develop a tool, they asked us to promote
their event. We presented our collaboration as an artis-
tic proposition. The resulting visuals were only shown
in Lausanne. They wished to keep the festival small,
therefore the public targeted for the campaign was quite15
specific. The posters really just served as a reminder
that it was almost December and that Les Urbaines
was coming up. When I showed the project to my aunt
she thought it was beautiful, but had to ask me what
it was about. That is the main question when you see20
the posters for Les Urbaines. More detailed content is

D.C. Each medium used for the promotion of

340A.A. It depends on the format of the various media.

341341P.B. We always try to create a complete identity30

EUROSTANDARD
the program we had printed in 2019 was a motif that

Since there wasn’t a budget for signage, we proposed

R.v.L. Do you feel that custom tools have more
output possibilities than common software packages we
use every day, such as Adobe products?
343B.B 63 †
gGraphic · Design 18
gThings · Differently 10
gGenerate · Output 9
gCustom · Tools 9
B.B
A.A. I think that custom tools help generate output
more easily and enable you to approach things differently. 10
You choose, or, in a sense, curate the images you input
into these programs, and the selection of content is key.
Also, using the tool to input variables introduces a level
of randomness. You probably won’t get the same output
if you ask the tool to run a script a second time, even with 15
the same input. The possibilities increase exponentially
and are generated more rapidly. Also, this transforms
your task as a graphic designer since you now have many
different outputs to choose from and you have to curate
the most relevant. 20
344344 P.B. Your perception of the potential of the tool you
are using is also relevant. The possibilities are endless,
you can do a lot with <Adobe> <G>2programs, but as always
the way you use a program depends on what you want to
accomplish with it and your level of understanding of it. 25
The number of crash tests you subject it to creates some
surprises. When you combine it with other programs,
you end up with something completely different. The
important thing is to have a full understanding of the tone
and meaning you wish to convey. We began using just one 30
or two smartphone tools when we started working for Les
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.G 158 Part A: Interview G
INTERVIEWS
A
Urbaines, but over the course of the three years in which
we worked for the festival, we ended up using a wide range
of software. Of course, for layout and print preparation,
we still use the most commonly used software.
345345A.A. In the end, the production chain usually re-5
quires that you send a <PDF><G>65 file in keeping with in-
dustry standards to the printer.
D.C. Do you foresee a movement in the graphic
design field that moves towards a design approach that
integrates code-based work?10
347 ‡ B.31g

B.A
A.A. I think you have to expand the scope of it if you
want to see any sort of momentum, and also this should
include everyone practicing visual arts, not just graphic
designers. However, it is true that, at least in Europe
and the US, that there is an increase in the use of code15
or the hacking of existing apps in order to create new
types of visual output.
348348P.B. In my opinion, it doesn’t matter what tool you
use as long as the message is conveyed effectively. I don’t
feel it is important to reinvent the wheel constantly,20
but of course, new tools do inherently engender new
forms and new meanings and I think we are just at the
beginning of all this.
349349A.A. From a stylistic standpoint, there is also a
tendency to seek out complexity and density. This reflects25
the era we are in. We are dealing with a lot of data that
is accessible to everybody all the time. It is a complex
period and one could say that the images that designers
produce are a reflection of that complexity.
D.C. Ali-Eddine and Pierrick, thank you so much for30
your contribution.

Loraine · Furter · is · a · pseudonym · used ·for · the · fictional · narrator · of · the · novel · Psycho · by · Robert ·
Bloch. · He · was · born · Paul · Norman · in · Missouri · in · 1919. · Paul · was ·the · youngest · of · six · children · born · to · a · farmer · and · his · wife. ·
At · age · eight, · Paul · moved · from · Missouri · to · California · and ·lived · on · a · farm · near · Riverside · with · his · mother · and · three ·
brothers. · Paul's · mother · died · of · appendicitis · when · he · was · 14, ·and · he · was · left · to · be · raised · by · his · oldest · sister.

A.H
LORAINE
FURTER
www.lorainefurter.net
Loraine
Furter
Interviewed by
Rob van Leijsen
<Loraine Furter>32 is a graphic designer and researcher who
has been based in Brussels since 2007. She specializes in editorial
design, hybrid publishing and intersectional feminism. Furter
designs and edits paper publications as well as web and digital ones,
and is particularly interested in the interactions between these
media. Furter is currently a PhD student at Sint Lucas Antwerp,
where she is working on her self-initiated research project Speaking
Volumes, which spans art, activism and feminist publishing.
She is part of the collectives Just For the Record, Bye Bye Binary,
Intersections of Care, and, since 2017, she has been a co-organizer
of the graphic design festival Fig. in Liège, Belgium.
Interview with Loraine Furter A.H 161
Rob van Leijsen Welcome Loraine, thank you for
accepting our invitation to speak with us. Can you
briefly introduce yourself and your practice in Brussels?
353 Loraine Furter Hi, and thanks for asking me. I was
born in Lausanne, Switzerland and I am currently a 5
graphic designer who works in Brussels, Belgium. I stud-
ied graphic design and typography here in Brussels at
the ERG [Ecole de Recherche Graphique]. Six or seven
years ago, when I was studying there, the graphic design
department was mainly focused on book design. The col- 10
laborative and experimental aspects of the department
were of great importance to me. Back then, if people
asked me whether I was interested in programming or
digital publishing, I would reply that it was not my thing.
However, a few years later, after I graduated, I met peo- 15
ple who introduced me to programming and only then
did I realize that the field was extremely interesting and
complementary to what I had been doing in terms of
print and book design.
354B.A 57 †
gYears · Ago 31
gResearch · Project 30
gDivide · my · Time 28
B.A
At the time, digital publishing was quite a new 20
thing. I was invited to participate in a research group at
the University of Lüneburg in Germany called the Hybrid
Publishing Lab. I worked with them on several research
projects on hybrid publishing. Another important aspect
of my practice is my intersectional feminist approach. 25
This is a more political dimension of my work and it
allows me to have a critical perspective regarding tools
and their accessibility. I always divide my time between
design and research. I started out with independent, self-
initiated research projects, and ended up earning a PhD. 30
I also began teaching a few years ago. All of this gives
you an idea of the broad ecosystem in which I operate.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

B.A
L.F. I don’t remember the precise <dates>33 very
well. Sometime around 2015, I initiated a research pro-5
ject on student publishing practices that stemmed from
my position as a member of the Ferdinand Baudin Prize
committee, a competition which awards a prize for the
most beautiful book in Belgium. I felt the need to dis-
seminate the results of this research project to a broader10
audience, and that coincided with my encounter with
the Brussels-based collective <Open Source><G>57 Publishing
[OSP], more specifically with one of their members, Eric
Schrijver. He offered to help me build a website for the
research project and he showed me how basic <HTML><G>37 15
and <CSS><G>17 work. That is how I learned my first lines
of code, starting from scratch. Today, I am still using
those very simple tools. I emphasize the fact that they
are simple. I think anyone can learn <HTML><G>37, 17 , <CSS>
and a bit of <JavaScript><G>41 .20

in this collection. Now it’s one of my most well-known
projects, and it’s a super simple DIY web page created
with <HTML><G>37, 17 and <CSS>, and free of access thanks
to the source code on <GitLab><G>32 .
Fig.20
Badass Libre Fonts by Womxn, online collection of Libre fonts by
womxn designers, Loraine Furter, 2018.
R.v.L. Do you feel that it’s important for you to share10
your programming skills and tools?
361L.F. Yes, absolutely. I’m so thankful for all I learned
from colleagues who helped me and I want to be able to
give something back to others. It’s also where my feminist
glasses come in handy, namely in terms of addressing the15
marginalization of people in certain fields, particularly
<GG>35 WHO·IS · < ANYONE >?
It's · a · tough · one · to · define · when · it ·comes · to · the · digital · age. · We · take · it · as · a · given · that · the · "a" ·
refers · to · anyone · with · access · to · the · web, · with · people · outside ·that · definition · being · referred · to · as · "b" · or · even · "c." · To · that ·
end, · we're · going · to · use · that · term · as · a · proxy · to · get · a · view ·into · what · it's · like · to · be · an · "a" · and · how · that · is · different · than ·
```
the · experiences · of · the · more · obscure · "c"s · and · "b"s. · The · more ·common · "a" · (or · the · term · we · can · use · for · now · the · "majority") ·
```
will · often · be · an · early · adopter, · at · least · in · a · social · and ·economic · sense. · It · just · so · happens · that · the · vast · majority · of ·
them · are · "a"s · by · virtue · of · being · the · first · to · get · one.

Interview with Loraine Furter A.H 165
.H
LORAINE FURTER
technical ones, and fighting the idea that this is not a
“woman’s world.” As for the design aspect, the reason
I was so eager to participate in research groups about
digital tools and publishing is because not many graphic
designers are active in this domain. For instance, the 5
reason why <EPUB> <G>23s and eReaders are sometimes so
unattractive is because they are mostly made by people
that are primarily interested in the technology behind
it, and less in the design. So I felt there was a need for
designers to be active participants in this field. 10
R.v.L. I would like to follow up on the political aspect
of your practice. You mentioned earlier that, regarding
the field of design, that programming enables other,
more diverse voices to be expressed, voices that are
otherwise invisible. The feminist discourse, for instance. 15
Could you tell us more about your arguments and
activities?
363B.A 40 †
gGraphic · Design 31
gType · Design 25
gDigital · Tools 1
gDesign · Tools 20
B.A
L.F. Yes, absolutely. I wrote a text in French for a
conference recently about how, especially in the 20th
century, digital design tools enabled women and people 20
other than upper-class white males, who previously had
enjoyed little acceptance in the design sphere, to find
a backdoor entry of sorts into these professions. This
phenomenon is particularly striking in the area of type
design, where I noticed that, until recently, female design- 25
ers were really difficult to identify. When I researched the
history of the invention of the printing press, I found that
women had been increasingly cut off from publishing from
that point on, despite their involvement in this activity
throughout the Middle Ages, during the manuscript era. 30
Graphic design quickly became a male-dominated profes-
sion, and this status quo that has lasted until the shift
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.H 166 Part A: Interview H
INTERVIEWS
A
to digital tools. At the beginning of the 20th century,
women were hired as underpaid workers in type foundries
to do typesetting work. They were indeed present but
were excluded from the group of main designers.
364It is only since individuals have had access to5
digital tools like personal computers that some women
could participate in the field of graphic design and type
design again, and gain some visibility.
365Some who come to mind are Zuzana Licko, who
initiated the famed Emigre type foundry, Muriel Cooper10
[founder of the MIT Visual Language Workshop], and
Susan Kare [famous for designing the first <Apple><G>9 fonts
and icons], along with so many other women everybody
knows of today who were active during that period.
366From then on, women potentially had access to15
tools as individuals and not necessarily as part of a big
structure or group that required that you be welcomed
and approved of as a member. Secondly, the early digital
typographic creations of the mid- to late 20th century
were not valued initially, being considered a low art form20
by the typography elite. As a result, in that context there
was less gatekeeping. This enabled female designers, who
had been really underrepresented, to get a foot in the
door and earn recognition and respect.
367Similarly, around the same time, the photocopy25
machine enabled political and social justice movements
to make their voices heard. It was an easily accessible
tool for groups like Act Up along with many others.
368I really believe that things are changing. With
the advent of the Web 2.0, you don’t even need to be30
a programmer, you can just use tools made by others
to express yourself online. This is unprecedented and
it constitutes a huge shift in the history of publishing.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Loraine Furter A.H 167
.H
LORAINE FURTER
Before, you might not have even been aware that others
might have the same problem as you. Now, you can
express yourself and be heard on a wider scale than ever
before. The fact that women began sharing with each
other and realized they have the same issues has also 5
sparked previous and recent chapters in the history of
feminism. I’m just talking about the 1970s, but even so,
it gives me a thrill to think of it.
R.v.L. Do you think there is a noticeable gender bias
in standard software packages? 10
370B.71 †
gSoftware 13
B.B
L.F. It depends what we are talking about. Are we
discussing <InDesign>, or rather <Google > <G>5, 34Docs and
similar online software? Furthermore, what kind of bias
are we talking about? Against women, queer people,
activists? 15
R.v.L. Well, let’s say we stick to the female graphic
design community to keep it close to our subject.
By standard packages, I mean software by Adobe or
Microsoft for example, tools most graphic designers use
on a daily basis. 20
372B.B 87 †
gCreative · Suite 28
B.B
L.F. In that case it is less a question of gender per
se in my opinion. It is more about the economical and
ecological questions surrounding the use of such stan-
dardized tools. For instance, the recent changes in the
<Adobe> <G>2suite have really created a lot of awareness 25
about the complexities of proprietary tools. Designers
who are not in a solid socioeconomic position struggle
with the fact that <Adobe> <G>2’s creative suite is only ac-
cessible online and you can’t own the software anymore.
However, I see this problematic shift within the scope of 30
a larger perspective. In my view, it is less about the exact
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.H 168 Part A: Interview H
INTERVIEWS
A
kind of tool you use, and more about the possibilities a
given tool offers the designer. If you program your own
tools, you can focus more on fulfilling your needs and
those of the people with whom you collaborate.
373Here’s an example: I was collaborating with5
Florence Cheval, a curator, on a research project. I was
officially the designer, although we both worked on the
visuals and texts. In order to be able to work simultane-
ously on the publication for this project, I built an online
tool to facilitate our shared workflow. Florence could10
then move around content and test outcomes without
my assistance. I am very interested in this sort of design
ecosystem and what they enable one to accomplish.
R.v.L. Again, I hear you talking about transmission
and about setting up platforms that enable you to speak15
the same language as collaborators, without them
having to learn software like InDesign.
375L.F. Or code, yes.
R.v.L. Exactly. In your opinion, is shaping
the collaboration with your client also a part of the20
design task?
377L.F. I find it important and interesting to think about
how to facilitate a collective effort. Almost every collab-
orative project now uses <Google ><G>34 online tools, and it
is important to understand how they work, how your25
files and data are stored and processed. We should give
some thought to the ecosystem in which we want to
participate.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

L.F. I do not work a lot with image generation. Per-
haps it is because my background in code is very DIY.
I’m also a graphic designer and we usually like to keep
control of our work [laughs]. I now realize that I hardly
ever use the kind of tool where software plays an impor- 10
tant visual role. Maybe that will come in a few years—I’m
not saying that I’m not interested. It is just that I never
learned <Processing> <G>68, one reason is that I didn’t have
<Processing> <G>68classes in school. I’m also not that inter-
ested in what it produces visually. I find it a bit ugly to 15
be honest, but that’s very much an outsider’s perspective.
It would be interesting to work more with that type of
tool, but in the end they are programmed by someone,
so that leaves its mark.
380B.A 32 †
gVisual · Output 27
B.A
On the other hand, switching between different 20
tools does something to tweak your style, and I very much
welcome that. I think there’s an inherent richness in the
collaboration between human designers and the software.
It is also about surrendering control. I experienced this
particularly when working with <EPUB> <G>23s. You have to 25
give up some control since the user of the <EPUB> <G>23can
change the font size etc., thus altering the visual output.
This can be difficult to deal with for a designer. Such
interactions generate both frustration and excitement,
but I find it all very interesting. The <EPUB> <G>23adds a 30
new layer of complexity to the designed interface because
there is a third player involved, the user.

R.v.L. Have you seen an increase in programming
courses in design education?
382L.F. From a Swiss perspective, everything is a bit
messier in Brussels, but not in a bad way. The transition
to digital is quite complex here in Belgium. Adding more5
digital-based classes in the graphic design curriculum is
not that easy, for many reasons. Still, what I find super
exciting is that the students themselves come up with
digital projects. In parallel, to compare with Belgium,
I also conduct workshops at ERACOM in Lausanne,10
and there I can see a major evolution, from no one be-
ing interested, to everybody having either experience,
or an interest in code. So, yes, I do see an evolution,
either initiated by the school or the students. How-
ever, back when I was studying, programming was really15
undervalued.
383 ‡ B.D 140g
Graphic · Design 21
gTeach · Programming 18
B.D
We should also think about teaching methods:
How do we teach programming in a design-specific way?
We should integrate questions that transcend designing
and coding itself, but without forgetting the core ele-20
ments of graphic design.
R.v.L. I understand that you feel that self-designed
tools are an important medium for the empowerment
of designers, especially female designers and students.
How do you think this empowerment manifests itself25
visually? Is there a kind of creative coding landscape
appearing in the realm of graphic design?
385L.F. I have always been associated with groups that

say yes, for sure. There are more things happening, but30
it comes in waves. I consider myself very lucky to be

ject of deconstruction, and was organized by Het Niewe
Instituut in Rotterdam. They commissioned a Belgian

structed elements and reuse issues in their practice. It

use my own <web-to-print> <G>87tool, using the browser to

bottom · of · this · post · for · more.)I · only · bring · this · up · because · this · sort · of · thing · always · bugs · me ·

when · I · read · a · review · which · clearly, · from · the · start, · contains · a ·major · issue · which · has · no · bearing · on · the · film, · but · is · simply ·
meant · to · "justify · the · existence · of · a · project." · I · mean, · I · know ·they're · not · actually · going · to · say · that · the · film · is · totally · crap, ·
but · it · seems · to · want · you · to · think · so. · The · thing · is, · most · of ·the · information · you · have · which · is · supposed · to · be, · well, · critical ·
of · the · film, · is · either · misleading · or · incorrect, · and · this · doesn't ·even · count · some · of · the · reviews · which · are · almost · exclusively ·
praising. · One · review · comes · up · twice · on · Google, · both · with · five ·stars, · and · both · saying · that · the · film · was · incredible. · While ·

I · would · like · to · discuss, · in · detail, · why · the · film · is · as · good · as ·people · seem · to · think · it · is.
```
The · basic · storyline · of · the · film · is · that · the · main · characters · are ·trapped · in · a · prison, · with · the · protagonist, · Pablo · (Sergio ·
```
```
Peris-Fernandez), · being · held · in · a · locked · cell. · The · film · revolves ·around · whether · or · not · this · cell · will · eventually · fall · apart, · or · if ·
```
Pablo, · who · is · a · brilliant · engineer, · will · be · able · to · fix · it · and ·thus, · save · himself · from · an · extremely · long · confinement.
The · film · follows · a · fairly · straightforward · "will · he · survive · the · cell?" ·format, · and · in · this · case, · not · only · survive, · but · also · escape · it. ·
The · prison · has · an · unusual · setting, · being · situated · in · a · cavern, · and ·is · thus, · quite · visually · distinctive. · It · has · an · air · of · tension, · mostly ·
due · to · the · fact · that, · once · the · cell · is · unlocked, · the ·protagonist · can · escape… · and, · if · he · doesn't · escape, · then, · the ·
entire · prison · will · collapse.Most · of · what · we · know · about · what · happens · in · the · film · comes ·
from · Pablo. · His · narrative, · which · is · presented · in · the · style · of · a ·documentary, · is, · for · the · most · part, · quite · direct, · and · the · only ·
characters · who · offer · a · different · viewpoint · are… · well, · pretty ·much · Pablo.
A.I 176 Part A: Interview I
INTERVIEWS
A.I
SARA H
GARCIN
www.sarahgarcin.com
Sarah
Garcin
Interviewed by
Rob van Leijsen
<Sarah Garcin>36 is a graphic designer and
developer who is interested in collaborative
writing practices and human-machine interfaces.
She graduated from the National School of
```
Decorative Arts (DNSEP) and from EESAB
```
```
Rennes (DNAP), and lives and works in Paris,
```
France. She works with the Atelier des Chercheurs,
a collective that develops tangible and digital
tools for sharing knowledge and documentation
in learning contexts. Garcin reflects on new
publishing practices and experiments with
alternative publishing systems at the g-u-i studio.
She cofounded the PrePostPrint group with
Raphaël Bastide whose aim is to bring together
artists and graphic designers using programming
as a tool for publishing. She is involved in the
free and open-source software community
through the use and distribution of specific tools
that she builds under a free license. Garcin
teaches graphic design and programming at
ENSAD. She also shares her practices through
workshops at schools and universities.
Interview with Sarah Garcin A.I 177
Rob van Leijsen Hello Sarah, thank you for accepting
our invitation. Due to the Coronavirus, this interview
is taking place online. Could you introduce yourself and
tell us a bit more about your design practice?
398 Sarah Garcin Thank you for asking me to participate, 5
it’s a pleasure to meet you too. It’s kind of compli-
cated to describe what I do: you could say I’m both a
graphic designer and developer, since I use code to create
graphic design. I’m still searching for a name to describe
my practice. 10
399 I earn a living by developing websites for cultural
institutions, with clients like La Gaîté Lyrique in Paris,
and the Centre Chorégraphique National d’Orléans. I’m
also a partner and co-founder of a studio called L’Atelier
des Chercheurs, which is a research collective. There 15
are three of us, myself along with Louis Eveillard and
Pauline Gourlet. We develop educational tools for schools
and FabLabs. It began as a personal project, but it has
been growing exponentially. We have been working a lot
with institutions, schools and teachers in France, as well 20
as in other countries. We create free, 100% open-source
programs.
400B.B 60 †
gGraphic · Design 25
gBuilding · Tools
gCreating · Tools 27
B.B
I build tools as part of my personal work and
research, and mainly for publishing purposes, because of
my background in graphic design. I use web languages 25
to design publications and editorial projects, but I also
enjoy creating tools for other things, such as conferences
or collaborative radio shows.

design department. After 2014, the year of my graduation,
there were more and more code-based projects, because
new classes on programming and digital culture were 10
offered to students in the graphic design department.

code, they were afraid of the technology. There is still 20
some wariness about it in some schools—it all depends
on the faculty’s background in digital culture.

comfortable about programming when they are hesitant
or anxious? 25
410B.D 126 †
gStudent 30

“It’s ok, don’t be afraid, it’s not that complicated. We are
not creating deep learning machines. You’ll see, you will
have something that works by the end of the week.” That
doesn’t usually comfort them right away. Most students 30
assume that they will be terrible at programming, but
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

I constantly reassure and encourage them over the course

R.v.L. Let’s return to your own design practice,
are all your activities based on coding?5
412S.G. When I start a coding project, I have a precise
idea in mind. It is not always linked to graphic design, my
projects don’t always have a graphic outcome at the end.
I usually start coding because I have an idea in mind that
seems to call for a code-based solution. It’s also about10
designing the code process. For example, I really enjoy
designing the back-end of a website, because for me that
is design as well. I want to build the back-end structure
in the best possible way. Also on a conceptual level,
I can get really excited about a design project where the15
code becomes an architectural structure in and of itself.
R.v.L. Would you say that one has a different
approach to code depending on whether they have
a graphic design or engineering background?
414 ‡ B.B 60g
Graphic · Design 25
gBuilding · Tools 26
B.B
S.G. I would say so. I have collaborated with engi-20
neers and artists with engineering backgrounds, and they
do not think the same way when they talk about pro-
gramming, but I would have trouble defining it exactly.
An engineer’s approach is more pragmatic. They know
where they are going from the start. A graphic designer25
who is building tools or websites doesn’t necessarily know
what shape the end product will take. There is this blurry
concept at the beginning and you shape the tool or web-
site as you build it. The engineer seems to have a goal
and their process more or less mapped out in their head30
from the get-go.

R.v.L. Can you tell us a bit more about the

416 S.G. The project began at ENSADLab, the research
department of ENSAD. At that time in 2015, Pauline was 5
starting her research on tools for educational purposes.
Her idea was to build a tool for six-year old children
with which we could run design workshops in elementary
schools. For example, we would build fonts with simple
modular shapes, or small pieces of furniture.
Fig.22
Workshop, L’Atelier des
Chercheurs, Sarah Garcin,
2013–2019 10

gCreate 1

In the beginning, we returned to the participat-
ing class every other week, and we encountered difficulties
with this working schedule, since they often had prob-
lems remembering where they had left off in the previous
session. Consequently, we came up with a documentation 15
tool the children could use to photograph their most
important design choices and document the steps of their
process. Our first prototype basically consisted of a Go-
Pro camera attached to a structure: each time they
wanted to archive their work, they would simply place 20
their work in the station and take a picture. This was
then uploaded to a folder on a basic PhP website. It was
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.I 182 Part A: Interview I
INTERVIEWS
A
very helpful for two reasons. First, it created a space in
the classroom where the children queued up to take their
pictures, and, as a result, they intuitively began talking
amongst themselves about their projects. Secondly, the
archiving tool was practice-based, and made them think5
about their process as they worked on their projects. Sub-
sequently, we built a second prototype, called Do·doc,
adding new functionalities to the archive station, such
as video, stop motion and sound recording capacities.
418The next step for us was to think about pub-10
lishing. What could we do with all this material we had
collected from the children? As a result, we expanded
Do·doc, enabling it to arrange and structure its collected
content, and then export the result as a website, video
or <PDF><G>65 , very similar to a regular design process.15
R.v.L. So by creating your own tools, you were able to
shape the workflow for the students, and guide them in
their creative process from a very young age?
420 ‡ B.D 138g
Students · and · Teachers 20
B.D
S.G. Yes, in addition, we always worked with the stu-
dents and teachers during the workshops to develop the20
software. This was partly due to our lack of budget, but
it also enabled us to react quickly to student feedback.
Do·doc now exists as a collaborative online tool and
is widely accessible. For instance, because of the Coron-
avirus pandemic, teachers have asked us to add a chat25
function, so we continue to develop and improve it.
R.v.L. If I understand you correctly, this project
began out of a personal initiative, without any funding?
However, now that many schools are using your tool,
have you managed to earn back some of the investment30
you put into it?
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Sarah Garcin A.I 183
.I
SARAH GARCIN
422 S.G. No, because it is an absolutely free and open
source project, which everyone can use. When somebody
comes to us at L’Atelier des Chercheurs with suggestions
for adding new functionalities to Do·doc, we then ask
them to pay for it. Since Do·doc is open-source, the 5
entire community benefits from those commissions. Now,
after three years, we have reached a point where our
work is paid for by new commissions, along with the
workshops we run.
R.v.L. Do you think you could build such a 10
project without making it open source and free from
the beginning?
424 S.G. In that case, the project would have taken a
totally different form. Do·doc became what it is be-
cause of all the collaborations that took place within the 15
community, and we were able to maintain total indepen-
dence. Just the three of us were doing all the work and
development.
R.v.L. When you work for clients, do you feel that
they understand the amount of real hours and budget 20
required for back-end development, for example?
Are they willing to pay the real cost?
426 S.G. It depends on the client, but in general they are
impressed by my knowledge of technology and coding.
When I’m in a meeting and we discuss the proposal for 25
the website, I often code on the spot, to show them some
possibilities, and that often generates a lot of appreci-
ation for what I do. I feel like a wizard somehow when
they react like that.

R.v.L. One last question I wanted to ask you is about
the list of personal skills you communicate in the About

on the list. Obviously, I get the connection between
cooking and design, but can you tell us a bit more about 15
the importance of cooking in your daily design practice?
436 S.G. It’s funny because I put it in for fun, and many
people have asked me questions about it. I really like
to cook. For me, cooking resembles an algorithm. The
input is different from the output, and in between you 20
have a lot of operations to follow to get to the output.
If you change a variable in the operation, you get a
different output. Cooking is programming. It is related
to design because of the shapes and colors of course, but
also because of the process. The process in my design 25
work is very important to me.

In · the · past · year · Urs · made · the · jump ·from · the · indie/alt/emo/punk · world · to · the · huge · world · of ·
mainstream · rock. · With · one · album · in · the · mainstream, · "Till · the · Sun ·Turns · Black", · he · now · joins · the · ranks · of · bands · you · have · heard ·
on · "Jimmy · Kimmel · Live". · Hofer · has · a · fresh · take · on · current · rock ·standards · and · the · bands · and · personalities · he · sings · about ·
are · genuine · and · relatable. · One · song · about · Jimmy · Kimmel · and ·the · whole · band · "Live."
What · is · the · difference · between · self-produced · albums, · and ·albums · that · are · recorded · by · a · "real" · recording · studio?
You · could · probably · say · that · the · record · companies/record ·producers · do · not · really · care · about · self · productions · but · if ·
you · want · to · release · on · the · big · labels · you · have · to · follow ·the · rules. · Basically · they · want · to · make · money. · So · if · you · are · a ·
indie/self · producing · musician · it · is · better · to · invest · time · and ·money · into · learning · the · basics · so · that · you · can · release · on · your ·
own · when · you · get · to · the · point · of · releasing · the · finished · album, ·as · you · probably · won't · receive · any · labels · money · if · you · sell ·
through · your · own · website · for · example. · Most · indie/alt ·recording · companies · are · looking · for · a · songwriter · with · a · lot ·
of · demo · songs · and · a · few · albums · so · they · can · put · out · that ·songwriter's · music · on · their · own · label · and · still · make · money · off ·
of · it. · So · as · a · songwriter, · the · most · important · thing · may · be · to ·get · at · least · one · or · two · albums · out · as · a · demo · and · have · at ·
least · a · few · of · those · songs · written. · They · should · be · decent · at ·least, · so · that · they · can · get · someone · to · release · it. · The · next ·
important · thing · is · to · know · how · to · get · a · good · demo ·together. · You · have · to · have · a · lot · of · songs, · but · they · should ·
not · all · fit · to · the · same · genre. · It · would · be · impossible · for · you ·to · get · a · label · to · release · your · album · in · a · certain · genre. · They ·
want · music · that · is · marketable · and · they · want · your · music · to ·sound · good · and · not · like · a · garage · band. · If · you · are · a ·
self-produced · recording · artist · the · next · thing · is · to · go · into · a ·small · recording · studio · to · record · the · album. · You · will · have ·
to · pay · for · it · anyway. · It · may · be · easier · to · choose · a · good ·studio · to · record · at, · but · they · can · be · very · expensive · too. ·
The · final · thing · is · to · mix · the · songs · and · upload · them · to · the ·"net" · to · be · distributed. · There · are · very · few · of · these · places ·
and · they · require · you · to · pay · money · to · put · your · songs · up · there.

Interviewed by

```
<Urs Hofer>38 (b. 1976, Switzerland) graduated from the
```
University of Zurich, and holds Masters in Media, Film,
and Computer Science. He lives and works in Zurich as a freelance
programmer and artist. His works mostly consist of collabora-
tions with graphic designers or artists, which focus on combining
computer technologies with other arts, design or literature.
Hofer has been awarded several grants and residencies, and his
installations and performances have been shown at the Ars
Electronica, the Bauhaus Dessau, the ZKM as well as at various
theaters in Germany and Switzerland.
Interview with Urs Hofer A.J 189
Rob van Leijsen Urs, thank you for welcoming
us to your studio. I want to begin the interview
by asking you to introduce yourself, explain how you
got into creative coding, and how this has evolved
in your daily practice. 5
441 Urs Hofer I was born in 1976 near Lucerne. How I got
into everything is kind of important. I didn’t study design,
or computer programming. I actually studied film science,
but that doesn’t matter too much either. My orientation
was always interdisciplinary. It also has something to do 10
with the period in which I grew up. There was the rise
of the Internet, a sense that everything was possible, a
time when concepts like remixing, combining, and “do-
it-yourself” proliferated.
442 I first learned to program on my older brother’s 15
[<Commodore> <G>18] C64 computer, which was in a way
interesting, because you always had this visual output.
For me, from the beginning, programming was associated
with the idea that, once you do something, you see
something, namely a visual representation of your code. 20
R.v.L. Was there a key project where you moved
into graphic design?
444 U.H. For me there was no key project per se. There
were some major projects that provided an opportunity
to do some things. I had to earn money during my studies 25
at university, and everybody wanted websites back in the
day, and I could make them. As a result, I met graphic
designers, who went on to become successful. I began
collaborating with them, since they were looking for
```
programmers: at the time, everything regarding Internet 30
```
technologies was brand new.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.J 190 Part A: Interview J
INTERVIEWS
A
445Come to think of it, there was this project that
inspired my special interest in generative graphic design
in terms of generated books. We started a project called
Encyclopaedizer, which had begun as a collaboration
between friends who worked in the field of literature.5
I worked on it from 2002 to 2005. They wanted to create
a new encyclopedia, and I proposed doing something
```
generative (i.e. with generated texts). It was during the
```
rise of <Google ><G>34 , and this was all totally new. The idea
was you could type in anything: we tried not to generate10
a link list, but rather short definitions, like what you
would find in an encyclopedia. The last step followed
logically, which was to put this database, that was just a
website, back into book form. Anyway, since we had done
everything by ourselves, it also felt natural to write the15
code to generate a <PDF><G>65 of the book at the end, and
make use of some technologies that were new then, like
<books on demand>39. You could say that, from that
point on, I developed a special interest in programming
books. It’s not just about design, but also about the20
organization and structuring of content, which I always
found to be just as interesting as all the rest.
R.v.L. Did you work with another designer on
this particular project?
447U.H. I worked with Raphael Koch, who is a trained25
graphic designer. We did the layout together. It’s funny
because it won an award as one of the most beautiful
Swiss books, despite the fact that, from a layout stand-
point, we made many mistakes. Still, we played off of
those errors. On a technical level, it was done with La-30
TeX, this famous typesetting language which goes back

A Collaborative Dictionary,
Rafael Koch, Urs Hofer, 2006.
to Donald Knuth. I didn’t study graphic design, but
I was at a university where it was a quite well-known as
a tool for typesetting scientific papers, so it wasn’t rocket
science. The new aspect was the combination: taking a
database, and attaching it to a script that communicates 5
with a tool known in scientific fields, generating output,
and then inputting it back into a graphic design context.
I was always thrilled to discover new combinations and
reuse or even abuse existing tools.
R.v.L. Did the collaboration with your colleague result 10
in your acquiring layout skills, and did he also learn

449 U.H. Yes, that always happens.

451 U.H. The script did not dictate the typography, but
on a certain level it did influence aspects of collection,
selection and arrangement. To be honest, when it comes
to design, I was always more interested in the scripting
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.J 192 Part A: Interview J
INTERVIEWS
A
component. For me, it was not about experimenting on
the letter itself, but more about collecting and arranging
things, and coming up with abstract rules. You create
a couple of basic layouts, and then you write a script
that combines these layouts over a couple of hundred5
pages. This kind of serendipitous effect, or the idea of
randomness in a book, is a unique thing, because, when
you generate it again, the new document looks totally
different from the first one. Actually, the idea of the fixed
book no longer exists.10
R.v.L. Does the script create some surprises?
453 ‡ B.B 76g
Parameter 20
gAlgorithm 17

B.B
U.H. Always, that is what is interesting for me and
my colleagues. I have another example. It was an almanac
about bibliomania. It is made up of a collection of text
fragments from notes on bibliomania. You start with a15
love for books and texts, and it ends in chaos and death if
you’re too obsessed with books. Here also, the algorithm
reflects the degree of chaos, because it starts in a quite
ordered form. The more chaotic the texts are, the more
the layout floats around. All of these parameters were20
stored in a text database as well, and the amount of
so-called “bibliomaniac chaos” was stored next to the
text fragment.
454We didn’t know what the book was going to
look like. We probably generated 500 versions of a final25
<PDF><G>65 , because we were not happy with the algorithm,
the way it developed, and how it laid out the fragments.

R.v.L. Did you modify the algorithm after each
iteration?
456B.C 101 †
gGenerated 13

gApproach 9
gGenerated 13
B.C
U.H. Sometimes. Because randomness is a factor, there
is no modification possible once the file is generated. At
that point, the final <PDF> <G>65cannot be edited. It was 5
simply created and immediately sent to the printer. So
we selected a “good” version, whatever “good” means.
There was a lot of tinkering around with ranges of font
```
sizes; it was a totally different approach to bookmaking,
```
not like just thinking about typesetting and doing correct 10
and accurate work. We only had one book—if it wasn’t
