generate one that satisfied us.
R.v.L. Was editing the book afterwards initially
a design choice? 15
458 U.H. For me, it was kind of an important decision,
because to do otherwise felt like a lie. If I write a script,
and if I say “this book is generated by a script,” it
feels wrong if you then edit the document by hand in
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.J 194 Part A: Interview J
INTERVIEWS
A
order to make it beautiful. From the beginning, a fun-
damental idea was that, if a kind of esthetic or beauty
exists that emerges from the code, then I’m content
with that. When my code creates a visual representa-
tion, like a book or like a layout, it should be interesting5
because of the coding decisions alone. If you tweak it a
little bit afterwards to make it better, it makes no sense
anymore to me.
459I have a third example, a text archive left by a
journalist, now deceased. He left a large archive of texts10
behind, which he could no longer manage to organize. He
wrote that his dream would be to have a machine that
would organize all his texts and create a structure. So
basically we set out to make his dream come true. The
resulting book is very simple, on a design level. It only15
has three different layouts: one, two, or three columns,
and three basic type styles. Some of the text is a little
bit larger because it was marked in the database by the
editor as being an essential part of the whole.
460The adoption of those three different layouts was20
very simple, the shorter a text was, the fewer columns
it had. This basic graphic design rule turned into a
biographical element of information, since he wrote longer
or shorter texts at different stages of his life. Towards
the end, he became ill and couldn’t write anymore, and25
the texts got increasingly shorter. We didn’t have this in
mind when we were creating the basic parameters, but
we ended up with this kind of representation of his life
through the use of just one, two, or three columns. In
my opinion, that’s when you see how code, or an idea,30
can work. That’s also one of the main reasons I like to
do projects like this, because you can be surprised by
the work you do.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Urs Hofer A.J 195
.J
URS HOFER
461 There are various ways in which you can read
the book. No one is going to read the book in a linear
way. You basically have ten fields of interest, and they
all begin on the cover itself, with these keywords. So
this arrangement of texts, which had nothing to do with 5
design, which really is more of a mathematical, statis-
tical, and editorial element, became a main component
of the book.
R.v.L. Is it an efficient way of working, in terms of
process and going back and forth with the editors and 10
publishers?
463B.C 102 †

U.H. It was a different form of collaboration, but it
was not highly efficient or super quick, because for that
one should reuse the algorithm. I did other projects
where there was more of a focus on the economic aspects. 15
However, this only works if you can reuse the code, and
do another ten books based on the same system. That
would probably go much more quickly. However, for this
one piece, we were working for the love of it, and as an
experiment, exploring this new approach of collaboration 20
between programming and design.
R.v.L. While working on books with graphic designers,
do they express any regrets about not being able to
modify or correct anything after generation?
465 U.H. I think this type of collaboration only works 25
with designers who are willing to give up their control.
As a programmer, I always try to respect graphic design
decisions as much as I can. I often do the graphic design
myself, and I know how important it is.

```
information · systems. · They · are · physical · objects · used · for · one · or ·more · of · the · following: · input; · processing; · output; · display. · Tools ·
```
consist · of · one · of · two · kinds, · user · interfaces · or · programming ·languages. · User · interfaces · are · things · like · pushbuttons · and · sliders ·
that · allow · operators · to · enter · data, · make · decisions,...

U.H. Sometimes it does. I mean, whatever I do, whether

the main part of your business? Is it a viable service

B.B
U.H. My opinion is that if somebody creates a tool
or a font, he or she should get money for it. I can totally
understand somebody saying: “I’m writing code and 10
people should pay for it.” I have always been against
stealing property, because it’s hard to make money with
an open-source project, and with design tools it is prob-
ably impossible. I mean, you give away the tool for free,
so how can you make money with it? Probably only by 15
selling services based around the tool. All these excel-
lent open-source tools die because the creators behind
the tools don’t have the energy to maintain the project
without any financial resources.
R.v.L. Do you see any barriers that remain in terms of 20
graphic designers becoming acquainted with coding?
483B.B 93 †

U.H. If graphic designers want to learn programming,
they should start doing it. They shouldn’t be shy and
worried that the result will not work. There are so many
tools around nowadays that bridge the gap between 25
abstract coding and graphic design. I think it has gotten
easier for graphic design students to learn to code, but
it all starts with curiosity.

A.J 200 Part A: Interview J
INTERVIEWS
A
R.v.L. Do you think that the new generation of
students is picking up programming more easily?
485U.H. Younger generations know a lot more about
various disciplines and about tools and coding, but they
also have a different approach to coding. When I started5
to code, you really had to write the code, you had to
write everything. Now you can just pull chunks of it
together. Anyone can make their own website just by
using the proper tools, you don’t really have to code.
The boundaries between coding and designing are much10
more blurred now. I’m not saying that it’s bad, it’s
just another approach. The way we work with designers
has changed dramatically. When I started, there was a
clear separation between coders and designers. I would
work with a designer who made the design and I was15
writing the code, and the outcome would be a website,
for example.
R.v.L. You don’t see it as that separate anymore?
487 ‡ B.A 51g
Websites · Look 24
gTools · Available 23
gLot · of · Websites 24
B.A
U.H. No. Also if I work on a website with a designer,
the first question that comes up is “what’s possible nowa-20
days?” Back in the 2000s, it was the designer who dic-
tated what was possible. Now, to a large extent, the
variety of devices and technological tools available dic-
tates what is possible. Also, a lot of websites look simi-
lar because they need to run on smaller displays. This25
has a major impact on design, on all levels really, from
the domination of the device, down to the coder and
the designer.

R.v.L. Have graphic designers lost their prime position
in the design process?
489 U.H. Yes, perhaps. I also think a lot of designs end
up being somewhat similar when views on design are
defined at a corporate level. If <Google > <G>34, for example, 5
releases a new style guide, like <Material Design> <G>52, this
has a huge impact on the clients’ perception of design,
and designers have to deal in those terms. Consequently,
one sees that the Internet, with its tendency towards
big corporate monopolies, does not inherently promote 10
a diverse approach to design.
R.v.L. Thank you very much, Urs.
491 U.H. You’re welcome.

<GG>42 WHO·IS ·< DIMITRI·JEANOTTAT >?
Before · I · get · on · with · the · review · I ·want · to · give · Dimitri · Jeanotte · a · few · words. · His · name · will ·
doubtless · not · be · unknown · to · anyone · who · has · been ·following · the · Eurostar · and · other · Belgian · racing, · although · he ·
was · not · an · immediate · success, · and · is · now · a · little · known · figure. ·Jeanotte · was · the · son · of · a · schoolmaster, · who · had · moved ·
to · Antwerp · on · the · death · of · his · former · master. · His · father · was ·the · son · of · the · former · Belgian · postmaster, · and · had · moved · to ·
Antwerp · from · Leuven. · His · mother · was · the · daughter · of · a · Belgian ·industrialist · called · Jacques-Jean · Vogelein. · The · Vogeleins · were · an ·
Antwerp-based · bourgeois · family · who · moved · the · business · to ·Charleroi · when · that · area · of · Belgium · was · annexed · after · the · First ·
World · War, · and · they · were · to · become · closely · allied · to · the ·French · Fédération · Française · des · Automobile · Clubes · de ·
l'Hexagone. · That · family · name · is · the · source · of · the ·Vogelein-Marqueurs-Jeanotes · race · team · in · which · Jeanotte · and ·
one · of · the · main · characters · of · this · story · competed, · and · I ·intend · a · post · on · this · side · of · Jeanottes' · career · some · day. ·
Anyway, · Jeanotte's · father · was · a · schoolmaster, · so · it · can · be ·assumed · that · he · was · intelligent, · and · that · probably · played · a ·
big · part · in · making · him · a · professional · bike · racer. · But · as · I've ·said, · he · was · not · famous · for · his · speed · at · the · end · of · his ·
career.

<Dimitri Jeannottat>42 is a graphic designer
who lives and works between Switzerland and
The Netherlands. Since 2013, he has been collabo-
rating closely with the Amsterdam-based Studio
```
Joost Grootens (SJG), a design office specializing
```
in editorial projects, data visualization, and
cartography. In 2019, Dimitri Jeannottat set up a
second SJG office in Biel/Bienne, Switzerland.
Since 2014, he has been teaching graphic design at
```
the École d’Arts Appliqués (EAA), La Chaux-de-
```
Fonds. Together with Joost Grootens, they
have held workshops for institutions such as the
```
École Cantonale d’Art de Lausanne (ECAL),
```
```
Zürcher Hochschule der Künste (ZHdK),
```
```
and Design Academy Eindhoven (DAE). His book
```
designs have earned awards in competitions
such as Best Dutch Book Designs, Die Schönsten
Deutschen Bücher, Best Book Design From
All Over the World and The Most Beautiful
Swiss Books.
Interview with Dimitri Jeannottat A.K 203
Demian Conrad Good morning, Dimitri. Thank
you so much for participating in our research project
Post-Digital Graphic Design. Let’s start by asking
you to explain the project you did for the Lausanne
```
Underground Film and Music Festival (LUFF). 5
```
Tell us the story behind this project.
494 Dimitri Jeannottat Well, Tiphaine Allemann and
I were hired to do that assignment for three years run-
ning. Tiphaine is an illustrator and artist and I am a
graphic designer: the commissioners decided to work 10
with very different profiles to see what would come of
such a collaboration. The first year, we began by precisely
following their guidelines. This resulted in a project that
we liked, but the end result was not very adventurous,
rather classic even. For the second year, we thought we 15
should bring something more to the table and we decided
to play with the concept that LUFF is all about discov-
ering new things… they don’t invite big names, the idea
is to provide a means of exploring lesser known talent.
Fig.26
Poster design, Lausanne
Underground Film and Music
```
Festival (LUFF),
```
Dimitri Jeannottat, 2018.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.K 204 Part A: Interview K
INTERVIEWS
A
495We had a mutual friend, Pierre Charmillot, who
had trained as a graphic designer at ERACOM years ago.
Afterwards, he went in another direction and became
a software engineer, programming, developing software
and coding. We thought this might be an interesting way5
to lend an extra dimension to the project, and focus on
the process of the design rather than the end result, even
relinquishing a bit of control in terms of the end result.
So that’s what we did.
Fig.27
Poster design, Lausanne
Underground Film and Music
```
Festival (LUFF),
```
Dimitri Jeannottat, 2019.
496Ultimately, we decided to use digital printing10
instead of a nice silkscreened poster. The end result was
a bit less beautiful, but it opened the door to multiple
visuals without increasing the budget. We felt that, in
the case of this project, that was more important than
having a high-end, perfect silkscreen poster on the streets.15
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Dimitri Jeannottat A.K 205
.K
DIMITRI JEANNOTTAT
So, for the first time in 2018, we used that process: we
ended up with ten different posters on the street, as
well as lots of other printed material, along with digital
output. [In 2019], for our third festival, we decided to
push that idea a bit further. We used the same software 5
as a starting point, but then tweaked it slightly, in terms
of intent and method of use. We also decided that all
the posters should be unique—I think we had something
like 200 posters printed, all different and numbered. We
thought this was a nice way of using the technology be- 10
hind it, as well as a way of reflecting the individualistic
aspect of the festival, which is a very different experi-
ence for everyone. We are not all interested in the same
things and each of us has our own perceptions. It’s all
quite adventurous, even haphazard if you think about it. 15
You can end up seeing things you never expected. For
us, it was a good mix between how we developed the
project and what the results actually conveyed about the
festival itself.
D.C. Dimitri, could you dive a little bit more into 20
the visuals, and also how the mechanics of this coding,
i. e. the creative coding, was designed and how that
process developed over the course of the project?
498 D.J. Our starting point was to have a very simple
tool that could take pictures in a folder and just place 25
them in a defined format, then cut and combine them
in random ways. That really was our point of departure.
Little by little, we added layers to the functionalities
and increased what we could do with them. We did a
lot of testing. I have several folders filled with hundreds 30
and hundreds of exports over the two years we did it.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.K 206 Part A: Interview K
INTERVIEWS
A
I don’t even know how many thousands of images we have,
because they are generated in a second. Consequently,
the biggest part of our job was to focus on playing with
it and to see what works and what doesn’t. To return
to the basic concept, I collaborated with Tiphaine, who5
worked exclusively in an analog mode. Our intent was
to create a triad between Tiphaine, myself, and a sort
of external digital treatment, in essence to confront her
analog, artisanal, handmade approach with a digital tool
that would use these images in a an extremely clinical10
way. We were looking to highlight this contrast. My
job was more about setting up the parameters for the
software with Pierre, choosing what worked and what
didn’t, and so on. I think that, when we started out,
each of us was really entrenched in our own areas, but15
gradually, we were all on an equal footing, in the sense
that we each had our own part to contribute to every
aspect of it. So, yes, it sort of evolved over time. As
I said before, we slowly added functionalities. In the
beginning, we just picked out random images in different20
folders, cutting and pasting them. Then, little by little,
we were able to add details, such as the way in which
we organized the folders. We had one folder that only
had typography, one that only had textures, one with a
variety of images provided by Tiphaine. Then we began25
experimenting with them, mixing them for example with
digital image patterns, or simple shapes, like rectangles
and circles.

In 2018, the first year, we really focused fully on
the drawings, illustrations and collages made by Tiphaine.30
Her work really informed the end result. The year after
that, we used her input in a very different way. We took
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

DIMITRI JEANNOTTAT

a specific type of illustration that she did, that consisted
of sorts of black shapes with almost no gradients or tex-
ture, something really simple. Actually you don’t sense
the software as much. The idea was for her to create
a few basic illustrations and then we would use it to 5
generate tons of other ones. You no longer know if you’re
looking at the original one or one generated by the soft-
ware. The previous year, the concept was different—we
really wanted one to be aware of the difference, so we
used a more impersonal treatment that distinguished 10
the software in juxtaposition with her illustrations. In
both cases, we used the same tools, just in very different
ways. Apart from cutting and pasting, we created a few
effects that we sometimes used. It’s a very simple inter-
face you can just turn on or off, to select an effect or 15
treatment, or whether to use a folder or not. Depending
on our input, we could make the grid behave differently.
Whether you used a mathematical grid—i. e. dividing
the width by five, or a random one, or using higher or
lower numbers—a variation of any of these parameters 20
can yield extremely different results.

500Actually, what I liked most about the project

the festival.
501At this point in the process, it’s not random

poster to poster, you no longer get the message across.
Consequently, we had to make decisive choices, and leave

the process was far more important than the end result.20
So there are the selections we made that you could see
on the streets, but we also had the option of going in
quite a different direction: seeing it as a classic graphic
design commission where it is necessary to fall in with
the client’s wishes and be consistent.25

in terms of graphic design, since most of my work was

B.C
It brings to mind another project that was ex-
tremely different. Several years ago, I worked with Joost
Grootens on the main dictionary of the Dutch language.
Subsequently, we also did pocket editions in different lan-
guages. Since it was the major dictionary of the Dutch20
language, it had been under way for years before I was
even involved in the project, which was ongoing when
I joined the studio, however I did the pocket versions
on my own. We spent weeks on basically two pages of
text that included all the possible cases, and that was25
it. Then they would generate it automatically based on
the cases you had envisioned beforehand—it literally
comes to something like four pages. However, these four
pages created a template that enabled them to generate
nine volumes of pocket edition dictionaries, so it was30
also a very fascinating approach. I’m generally used to
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Dimitri Jeannottat A.K 211

DIMITRI JEANNOTTAT
going page by page and diving into details for each text-

person who continues the project and cross your fingers
and hope you will be happy with the end result. This
sort of coding, with this type of complex content, really 5
works extremely well, and, while it had been a new way
of working for me, it’s actually been around for quite
a while.

Van Dale Dictionary,

D.C. We would like to know how the process of using
algorithms and scripts affects your own work, your own 10
way of shaping the design?
509 D.J. I think in the case of the dictionaries, for exam-
ple, you cannot deal with exceptions if the content is
generated. You have to set the rules beforehand, unlike
what you can potentially do in a book you design page 15
by page. In the latter case, you can say, “Ok, this is
the principle, but in a few places I will deviate from
that and do things slightly differently.” I think the fact
that it’s generated forces you to be extremely clear and
maybe a bit more simple in your choices. You need to 20
find solutions that fit anything that could happen. That’s
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

definitely not the case if I do a book page by page, where

510For example, I am teaching one day a week at

show them a few tools for working with data, whatever it10
```
is. We use online tools we know; for example, we often use
```
a website called Rawgraphs because it’s quite accessible,

visualization of their data that fits the project.

how they work, and to test them within the parameters

D.C. How do you think in the future this way of
working is going to impact our work? How do you think

522B.B 72 †

B.B
D.J. I’m not really worried about what it will become. 5
I think it would most likely make our life increasingly
easier. I mean, in terms of tools, I remember my soft-
ware when I was a student, compared to now, it’s just
improved. I imagine that some aspects of design will be
replaced in part by automated processes. On the other 10
hand, I think as long as there is no standardization,
you will always need someone backing it up, making the
decisions and sifting out what’s specific to this or that
project or context. I can imagine that bigger companies,
or companies that are really into template-based produc- 15
tion, will need less people at some point since it’s faster
and because certain things can be done differently once
they are automated.
523 Still, I think as soon as you do something a
bit more ambitious, like a book or a bit of advertising 20
for something like LUFF or whatever it is, then you
need an idea behind it. You really cannot replace the
decision-making process. I am not scared or even cynical
about it. I think it’s actually quite exciting to see what
you can do. 25
524B.124 †
gStudent 27
gStart 27

In a way, I’m a bit jealous of my twenty-year-

what will be possible afterwards, it’s more exciting and
fascinating than scary. I think the challenge is just to 30
understand it, digest it, and appreciate what you want
and can do with it.

D.C. Do you see a creative coding community in the
European Union? Is it just a few disparate individuals,

D.J. Actually, when it comes to graphic design, it
is closely related to Instagram. It became the common5
language to have things moving instead of static. I don’t
see it as a takeover, where it will be the standard and
everything has to be like that. I’m not convinced, for
example, by the moving poster trend, where you need an
app to check the poster. I think it’s a nice and sometimes10
entertaining addition, but that doesn’t make a poster.
I remember, when it came out a few years ago, that a lot
of people were seeing it as the new Thing, and if a poster
didn’t move, then it wasn’t complete or something, but
I really don’t agree with that. I don’t think people will15
ever check posters on the street with their phones. Per-
haps it does work for big screens in a station. It’s fasci-
nating because it’s new. Then you digest it and then it
becomes just one more option among all the others, but
it’s not really something that will become the standard.20
R.v.L. Do you see a more general creative coding

A.L 220 Part A: Interview L
INTERVIEWS
A.L
JOHN-
SON /
KINGST-
ON
IVA N WEISS +
MICHAEL
KRYENBÜHL
www.johnsonkingston.ch
In 2012, after a six-month residency in New York,
granted by the Swiss Federal Office of Culture,
Ivan Weiss and Michael Kryenbühl founded the
<Johnson/Kingston>43 studio. Their core focus
is on self-initiated and commissioned projects at
the intersection of design and technology. Their
work is rooted in the exploration of experimental,
contemporary forms of graphic design, type design
and programming. The duo have been artists-in-
residence in New York, Paris and Berlin.
They also teach and conduct workshops and speak
in Switzerland and internationally. Their work has
been shown in Russia, America, Japan and Korea,
and they are recipients of the Swiss Design Award
```
(2017 and 2010). In 2017 they have accepted a
```
six year professorship in communication design
with a focus on new media at the Hochschule
für Gestaltung Karlsruhe in Germany. In 2019,
Massimiliano Audretsch joined the studio.
Johnson
/
Kingston
Ivan
Weiss
Michael
Kryenbühl
Interviewed by
Demian Conrad
Interview with Ivan Weiss + Michael Kryenbühl A.L 221
Demian Conrad Hello Michael and Ivan. Thank
you for meeting with me today. Could you please start
by introducing yourselves and your practice?
533 Michael Kryenbühl We founded our studio in early
2012, after returning from a six-month residency in New 5
York granted by the Swiss Federal Office of Culture. Win-
ning a Swiss Design Award with our Bachelors thesis
project was a great motivational boost and made it pos-
sible for us to experiment freely within the scope of the
residency. After getting to know each other through a 10
couple of joint projects during our studies at Hochschule
Luzern – Design & Kunst [Lucerne School of Art and
Design], as well as collaborating on our thesis, we de-
cided to explore our shared interests through a shared
practice. From the very beginning, the way technology 15
and design could be combined with tools in order to
create different workflows, outputs and visual languages
was an important element in our approach.
534534 Ivan Weiss In addition to our own practice, we both
teach at the HfG Karlsruhe [Karlsruhe University of 20
Arts and Design]. We are professors in the Communi-
cation Design department, who teach courses on digi-
tal and networked media. Every now and then we also
try to make some time for self-initiated projects that
center around topics in which we have developed a 25
growing interest.
D.C. Could you tell me more about your work
with the PosterTown book, which was completely done
with scripting?
536 M.K. We got together with a couple of people in the 30
graphic design community in Lucerne, and discussed our
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.L 222 Part A: Interview L
INTERVIEWS
A
desire to produce documentation for the Poster Town
exhibition, which had been traveling around the world
at that time. We joined a group that had formed around
Megi Zumstein and Erich Brechbühl, which also included
Klaus Fromherz and Martin Geel from Peng Peng studio,5
as well as Raphael Schoen and Simon Rüegg from Präsens
Büro. We started to conceive a format that would draw
attention to a very active scene, instead of merely pro-
ducing another “best-of” poster collection. We wanted
to focus on the diversity of the community in Lucerne,10
and the links between clients and several designers who
had worked on similar commissions over a period of time.
These are interesting connections to highlight, especially
in a small city like Lucerne. This was the starting point
for our collecting posters through an open call for par-15
ticipation, with the goal of collecting as many posters
linked to Lucerne as possible.
537We held an editors meeting in which we culled
the selection down to half of the 2,000 entries we had
received. From that point on, it was clear that we would20
still have to deal with a huge amount of posters in the
book. So we had to come up with a way in which we
could easily organize all these posters and be able to
get a rapid overview. That’s when we came up with the
idea of a website as a database which we could use to25
manage all the files. We then started to think about how
we could outsource some of the design decisions to an
algorithm, and how we might approach the design of
a book about poster design in a rather objective way.
We asked ourselves how we could deal with the works30
properly, in a way that would encourage new connections
and comparisons.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Ivan Weiss + Michael Kryenbühl A.L 223
.L
JOHNSON / KINGSTON
538B.C 103 †
gWay · of · Working 12
B.C
538B.C 103 †
gWay · of · Working 12
B.C
I.W. In order to enable as many connections as pos-
sible between the posters, we worked with a tag system.
For example, a poster might have the tags “italic typog-
raphy”, “animal imagery” and “black and white”. The
tags enabled us to connect posters from different design- 5
ers and display them side by side. They are the central
element of the concept, as they attempt to represent
the different approaches as well as the connections be-
tween the designers. It was quite fascinating to see what
happened with the rules we set up for the layout, and 10
how the algorithm would generate the pages of the book.
It’s a different way of working: you don’t lay out every
page, but you write the rules that will do that for you in
<JavaScript> <G>41. I think it shows up in the characteristics
of the design throughout the book. 15
539 We used the website as our tool. This enabled
us to tag posters and group them within collections in
the database. The website gave us a good overview. We
exported all the pages of the book directly from it. In
the beginning, we hadn’t intended to work like this, but 20
it turned out to be quite a smooth process.
540540 M.K. All of the designers were granted access to
the back end of the website. This reduced a lot of our
workload. There was no actual proofreading for the poster
details, every designer was responsible for the accuracy 25
of the information in their own work.
541 Also, we didn’t perceive the book in itself as an
end result. For instance, right now we are holding another
round of open calls for new works. The newly arrived
works will be evaluated by the team and then added to 30
the website. We see the project as an ongoing enterprise,
one that hopefully will motivate local designers and
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.L 224 Part A: Interview L
INTERVIEWS
A
recent graduates to contribute to the poster heritage
of Lucerne.
D.C. If I understand you correctly, you still used
InDesign to generate the PDFs?
543I.W. Yes. The database is a flat CMS system in which5
all information entered by the <participants>44 is stored
in small text files. They were exported through the web-
site into a text file that was then imported and laid out
in <InDesign><G>5, 15 with <basil.js>. Then we exported the
<PDF><G>65, 5 files for the printer from <InDesign>.

A student at HfG Karlsruhe created an open-
source software for vector editing in a browser. Although

strator><G>2 or similar software, it raises questions regarding

more about raising awareness about how it influences

Within the <Adobe><G>2 palette for example, the
way you use and work with the limitations of each format
changes the outcome completely. When working on a
poster design in <Photoshop><G>6 , you immediately have to
deal with lots of layers and start stacking elements on30
top of each other, because there is no space to set things
aside. They are just gone and lost. On the other hand,
in <Illustrator><G>4 , there is this idea of endlessness, where
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

you end up having lots of visual and graphic ingredients
around your actual work because you can easily place

which is clearly about multi-page documents, you keep
duplicating your pages while working on a draft until 5
you end up with 200 pages, all containing different ver-
sions with slight modifications. All three softwares have

553 M.K. I was introduced to programming at the be-

555 If you want to work on web-based projects in an

In our courses in Karlsruhe, you could say we 25

M.K. It’s also about raising awareness about the
possibilities, and it’s definitely a long-term commitment.
In our experience, if you define a set up for the students5
that provides a basic introduction to coding and allows
several paths or outputs, digital or physical ones, then,
at a certain point, students actually begin considering
using code for their projects and even have fun moving
in that direction. It’s really nice to see students getting10
excited about code. Still, it is difficult to know exactly
which teaching method is best to get them there. We just
think that it’s really important to sit down with students
at some point, work with them on their projects, and
show them different approaches.15

560I.W. Yes, that is true. If you are lucky, there will be

JOHNSON / KINGSTON
562 M.K. We could talk about the work we did for the
B-Sides Festival, a yearly music event in Lucerne. We
were commissioned to do all the visual communication
for the 2016 edition, which consisted of a lot of printed
matter, motion design and merchandise elements. We 5
began our process by designing the website, and built
all the other communication material around the online
platform. Up to that point, the website had not played

Obviously there was a website in place, but it was more 10
of a placeholder and template for a static identity.
Fig.32
Website, B-Sides Festival,
Johnson / Kingston, 2016.
563563 I.W. On a conceptual level, the identity was based
on a playful use of somewhat strange typefaces. We
stacked the same texts, set in several typefaces, on top
of each other, using different colors. This is very easy to 15
do in the browser: three times you have the same text
with different <CSS> <G>17, and they are stacked on the same
position. It’s a simple duplication routine.
564B.A 32 †
gVisual · Output 23
B.A
This treatment of typography changed the whole
identity and became a central part of this project. In the 20
final analysis, these multicolored typefaces and strange
typographical shapes, readable or not, really influenced
the tool we used for all visual output. Doing this in
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.L 230 Part A: Interview L
INTERVIEWS
A
<InDesign><G>5 might have been possible, but not as quickly
and easily as in the browser.
565On top of that, we came across the <JavaScript><G>41
library three.js which made it possible to render 3D sce-
narios in the browser. This was totally new and very5
exciting for us. We used this technique to create individ-
ual visual worlds for each artist through which you could
navigate and discover what was going on. Visitors could
then generate screenshots from this interactive experi-
ence on the website, which were subsequently printed on10
items of merchandise, such as T-shirts. In this manner,
we were able to link the digital output to the printed
identity, and it revolutionized the conception of the
printed items.
566566M.K. These tools and techniques definitely influenced15
the output of the printed matter. The screenshots for the
T-shirts were uploaded by the community. Each visitor
could upload screenshots from the 3D scenario linked to
their favorite artist.
567We really wanted to use community-generated20
material throughout the visual identity. For instance,
in the booklet, where the program is announced and
you find short introductory texts about the artists, we
included YouTube comments about them in order to
provide new perspectives and create cross-references as25
well as contextualize the artist and their music. We think
this way of working wouldn’t have been possible if we
had started out with the printed matter.
D.C. How do you manage your toolmaking with your
clients? When you create a tool do you explain it to30
them? Do you charge separately for that work as
opposed to the design work?

B.A
M.K. One important thing in that regard is that often
the clients are not really aware of what they actually
need. They often approach us with a request for, let’s
say, a poster. Then, while we are working on that, it
becomes clear that additional social media elements are 5
required, or even a complete visual identity. To give you
an example, Stube im Progr, an open space in Bern,
asked us to design their visual identity. They paid us
for the identity, but we produced a tool for them with
which they could produce visual outputs on their own, 10
posters and flyers mostly. We simply used a part of the
budget for this tool, to enable them to independently
produce printed matter. Consequently, in the end, they
paid us for something different, but we managed to work
on the project in our own way without feeling like we 15
were not being paid for the coding part. We simply used
a different working method in terms of our energy and
budget. This approach has worked out well in several
cases, with a variety of clients.
570570 I.W. In general, it is really difficult to bill clients for 20
this type of programming work. In addition, you can’t
always copy and paste snippets of code, you really have
to write your own programs and libraries, etc. As in
many other studios, our teaching activities cover a part
of our income. That removes some financial pressure from 25
the studio.

M.K. We are very grateful for the projects where we
can really explore new techniques and working methods,
such as for the B-Sides Festival website, for example.
In that project, we knew beforehand that the available 30
budget was low. However, all the people involved in the
organization are equally paid and equally committed,
so we chose not to count our hours. The budget often
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.L 232 Part A: Interview L
INTERVIEWS
A
doesn’t necessarily relate to the amount of work you
put in the project in the end, but is instead more of
an investment in the exploration of new things and the
ability to test them in their applied context. In this
case, the payback didn’t translate into money, but rather5
visibility and the chance to learn new tools. You could say
there was a monetary payback at a certain point, since
we received a Swiss Design Award for that project. That
was great, but it is, of course, an exception to the rule.
However, from that moment on, in terms of commissions10
for projects for new clients, we began receiving the proper
amount of money required for building a website. This
then influenced the way in which we work: we didn’t see
tools as an additional commodity for which we would
charge a specific amount of money, but rather as an15
integral part of the design process. Conceptual sketching
and coding goes hand in hand. A custom-coded website
becomes a tool with which one can develop the concept
```
and visual language of the identity, for instance; the
```
entire creative process is intertwined.

One good comparison is the way in which we
deal with type design. If we would estimate the value of a
custom-designed typeface that the client didn’t explicitly
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Ivan Weiss + Michael Kryenbühl A.L 233
.L
JOHNSON / KINGSTON
request, what would we charge? 50 per cut? Or 20,000?
We put a lot more energy into the design than we could
possibly recover in charges because it is custom-made
but was not commissioned as such. Still, by viewing it
in perspective as a tool that helps us define and shape 5
a visual language, it becomes less important to charge
specifically for that work. Again, the typeface becomes
part and parcel of the process, much like coding, all
of which helps us find the right visual language for the
commissioned project in question. 10
573 Moreover, we don’t have the aim of producing a
subsequent retail typeface, which also makes it way easier
and faster. These tools are supposed to work within a
specific context.
D.C. That’s a very interesting take on the status 15
of tools within your creative process. How would you
actually describe creative coding?
575 I.W. Creative coding is applicable within a graphic
design context if you use programming, not as a trained
engineer would, but rather as a tool to solve a problem, 20
but without actually having an outcome or particular
rules in mind beforehand. I would define it as a sketchy
way of working with programming languages.

D.C. Do you see the development of a kind
of creative coding movement in Switzerland or even
internationally? Or would you say it still remains
a niche practice?
578M.K. In my opinion the movement is growing. It is5
comparable to type design: a couple of years ago there
were just a few small type foundries, and now they
have popped up everywhere because the software has
made it very accessible and knowledge of type design is
spread more widely. I think the same happens in coding.10
There are a lot more practices that rely on code than
there were even a few years ago. Increasing numbers
of graphic designers have developed an understanding
of coding tools. There is a trend, I don’t know if you
could call it a movement, but it is similar to what hap-15
pened in the field of type design, and now everybody is
designing typefaces.
579 ‡ B.C 109g
Graphic · Design 18
gWorking · with · Code
B.C
In the near future, I think every graphic designer
will work with code in some way, even if only for minor
automation tasks within a print project.20
580580I.W. Also, coding knowledge spread much more eas-
ily over the last few years through platforms like Stack
Overflow, for example. If you have code-related ques-
tions, you get plenty of answers there. The fascination
for new technologies today tends to push people to ex-25
plore creative coding as well, and the community just
keeps growing.

<GG>46 WHO·IS ·< JüRG·LEHNI >?
We · took · a · ride · in · his · electric · car, · and · he · explained · his · vision.It · took · less · than · a · minute · for · him · to · drive · by · me. · If · you · live ·
in · the · German-speaking · world, · you · might · have · seen · him. · Every ·Monday · evening, · during · the · winter · months, · around · 7:30 · p.m., ·
when · the · air · is · starting · to · grow · cool, · Jürg · Lehni · goes · for · a ·ride. · He · cruises · through · the · snowy · Berlin · streets · on · his · electric ·
car. · Jürg · wants · to · live · a · sustainable · life. · So, · for · the · past · ten ·years, · he's · been · using · an · electric · car · – · not · an · e-scooter · or · a ·
bicycle. · Instead · of · running · on · a · petrol · station, · it · runs · on ·electricity. · Because · he · drives · so · far, · he's · a · member · of · an · electric ·
club · – · Club · Elektra. · The · club · has · the · motto · "One · small · step ·for · mankind" · .
On · this · late · November · evening, · the · road · in · front · of · the ·embassy · is · almost · empty. · Two · people · in · a · car · are · driving ·
slowly. · We · stop · in · front · of · one · of · the · embassies · of · the ·German, · Austrian, · Swiss · and · Canadian · embassies. · The · German ·
embassy · is · on · the · right · side, · and · the · main · entrance · to · the ·building · is · on · the · left · side. · I · don't · see · any · guards, · and · the ·
gate · is · open. · On · the · other · side · of · the · pavement, · there's · a · gate ·with · a · guard, · presumably · for · the · embassy · of · another · country. ·
We · pull · closer · to · the · gate, · and · a · traffic · light · starts · showing ·green.Jürg · gets · out · of · the · car, · picks · up · his · bag, · and · goes · up ·
the · stairs, · which · the · guards · didn't · stop · us · from · using.The · car · is · not · an · ordinary · car, · it's · a · normal · car · with · a ·
modified · battery, · and · the · car · uses · solar · panels · to · charge · the ·battery · over · the · course · of · 24 · hours. · If · you · need · to, · you · can ·
also · plug · it · in · to · either · the · car's · charging · box · or · an · electricity ·supply. · You · might · notice · that · I · keep · using · the · term · "electric · car" · ·
and · "electric · battery" · , · but · Jürg · explained · that · it's · not · a ·battery, · but · a · "battery · pack" · .
I · ask · him · why · he's · not · using · the · car · for · a · longer · part · of · the ·week, · but · he · explains · that · if · you · drive · five · minutes · in · rush · hour ·
traffic, · an · electric · car · would · not · provide · a · good · solution. ·So, · instead, · he · uses · it · on · the · evenings, · but · just · for · short ·
distances. · He · says · that · he's · never · had · a · car · crash, · and · there ·have · never · been · any · maintenance · problems. · He's · not · the · only ·
owner · of · an · electric · car · at · the · club · – · there · are · probably ·between · 20 · to · 50 · club · members · who · use · electric · cars.
A.M 238 Part A: Interview M
INTERVIEWS
A.M
JÜRG
LEHNI
www.juerglehni.com
<Jürg Lehni>46 works collaboratively across
disciplines, exploring the nuances of technology,
tools and the human condition. His works often
take the form of platforms and scenarios for
production, such as computer-controlled drawing
and mark-making machines, as well as soft-
ware-based structures and open-source frame-
works, notably Paper.js and Scriptographer.
Lehni has shown his work internationally in both
group and solo shows at the MoMA New York,
Walker Art Center, Centre Pompidou, Institute
of Contemporary Arts, London, the Victoria
and Albert Museum, Design Museum London,
Kunsthalle St. Gallen, etc. His works have been
acquired by the public and private collections such
as the SFMOMA, HEK Basel and the Christoph
Merian Foundation. He has worked independently
```
since 2002, ran a studio in London (2008–2011)
```
and is currently based in Switzerland. Other
```
positions include foreign residencies (a Swiss
```
Design Awards residency in New York, a research
```
residency at Sony SET Studio, Tokyo), as well as
```
professorships abroad: Parsons New York,
```
UCLA (Department of Design Media Arts).
```
Jürg
Lehni
Interviewed by
Rob van Leijsen
Interview with Jürg Lehni A.M 239
Rob van Leijsen Jürg, thanks for accepting my
invitation to become part of this project. Please
introduce yourself and tell us how you got involved
with creative coding and programming?
587 Jürg Lehni My name is Jürg Lehni. I’m a designer, 5
artist, programmer and educator. I find it difficult to
summarize what I do in one sentence, since my work
involves multiple disciplines, but I think programming is
the core activity that connects virtually everything I do.
The intersection of design, art technology, communication 10
and language is the recurring theme. For lack of a simple
description, I often use the agricultural metaphor of crop
rotation—a strategy that keeps fields fertile—to explain
why I move between the various disciplines in which
I engage, often in collaboration with others. My brother 15
```
once called me a researching craftsman; I thought it a
```
very apt description.
588 My education followed a rather unusual trajec-
```
tory: I started out in the late 1990s at the ETH [Swiss
```
Federal Institute of Technology] in Zurich, planning to 20
learn electronic engineering as a tool for creative pur-
poses in order to use it as an artist or designer. I quickly
realized that the way they were teaching it wasn’t really
for me. You were basically just stuffing your brain for two
years with all this knowledge, and only eventually down 25
the road beginning to use it. I always need to understand
the things I learn visually, almost tangibly, in order to
be able to grasp abstract concepts and put them to use.
I would say that I have a certain affinity for mathematics,
geometry and abstract thinking through my visual un- 30
derstanding of things. Consequently, I wasn’t very happy
```
at the ETH; there weren’t enough niches in their rigid
```
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.M 240 Part A: Interview M
INTERVIEWS
A
program for people like me to flourish, which, looking
back, was a pity. As a result, after a year, I decided
to end my studies there and continue at HyperWerk in
Basel instead. It was a very new course that had just
come into existence at the time [late 1990s, early 2000s].5
I was in the second round of students of this new program,
which was an experimental post-industrial course dealing
with technology, design and the arts. As a student, you
had to reflect on how to fill in the gaps in between. At
the time, no one really seemed to have a clear idea what10
```
all of this was about; we were sort of figuring it all out
```
as we went along. Today, interactive design and media
arts are relatively defined fields in the realm of higher
education, with their own curriculum and textbooks and
required skills. Back then, this area of study was a bit15
of a catch-all for anything that wasn’t considered “pure”
art or design, and anything that had to do with technol-
ogy would end up there. All of this was a bit confusing:
I had already acquired quite a few technological skills
at ETH, and I felt I wasn’t learning enough in terms of20
artistic skills. So I decided to change direction once again
and applied to ECAL in Lausanne. That was perhaps
the most fruitful place for me, because there were clear
structures in place around the various areas of design
education. They enabled me to take my knowledge of25
technology and explore ways in which it could relate to
these more traditional topics, and how I could interact
with them.

B.D
Regarding your question about programming,
I learned it quite early—I started playing around with30
computers when I was around seven or eight. My older
brother Urs and I had received computers from our
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Jürg Lehni A.M 241
.M
JÜRG LEHNI
cousin, who was older and had trained as an electri-
cian. He handed down his old computers to us, and we
started tinkering with them. It all started with a <Com-
modore VC-20> <G>19that only had a cassette tape, not even
a floppy disk. All the programs came on audio cassettes, 5
and loading them took as long as playing back side A,
and sometimes side B as well, or you could manually type
in the listings of programs that were printed in special-
ized magazines. Later on, my brother received his own
<Commodore 64> <G>18with a floppy drive. So very early on, 10
I became acquainted with programming without really
understanding it. If we wanted to play a game featured
in the magazines, we had to enter all the required code
flawlessly—this would take hours. So we began trying to
figure out these instructions for the machine, although 15
we were not really sure what it was actually doing, or
how it really worked. The next step would be “let’s see
what happens if we flip two lines of code.” Usually, noth-
ing would work anymore.
590B.B 81 †
gProgramming · Languages 24
gLines · of · Code
gBasic · Programming
gCreate · Simple · Graphics 13
B.B
I initially became frustrated when I realized that 20
programming was hard, but it was quickly replaced by
an overwhelming curiosity. My brother Urs’ C64 actually
came with a manual of the <BASIC> <G>14programming
language, and I started eagerly reading and learning from
it. In hindsight I find it fascinating that, in the middle 25
of rural Switzerland, this machine provided us with a
gateway to this whole alternative culture, a cyber culture
or counter culture of affordable personal computers that
anyone could buy, then go on to learn programming,
and craft their own tools. I mean, this machine had 30
this type of mindset embedded, and the people who
made it were trying to make that a reality through a
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.M 242 Part A: Interview M
INTERVIEWS
A
commercial product. In Switzerland, these computers
were mostly sold as gaming consoles, but they had all this
potential, and were actually quite accessible compared
to today’s computers. Today, computers have so many
layers of complexity in terms of software and operating5
systems, but on these, to load a program, you just had
to type in and run some simple <BASIC><G>14 code. The
statement that would load the program from the floppy
drive consisted of one line of code, and another line
would execute it. The [Commodore] 64 had an extended10
character set of graphical glyphs in its font, like square
and rounded corners, dashed squares, etc., and the easiest
way to create simple graphics was simply by placing these
extended glyphs on the screen in a text grid. It was quite
gratifying, being able to figure this out as a kid, without15
any grownups telling you, you know… just by using this
C64 manual.
R.v.L. Did this curiosity follow you throughout your
studies and professional career?
592J.L. Absolutely. I think that I always kept in mind20
this idea of the purity of what that technology repre-
sented. It was an eye opener for me. Back when I was a
child, I had perceived that programming was a very flexi-
ble, friendly thing. Not so different from Legos® or some-
thing like that, but more exciting, and also something no25
one else really was playing with or even exploring, which
made it all the more attractive to me. We also had a
needle printer, and you could actually control the printer
using <BASIC><G>14 code. You could write a little program
that would print the same thing twenty times. You could30
control the type size, etc. At one point, we even had
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Jürg Lehni A.M 243
.M
JÜRG LEHNI
access to a small pen plotter that we had on loan from
our cousin. We were able to control the pen and make it
follow programmed movements, making drawings using
code. This discovery totally links to what I did later in
life. I felt that this interesting machine could be anything 5
I wanted it to be. It gave you a sense of control, which
is perhaps also quite appealing to a child. I lost interest
at one point, when I began coming up against limita-
tions. I didn’t know where to go from there, what to
do with that. Everyone else played video games instead 10
of writing them. However, later on, in secondary school,
I had a <math teacher>47 who gave us some program-
ming lessons in <Turbo Pascal> <G>78, and he quickly saw that
I had an affinity for it. He worked with me apart from
the standard school program, giving me assignments and 15
books to read. I learned <C++> <G>16that way, and started
writing more serious programs while still in high school.
I continued to explore different directions, perceiving
the computer as this meta tool that could be anything
you wanted it to be. However, doing this in isolation 20
often didn’t feel so good. You have to have a certain
kind of mindset to be able to learn programming. You
need to be persistent, even stubborn. You need to have
a goal you want to attain, and even if you keep hitting a
wall, you need to persevere. These requirements sort of 25
naturally filter out the people who acquire expertise in
this area. It might be different nowadays, but I think the
idea still exists among many people that programming
is not something they really want to do.

B.A
Around the time I left ETH, I began working
with my brother Urs, who was studying graphic design in
Lucerne. In 1999, he was working on a collaborative thesis
```
project: the creation of an ephemeral space for graphic
```
design run by artists called Transport. They were granted5
the use of a shop for a few months before it was going to
be torn down. They used the space to host exhibitions,
events, lectures and parties there, and produced a lot of
ephemera around these events. It was a very experimen-
tal, playful research project, and questioning and creat-10
ing their own tools played a key role in their approach.
I helped them create an interactive experience with a CD-
ROM called Visomat which explored and documented
their experimental project, and also started working on
small software design tools with them. As I look back,15
I think a lot of things evolved out of that period. The
idea of collaborating with graphic designers started there
as well. It became much more exciting than just doing
stuff on my own with a computer. Having a dialogue
with somebody with a specific need, or a challenge, or20
some questions within a field, was a great starting point
from which to develop my skills and my curiosity about
this way of working.
R.v.L. Would that dialogue happen with a
collaborator, or could it also be with a client?25
595J.L. It might be a client. However, in my experience,
those dialogues are less fruitful, in a sense. I think the
most productive collaborations did not happen when
there was a clear goal or need, but rather when you had
an open dialogue about an idea or a piece of technology.30
Sometimes the tool, or whatever I had already created,
already existed. Then we would raise questions about it:
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Jürg Lehni A.M 245
.M
JÜRG LEHNI
Why does this exist? What is it there for? What can
we do with it? Each and every physical device I have
built, from drawing machines to printmaking or marking
machines, can be seen as a platform. Nothing is truly a
finished piece. Basically they are all just starting points 5
with an infinite amount of possibilities. Once they exist,
you have to answer these questions, you have to somehow
define their features and the reason they exist. If you
don’t, then it feels like a missed opportunity.
R.v.L. Might there also exist a dialogue between your 10
machines and a random user? You are the key person
experimenting with your machines: you are interacting
with them, and using them in a specific context.
Would you be able to share them with your public and
say “OK, go ahead, here’s my machine, use it any 15
way you like”?
597 J.L. If I trust them [laughs]!
598 There’s a bit of a line in my work between the
things that I share on purpose, like all the open-source
software, such as <<Scriptographer>48> <G>76, the plug-in 20
for <Adobe> <G>2, 4<Illustrator> with which you can extend
<Illustrator> <G>4and create your own tools. There, of course,
the whole point is sharing <<Scriptographer>48> <G>76with
as many people as possible, and creating a community
that supports itself and exchanges on that subject, so 25
the openness, the generosity is kind of embedded. Then
there are other works, that are centered around my more
personal reflections on how we work through computer
technology, or how we express ourselves. Things like the
drawing machines I have made. For example Hektor, 30
my diploma project at ECAL in 2002, emerged from a
dialogue with Uli Franke, an engineer and good friend
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.M 246 Part A: Interview M
INTERVIEWS
A
of mine whom I met while studying at ETH Zurich.
I invited Uli to collaborate with me on my thesis project.
At that point, my idea was simply to create a large-scale
plotter that would translate graphics from the computer
in a slightly unpredictable or surprising way, one that was5
raw, imprecise, almost human. Something that was not
as precise and clean and perfect as standardized printers.
The path we took, bouncing ideas back and forth between
ourselves, and taking many detours, eventually led us to
the idea for Hektor. It was a very exciting, personal, not10
very linear process, full of ideas and experimentations.
Eventually, we ended up with the really simple principle
of using triangulation, two strings programmed to change
length through stepper motors. It was so pure, in a way,
so close to my fascination with geometry, which was my15
access point to math. The machine became a very direct
translation of my fascination for geometry, and a physical
manifestation of it, and we both felt very personally
attached to this idea. We didn’t feel we wanted to share
```
that; we didn’t feel like sharing our recipe so people could20
```
make their own versions. This was very personal for us.
We wanted to explore Hektor’s potential on our own,
give it an identity and create a body of work around it.

Interview with Jürg Lehni A.M 247
.M
JÜRG LEHNI
R.v.L. So you didn’t sell it either?
600 J.L. No, we didn’t. Now, other people sell it. There
have been hundreds of versions of that principle—of
course we can’t tell people not to create versions. You
can’t own the simplicity of such an elemental idea. That 5
is also a hard lesson of technology: once you create some-
thing and show it to the world, other people will have
ideas about what they would like to do with it, and they
will go on to make their own versions and expand from
there. Technology exists in a strange position between 10
creativity and culture, or language, something that we all
share. With my work, I want to communicate that open-
ness with technology as something that big corporations
don’t completely own and control. I think it’s much nicer
to think of it as something that we all shape, and change, 15
and that we can reappropriate and reinterpret. I think
that’s very positive, but it’s funny because, sometimes,
as an artist, I actually suffer from that openness, such
as when, as you know, people appropriate my work, and
suddenly I find myself saying, “that’s my idea!” 20
R.v.L. You can’t protect the work from being copied?
602 J.L. Well, you could, I guess. You could do what
people sometimes do, patent things and then take people
to court, and become angry and bitter. I don’t think it’s
really a good way of protecting your work. More than 25
any other field, I feel that technology has this inherent
openness. I also feel like open-source culture is very much
like that. This notion that there is a greater good in doing
open-source and that by simply open-sourcing an idea
that already exists, you are doing something good. 30
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.M 248 Part A: Interview M
INTERVIEWS
A
R.v.L. But that is not by definition the case?
604J.L. I don’t think so. I mean, I’ve seen this happen-
ing. People creating very similar open-source versions of
work that already existed, without giving any credit or
attribution to the previous work, or involving the original5
creators. Apparently the simple fact that something is
released as open-source makes them feel justified in doing
so. There is a sort of sense of entitlement in open-source
culture. There’s also a lack of awareness of history. It’s a
complex discussion, one which is strongly connected to10
the question of authorship.
R.v.L. That’s interesting, because our research project
also questions the economic value of programming. So
we have open-source on one side, and licensed or
embedded software on the other. Do you see anything15
in between?
606J.L. Open-source authors often release works under
dual licensing, where you can obtain something through
a creative open-source license, without technical support,
but you also have no right to use it commercially. If you20
want to use it commercially, you have to get a commercial
license. I was actually attempting to do something like
this with <Scriptographer><G>76 . If you want to create such
a license by yourself, it is really complicated. You could
possibly build from something that already exists. I mean,25
who understands law unless they’re a lawyer? It’s like
another programming language. However, you still have
to obey it. It’s the same situation with patents. In and
of themselves, they don’t earn you anything. You have
to go after the people who don’t follow the licensing or30
don’t respect it.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Jürg Lehni A.M 249
.M
JÜRG LEHNI
R.v.L. The machines you make have obvious, visual
outputs. The resulting images are very tactile, very
tangible. Does this enhance the fact that they are easy
to copy or appropriate?
608 J.L. Not always, but yeah, it’s true that in my work, 5
I always look for the simplicity of gestures. I think that’s
also what fascinates me with machines to begin with.
That kind of very gentle, resourceful hand gesture that
draws a line. Of course, there’s a certain transparency
in the process. You watch it. It’s not magic, it unveils 10
itself, it explains itself. It wants to be transparent. You
can probably figure out its internal workings if you’re
an engineer. However, sometimes the simple gestures
are the hardest to get right. For example, Viktor, the
chalk drawing machine that works on triangulation us- 15
ing four motors, may look as simple as Hektor, but it’s
actually the opposite. It’s endlessly complex because it
requires consistent tension in the cables in order to apply
pressure against the wall. Otherwise the chalk wouldn’t
leave much of a trace. The four motors all have to apply 20
an exact amount of tension to create the right amount
of pressure, and they all need to work in perfect syn-
chronization, otherwise they block each other. The coils
change diameter with each rotation, and the tangential
exit point of the cables depends on the tool’s position. 25
Everything in the system is dynamic, and figuring out the
math required in order to get the machine to position
the chalk precisely and move in perfect synchroniza-
tion with those four motors was endlessly complex. Al-
though the resulting visual expression is simple in the end, 30
I think this would be a hard thing to replicate.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.M 250 Part A: Interview M
INTERVIEWS
A
R.v.L. Do you reflect on the message you want to
transmit with your machines beforehand? Especially the
messages drawn by the machine?
610J.L. Yeah. It really started with an intuitive under-
standing of working through computation and through5
computers in a creative manner. I was trying to tran-
scend the confines of the screen, because I had begun
to get frustrated about working onscreen all the time.
Then there are the works—the contents became natural
reflections on that theme, and it started expanding from10
there. At one point, I began to understand that a lot
of it was about a machine having a certain character,
or voice. It almost became an entity on its own, I be-
gan to think, “Hektor does this, Hektor goes to MoMA,
Hektor meets Dexter Sinister.” The encounters might15
be with a person, a place or a subject. It was always
about what the entity did. There was a narrative flowing
through all these works. They were mostly about writing
or drawing. For instance, the William Morris piece that
I did with Will Holder was, to a great degree, about his20
reflections on the notion of addressing industrialization.
So we started out with Morris, an Arts and Crafts artist
working at the beginning of the era of industrialization,
whose response to machine production was the urge to
preserve the humanity in it. Hektor is at the other end25
of the spectrum: a post-industrial computer-controlled
device that’s designed to be imperfect and almost human.
Still, there is this strange connection between the two,
and pre- and post-industrialization. The Hektor piece
that is a realization of one of Morris’ repeating patterns,30
originally developed for textile prints, and is now repli-
cated in spray paint on a wall twelve meters long, is a
celebration of that connection.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Jürg Lehni A.M 251
.M
JÜRG LEHNI
R.v.L. These links or references and ideas for content,
do they come up once you finish a machine, or do these
reflections already pop up during the design process?
612 J.L. It’s hard to make a clear distinction in hindsight.
I would say it has been a complex interplay between the 5
themes I have researched and the technologies that I’ve
created over the years. During my time at HyperWerk
and ECAL, I worked on a bunch of web projects with
Urs, my brother, and Raphael Koch, along with some
other people—it all started with the Transport project 10
I mentioned earlier. We were creating screen-based design
applications, like Vectorama.org, a graphical online multi-
user playground, that was a kind of <Adobe> <G>2<Illustra-
tor> <G>4for the masses, as well as some other ones before
that where we took a single graphical element, for exam- 15
ple a Lego® brick, or a Casio® LCD Watch, and crafted
visual tools around them. In a way, you might describe
them as <parodies>49 of design softwares. I mean, they
were always a bit tongue-in-cheek, we were having fun.
I think the underlying notion was always to question the 20
status quo or <Adobe> <G>2, and the tools that we as pro-
fessionals are working with. This notion ultimately lead
to the creation of <Scriptographer> <G>76, which we launched
as a kind of meta-tool for designers to create their own
tools using a simple scripting language within <Adobe> <G>225
<Illustrator> <G>4, 76. Creating <Scriptographer> and making
it freely available was an act of political activism for
me, the plug-in was a symbiotic open-source piece of
software, living within its closed source software host

After <Scriptographer><G>76 , I reached a phase where
I grew tired of the confines of the screen, and wanted
to move beyond them. I continued to seek a means of5
critiquing the standardized ways of working creatively
with and through technology, and making a printer was
the logical next step for me. However, I don’t think that
I really understood what Hektor should be about at
that point. When I compare programming machines to10
programming purely onscreen, I would say I never had
as many surprises. This only happened when I stepped
outside of my comfort zone of working onscreen, since
working with pure software is not as unpredictable.
614When you program a lot, you reach a point15
when you kind of know what to expect from a piece of
code before you write it. Of course, there are surprises
that you can leverage and work with. However, I work
very much like an engineer in that respect, so there are

R.v.L. It depends on the level you’re at when you
step in, as well as what you know already.
616 J.L. Yes, that too. When I started working with me-
chanics, motors and physical contraptions, giving these 10
objects a body and even a behavior through software,
then all these new elements, like sound, friction, and grav-
ity, came into play. There’s no way you can anticipate
all this. With Hektor, there was a sort of Frankenstein
moment, after conceiving and crafting all the mechani- 15
cal and electronic parts of the device, and writing the
software, we eventually assembled all these components
and then, suddenly, it moves for the first time. It was
so different from what we thought it would be: some of
our ideas didn’t work out the way they were supposed 20
to, so we had to adjust our strategy and expectations.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.M 254 Part A: Interview M
INTERVIEWS
A
Suddenly you are face to face with this thing you have
created, but you feel like you have to get to know it, to
figure out what it actually is, and what it can become.
R.v.L. Did you have to debug, in terms of anticipating
and making modifications based on the feedback you5
got from the machine?
618J.L. The biggest surprise with Hektor was that we
actually had intended to guide the movements with four
motors, as we did later with Viktor. We thought we
needed the lower two motors for stability, but we couldn’t10
attain an adequate degree of precision with the stepper
motors and calibration techniques we used. The motors
kept blocking each other and they lost steps. So we re-
alized that we had to get rid of the two lower motors.
At that point, it was only two weeks until the thesis15
project was due. We had very little time left, and this
was the only option, but we thought that this was going
to be really unstable, we doubted that it would work
at all. The only option was to do something with the
software to compensate for that instability. I began to20
think of ways in which I could make it move more care-
fully, more smoothly. This actually resulted in Hektor’s
signature motion. I came up with an algorithm that was
very geometric, because that’s how I think. The concept
I started from was what if this were a car, and I was25
driving without brakes and reaching out of the window
with a brush, with the car moving at a constant speed
and I was trying to steer at the same time. How would
you draw a square in these circumstances? You would
drive away on circles in the corners and come back to the30
next line and then push the brush again. This is pretty
much how I visualized it. What was so surprising, was
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Jürg Lehni A.M 255
.M
JÜRG LEHNI
that I had thought of it purely visually and then, when it
translated into behavior and actual movement, it became
such an intrinsic part of Hektor’s identity. This had not
been something I could anticipate. It was just kind of a
lucky accident. You also have to accept things, because 5
there are so many limitations. The machine was very
slow because of all this extra movement. Still, when we
began to show Hektor in action, people would say things
like, “oh, it’s like a painter, anticipating or planning their
next stroke.” Hektor’s drawing trajectories, with all their 10
potential imperfections, immediately became part of the
machine’s identity, and we just embraced it.

I mean, I think everything you can do with a computer

ming in design. There is always going to be a current

it’s machine learning until the next new thing. The fact
that you’re using code is not enough. You have to have
a really good reason for using it.

A

Any kind of material that you’re working with
holds a certain potential, right? So you could call it
artistic potential, or poetic potential, or whatever. Un-
covering that potential is what makes the work good.
That’s the hardest thing, no matter what the technology5
or the material. However, with programming, or tech-
nology in general, there is this underlying seduction of
the notion of the complexity of the machine doing the
work. As if the machine is being creative, a rather false
idea. This whole narrative about how machine learning10
will replace the artists is kind of defeating. I do think
that there can be good art using machine learning, but
I have yet to see it. I remain very suspicious of this
celebration of the complexity, romanticizing the com-
plexity of the machine, of its ability to process massive15
amounts of data.
622 ‡ B.D 133g
Design · School 26
gArtists · and · Designers 18
gDon · t · Think
B.D
I don’t think it is our place as artists and de-
signers to be in awe of technology. I think that’s the
wrong approach and not the right message. If anything,
it should make us feel more humble, or friendly, or en-20
courage us to be more approachable, not less. This goes
back to what’s important in education. Technology is
all around us, and it’s part of our everyday life. It’s
part of our culture. I don’t think everyone will become a
programmer. I feel that schools that want to reach that25
goal will probably fail. If a design school has the goal of
having every student knowing how to program when they
leave, I think it will fail, and it isn’t even a necessary
achievement. Rather, I would say it is more about attain-
ing a certain literacy about programming, and a capacity30
to work with programmers and understand them, and
this takes an effort on both sides. Literacy is always both,
right? You write and you read. One form of literacy is
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Jürg Lehni A.M 257

JÜRG LEHNI
being able to cut through the bad examples. With every
new technology, there’s always this phase of feeling awe,
then we get bored, and then eventually, we start using it
in a more meaningful way.
R.v.L. When you are teaching, how do you prevent 5
