tendencies with technology?
624B.D 126 †
gTechnology 11
gStudent 8
B.D
J.L. It’s hard. I mean, I understand that students
have their motivations, they have their idols, their ideas,
things they have seen and want to replicate. Right now, 10
we live in a culture where that impressive technology is
floating on top. It’s also about the most visual thing,
the most juicy thing on Instagram, one might say. These
incentives are not the right ones, and I feel that steering
against that is quite important. 15
R.v.L. Do you have teaching methods that stress this
search for the most intelligent use of programming?
626 J.L. Not the most intelligent, but more importantly
the most personal. Do you have a connection to code?
Do you feel it’s yours, that you made it and you can 20
actually use it? When I was teaching programming us-
ing <Scriptographer> <G>76with Jonathan Puckey, or later
with <paper.js> <G>64, 41, the <JavaScript> version we created
together that was born out of <Scriptographer> <G>76, we
tried to teach a limited vocabulary within the capacity 25
of those libraries. We selected a vocabulary that could be
quite expressive. For example, one thing that has worked
quite well was making drawing tools that have some sort
of autonomy or behavior which you still use by hand.
What I like about that approach is that you’re going 30
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.M 258 Part A: Interview M
INTERVIEWS
A
back and forth between being the engineer or craftsper-
son of your tool, and also the user of that tool. This does
not necessitate drawing something beautiful right away,
but simply making a bunch of strokes and seeing what
happens. Then we get into tweaking its behavior, using5
that to see how things change. The idea is establishing
this direct connection between the code and its appli-
cation. I think this is very important for teaching. It’s
also where I struggled with teaching machine learning,
this idea of the direct connection. There’s Bret Victor, a10
researcher in human computer interfaces, who used to
work for <Apple><G>9 , but is now self-employed and working
on a couple of really interesting projects in the Bay Area.
He did a lot of lectures and writing on a topic he refers
to as the “immediate connection.” He basically posits15
that the way we are currently programming computers
is still stuck in the same paradigm as the 1950s with
<Fortran><G>28 . For the most part, we’re still writing linear
programs that the computer executes step by step, and,
once it’s running, we can’t really interfere with it. He is20
an advocate of reducing the threshold between the mod-
ification of a program and its application, but also the
visual perception of the impact of change. The shorter
this feedback loop is, the better for the humans who
write software. He doesn’t really have a solution, but25
he proposes a lot of things in his lectures that might
be seen as directions. A few years back he criticized
<Processing><G>68 rather strongly for remaining stuck in that
paradigm, and perpetuating that in creative coding. He
does have an interesting point there. You could say that30
<paper.js><G>64, 76 and <Scriptographer> are also stuck in this
paradigm, especially <Scriptographer><G>76 , which offers a
more immediate connection. The fact that you use it
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Jürg Lehni A.M 259
.M
JÜRG LEHNI
in a graphic design software—<Adobe Illustrator> <G>4—one
with which graphic designers are already familiar, means
you understand how a document is built in <Illustra-
tor> <G>4. It’s almost physical, right? You have an emulated
sheet of paper, with layers of visual objects, to which 5
one can assign properties such as color, scale, rotation,
etc.: it feels virtually physical, right? These are all ele-
ments that are familiar when you use <Illustrator> <G>4. If
you then go into a programming environment that offers
you access to those same things, you can draw an analogy 10
with the manual processes through which you created
them. So as you’re writing the program, you can kind
of understand what tool you would use if you had done
it by hand in <Illustrator> <G>4. You can use these familiar
tools to inspect and dissect the output of your program. 15
<Scriptographer> <G>76, 64and <paper.js> replicate this mental
model in their <API> <G>10through object-oriented program-
ming, which has a tangibility that other environments
lack because they are more pixel-based. Tools like <Pro-
cessing> <G>68, 59, <p5.js>, and openFrameworks, use a different 20
mental model where you work in a more procedural man-
ner, telling the machine what to do at every frame of the
animation, how to draw the image from scratch. You’re
basically redrawing the thing onscreen. Consequently,
it’s much closer to the machine, and that has its bene- 25
fits. Also, learning that is good because you’re learning
how the computer works, but the <Scriptographer> <G>76, 64and
<paper.js> method is perhaps more suitable for design-
ers. This is just a theory, but my assumption is that it’s
actually closer to the way a designer thinks because it’s 30
using the same abstractions that you already know from
designing software, and the physical world. So, when I
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

teach code, I try to find ways of making that connection
as close as possible.

R.v.L. Are you saying that you think programming is
somehow counter-intuitive for designers, since they have
to accept the loss of control?5
628J.L. Yeah, but then you also do that when you are
working with print, right? When you make a book, you
have to accept the limitations of the process. As with
any process, you have to figure out what your capabil-
ities are, what it represents for you, and how you can10
leverage this creatively. I think that good books often go
beyond that.
629Speaking of how surrounding circumstances in-
fluence a given work, it would be interesting to discuss
the various intellectual models behind the framework.15
Within open-source culture, there’s always a kind of stan-
dardization that occurs. A certain framework suddenly
becomes “the one,” you know. Right now <p5.js><G>59 has
become the de facto standard in art school education in
terms of programming, or creative coding, as it’s referred20
to these days. Lauren Lee McCarthy launched <p5.js><G>59
as the next version of <Processing><G>68 , native to the Web.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Jürg Lehni A.M 261
.M
JÜRG LEHNI
It was really important to Lauren that it be an inclusive
culture, where anyone was welcome, and there was a
clear code of conduct, upheld by good intentions and
values. Now, because of the drive to standardize I had
mentioned, it has almost become a threat of sorts to the 5
inherent diversity of programming culture in this area.
Perhaps I am overstating things a bit, but I do think it’s
an interesting notion to consider. Of course I say that
because I am an <author>50 of another framework that
has been used in art schools and now is perhaps being 10
used less, but I don’t have to feel bad about that, even
if I do notice the declining interest in this framework.
630 When I look at <p5.js> <G>59, I see a massive library
that offers a great deal of functionality and a pretty sub-
stantial memory footprint. They include so many differ- 15
ent ways of doing things. There’s no clear <convention>51
about how elements that are part of the vocabulary are

A.M 266 Part A: Interview M
INTERVIEWS
A
The variable font file is, in and of itself, just a
tool. It’s a way of making a lot of different weights. I
think Petr van Blokland told me that the reason why
variable fonts exist was <Google ><G>34 , he was in touch with
<Google ><G>34 , and had heard that it could save them a5
lot of file space and bandwidth. <Google ><G>34 has this
whole <Google ><G>34 Fonts repository of “free” fonts, “free”
in quotes because when you use their fonts through
<CSS><G>17 , and upload them directly from their server, you
give them all your traffic information at the same time.10
It’s the same with the Facebook “like” button, right?
Every website that places the like button actually pro-
vides Facebook with all their traffic information.
647 ‡ B.A 45g
Variable · Fonts 28
gReason · why · Variable · Fonts 28
B.A
<Google ><G>34 ’s whole business model is of course
based on data, and that’s how they get to that. Now,15
if they have a web font with lots of glyphs, supporting
lots of different language scripts and different weights
and styles, and you load the whole family, with all these
files included, you are loading a consequent amount of
data, in megabytes. If it’s a variable font, you only load20
outlines, and the rest is interpolated and extrapolated,
so the file decreases in size. That’s why they invested
so much money in it, apparently. That’s what I heard.
I think this is interesting, because a lot of technology
does come into existence in this very pragmatic way, and25
then subsequently shows some creative potential, and
it is the designer’s job to find ways to have fun with it.
The initial reason why variable fonts are here was not to
make cool fonts move. It was just a possible solution for
data storage problems, and to save money.30

650 Whatever the technology of the moment is, we
don’t really question it. We barely see it. We just notice
the content, but it’s standard. Very quickly, you get
used to new technology and displays, and then they just
become the new normal. Yet, when they disappear, like 20
say when the split-flap displays at the train station in
Zurich disappeared, people felt nostalgic, because they

appeared, because it was turning mechanically. 25

652 J.L. Exactly. Technology becomes a part of your cul-

A.N 270 Part A: Interview N
INTERVIEWS
A.N
ZACH
LIEBER-
MA N
www.zach.li
Zach
Lieberman
Interviewed by
Demian Conrad
<Zach Lieberman>54 is an artist, researcher,
and educator with a simple goal: he wants to
surprise you. His work consists of creating perfor-
mances and installations that take human gesture
as input, and amplifying them in different ways:
making drawings come to life, imagining what
a voice might look like if we could see it, trans-
forming the silhouettes of people into music.
He has been listed as one of Fast Company’s Most
Creative People and his projects have been
awarded the Golden Nica from Ars Electronica,
the Interactive Design of the Year award from
Design Museum London, and are listed among
Time Magazine’s Best Inventions of the Year.
He creates artwork by writing software and is a
co-creator of openFrameworks, an open source
C++ toolkit for creative coding. Lieberman also
co-founded the School for Poetic Computation,
a school which examines the lyrical possibilities of
code. He is a professor at the MIT Media Lab,
where he leads the Future Sketches group.
Interview with Zach Lieberman A.N 271
Demian Conrad Hi Zach, thanks for taking the time
to speak with me today. Can you begin by introducing
yourself and presenting your practice?
657 Zach Lieberman My name is Zach Lieberman. I’m an
artist and educator based in New York City. I tend to 5
divide my time between teaching, art work, and com-
mercial work. I sort of divide these three things evenly
as a model for my professional practice. I would put
commercial work in the category of design and computa-
tional generative design practice. My background is in 10
```
fine arts; I studied painting and printmaking, and I got
```
into working with code and software almost accidentally.
I had to get a job and everybody was doing web design,
so I got a job in a design studio, and I discovered a tool
called <Flash> <G>25. 15
658 <Flash> <G>25was almost a gateway drug for a lot of
young designers and people interested in creative coding,
because it was a tool for animation, but also had a
scripting capability. So you could do everything with the
timeline, but then you could also say “this object x equals 20
x plus one”, and it would move across the screen. For
me, there was something very beautiful about writing
language and having that translate into motion. Then
I went to graduate school, <Parsons School of Design>55,
and fell in love with creative coding. After that, I helped 25
start a school in New York, called the School for Poetic
Computation, a sort of experimental school, and two
years ago I became a professor at the <MIT Media Lab> <G>48.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

Interview with Zach Lieberman A.N 275
.N
ZACH LIEBERMAN
D.C. Is the choice of technology a political
statement?
666B.B 90 †
gMachine · Learning 12
gArt · and · Design 10
B.B
Z.L. For me it’s important that art and design be put
first, and technology second. The things that you want to
do, the stories you want to tell, or the experiments you 5
want to create are important, and technology should be
at the service of these ideas. I think one of the challenges,
especially in education, is that students can get very
excited about a technology and so I think you then have
a technology first and art and design second approach. 10
They get really excited about data visualization, or about
machine learning, basically the technology. I think those
projects suffer because they’re sort of tech first, so they
are basically creating demonstrations of the technology,
and I always worry about the stories—the work that 15
we do is all about telling stories. Especially in terms of
these big companies: I don’t want to make work which is
telling a <Google > <G>34or a Facebook story. I want to make
work that tells my story or other people’s stories.
D.C. Why and how did you launch the School for 20
Poetic Computation [SFPC]?
668B.D 133 †
gDesign · School 29
B.D
Z.L. In 2013, feelings were running high in New York,
where I’m based. There is a really famous design school
in the US named Cooper Union. Tuition had been free
for a hundred years because they had this really large en- 25
dowment. Then the board made a decision and suddenly
they began charging high tuition fees, and students got
really upset. They protested, and the prevailing feeling
was that universities cost too much. All design schools
[in the US] are very expensive, and Cooper Union’s being 30
free to attend was a notable exception. In America, at
that time, a lot of universities were building campuses
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.N 276 Part A: Interview N
INTERVIEWS
A
in Dubai, or Shanghai, and you started to see these sit-
uations come up, and you didn’t really understand the
economics. So I was really frustrated.
669 ‡ B.D 136g
Service · of · Making · Poetry 17
gSeven · Years 14
gTen-week · Program 12
B.D
As a result, some friends and I decided to get
together and start a low-cost school, where we would5
publish the financial data and be completely transparent
so you could go there rather than going for a two-year
Master’s degree that would cost several thousand dollars.
At SFPC, you can enroll in a ten-week program and
get a feeling for what this medium is all about, without10
necessarily committing to spending a large amount of
time or money. We do a ten-week program twice a year,
along with shorter programs, and we have been running
for seven years. For me, it was about creating a space
for organic education around computation, and trying15
to teach what it feels like and how it can be used in the
service of making poetry. The title, Poetic Computation,
is about creating poetry through code.
670It’s very much about learning very low-level
concepts. For example, if you go to learn how to use20
electronics creatively, you typically use <Arduino><G>11 mi-
crocontrollers, you start at this kind of level, where things
are already made. However, at our school, you start from
the ground up and you learn about the electricity, cir-
cuits and building half adders, adders, and all the things25
that go into making a computer. In the beginning, the
students are very frustrated, because they don’t get why
they are working at such a basic level, but later they dis-
cover that those ideas and concepts relate to what they
can do within <Arduino><G>11 . They also relate better to30
what you can accomplish with fancier equipment. We’re
trying to create a pedagogy around the essence of a thing:
what is the essence of electronics, what is the essence
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Zach Lieberman A.N 277
.N
ZACH LIEBERMAN
of code? In the school, it’s organized around three pil-
```
lars: code, electronics and theory, all in the service of
```
making poetry.
Fig.39
Detail from the SFPC online
site, Zach Lieberman, 2018.
D.C. In your opinion, why is poetry relevant today?
672B.D 140 †
gCreative · Coding 6
gWriting · Code 7
gTeaching · Code 14
B.D
Z.L. I’m in a field that you would typically describe as 5
creative coding, but that’s celebrating the act of writing
code, and to me it cries out for another name. Could
we celebrate poetry? Could we celebrate the act of
writing poems? It’s just a framing mechanism, but there
is also something very beautiful about poetry, which is 10
an oral tradition, as well as a technique for telling stories
and remembering texts, and for speaking. I think there’s
something very beautiful about it, especially when you’re
teaching code. So much of it is about talking, about
conversation, and perhaps these artificial languages are 15
still about talking. I think in poetry you’re trying to use
very precise words in the right order, and when you’re
coding, you’re trying to use the right words in the right
order. There’s a sort of terseness to a good poem, it can
be very short and precise, like a haiku. It can be three 20
lines, but tell you a really deep story, and the same goes
for code: if you have a very precise for loop, something
kind of magical can emerge from it. I think there is a lot
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.N 278 Part A: Interview N
INTERVIEWS
A
of interesting overlap, and feel it’s important when one
thinks about language as it relates to computation. This
is why we use the word poetry.
673Also, one of the things that we think about in
terms of the school is that computation itself is becom-5
ing increasingly smaller, more invisible and embedded.
Computers are getting smaller, cheaper, and less visible.
I think we need to explore ways to increase visibility,
to make them more comprehensible, more accessible,
since they play an increasingly important role and are10
an intrinsic part of our lives. We want artists and de-
signers to be involved, showing what’s possible, pointing
out the inconsistencies and pushing for a better future
with them.
D.C. How is experimentation important at school?15
675 ‡ B.D 122g
Think · it · s · Important
gShow · Students 29
B.D
Z.L. I would say that what is important for the stu-
dents is that they’re just constantly making, and not
overthinking. I like the word experiment, and I remem-
ber that, when I saw the famous poster designer Niklaus
Troxler speak, at the end of the talk, he said “to the20
students in the audience, I have three words of advice:
experiment, experiment, experiment.” I thought that was
really beautiful, and that it’s an important message for
students. If you’re making, you’re developing intuition,
discovering pathways and shortcuts, and all that is really25
important. I always seek to create an environment where
students are constantly creating and where you model
that curiosity is really valued. As a teacher, I think it’s
important that you show students how important it is
to be curious, and what provokes your own curiosity.30

B.D
You should come to class with a hundred exam-
ples of really esoteric, weird stuff, in order to provide
them with the space to be, to encourage that same kind
of mentality. You want them to be curious about what
they care about. As a teacher, I might be curious about a 5
specific 1980s Japanese poster design, or some graphical
language that I really care about, or some type studios
that I’m obsessed with or whatever. You want the stu-
dents to bring their obsessions into the classroom, and
feel good about being obsessed. As a teacher, all I can 10
do is share my story, show them the things that I care
about and how I got to do what I do and how I think
about it, but you want them to be able to write their
own story. You want to create the kind of atmosphere in
which they feel comfortable about sharing what excites 15
them. Experimentation is really important.

INTERVIEWS
A
677I went to Parsons, a design school. I always
hated classes where you just talked about what you were
going to make, you know. You go to a studio class and
everybody says what they are going to do, but from a
theoretical perspective. I hated it because it was not5
about actually making, it was about talking. I always
think whatever you can do to get students to just iterate
and create helps them understand their intuitions better.
You can have a better conversation about their ideas
and get out of the realm of the theoretical and into10
the practical.
D.C. What are your next steps in terms of
education?
679Z.L. We have had to move SFPC online, Covid has
been really difficult for us. Right now we are figuring out15
how to better organize ourselves, but we’re still pushing.
I’m currently starting up a new group at the <MIT
Media Lab><G>48 , so I’m also back at university. The group
is called Future Sketches, and it’s inspired by Muriel
Cooper, a former professor at the <MIT Media Lab><G>48 20
and a major figure in design. She had this letter that she
wrote in 1980, describing a visual essay that she made,
and, at the end of the letter she said “this will stand as
a sketch for the future.” I love this phrase, and in the
group we’re trying to imagine what the future may look25
like through sketching and making.
680 ‡ B.C 100g
Approach 3
gProject 29
gStudent 27
gPedagogic 3
B.C
What interests me is when students come in
with things about which they are passionate and make
tools and projects that relate to them. If they really
care a lot about typography, audiovisual expression,30
or theater, they can build a set of experiences and
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Zach Lieberman A.N 281

ZACH LIEBERMAN
work around that, but they also build platforms, tech-
nologies and tools that they can teach. It is almost a
pedagogical approach.
681B.D 125 †

B.D
In terms of my own experience, I feel really con-
nected to the Media Lab, because I came up studying the 5
work of <John Maeda>58’s Aesthetics + Computation
Group, and of course <Processing> <G>68, all of which are
products of the <MIT Media Lab> <G>48. I feel connected to
that design tradition in a lot of ways. I am interested in
creating a space for designers and artists to further ex- 10
plore the role of computation in their practice. In terms
of my practice, I have my students recreate work. I teach
a class called Recreating the Past, where we study a
different artist and designer every week, and this last
week I talked about Muriel Cooper, <John Maeda>58, 15
and my students are engaging with their work. That
makes me really happy.
D.C. In a digitalized future, how do you see paper
evolve as a medium?
683 Z.L. I always tell beginner students to print out code, 20
to take code that they like, print it out on paper, then
take a highlighter, take a pen, draw on it, mark it up,
work with it on paper because it’s text, it’s language. If
you have ever had the experience of writing, you write a
letter or you write an essay and you print it out and then 25
suddenly you can see spelling mistakes, you can cross out
a line, or rewrite it. I think if we only interact with text
on a computer screen, we have this very narrow window
into what text is. I always suggest to try reading and
writing on paper, or have something you can actually 30
hold in your hands since it’s about language.

B.D
I think you see also a lot of times when people
wish to return to things that are very physical. You have
experimentation with pen plotters, 3D printers, and laser
cutters and things where there is a physical manifestation
of a computational process. If you look at Instagram,5
you see videos of people doing calligraphy, you just see a
hand drawing. It’s so lovely because we are surrounded by
screens, and I think it is really important to be reminded
of the hand. Personally, I feel that drawing is a really
important part of our pedagogy at SFPC. We do a lot10
of drawing exercises, we do a lot of work on paper and
I think it should be an important part of design education.
D.C. How is it possible to monetize your
experiments on Instagram?15
686Z.L. I have existed for many years without sharing
on social media, just doing commercial work and working
with festivals and so on. For about four years, I’ve been
doing daily sketches and posting a lot on Instagram and
it has led to a lot of different types of jobs. The more you20
share your work and your style, the more likely it is that
people will hire you, because they can see something in
what you’ve done and they can connect what you are
doing with what they’re thinking about, and just say
“I want that.”25
687As an artist, that’s really great, because if some-
body says that, you know how to do it, you’ve already
done it, and there’s an aspect of that that I find really
valuable. Then there are all these side businesses—you
can make prints and sell them, etc. It’s a way of pushing30
yourself, as well as gaining a better understanding of
your artistic intuitions.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Zach Lieberman A.N 283
.N
ZACH LIEBERMAN
D.C. Would you say that the term graphic designer
remains relevant as a term to describe someone who
also does coding?
689 Z.L. I always think of graphic design as a kind of
funny name to describe what we do, because it’s so much 5
broader. If you think of graphic design, you think of the
person designing the graphics, who makes the pictures, or
says where they should go, but now design practice feels
far more system-oriented than object-oriented, dealing
with time and other variables that would perhaps have 10
been inconceivable to earlier generations. The thing that
I like about the discipline is that it’s full of people who
are intimately familiar with visual systems.
690 I think of myself more as a media artist than
a graphic designer, but when I hang out with people 15
doing type design, there is something so amazing about
the way we care about very tiny details. If you talk to
a type designer about the choices that they made in a
typeface, it’s all about very minute differences, a little
bit thinner here, thicker here, an angle change… As a 20
coder, a lot of what I do is about very minute differences.
I have these numbers and I’m saying, ×0.1 or ×0.2 or
×0.3. They are tiny mathematical changes that lead to
very different results. It’s the same thing for people who
tinker with designing typefaces or other types of graphical 25
systems. The term graphic design really brings layout to
mind for me. I think there’s a much broader field that
people currently work in, that includes UX, UI, motion
graphics, motion design, information visualization, so,
consequently, I feel like the term may be a little outdated. 30
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

D.C. What are the next steps for openFrameworks?
692 ‡ B.B 89g
Machine · Learning 12
gFocus · on · the · Creative 11
B.B
Z.L. It’s a project that has reached a certain level of
maturity. It started in 2004-2005, so it has had a long
lifespan. I feel like it was part of a generation of tools
and I don’t know what that next generation of tools5
looks like, but I think they should help artists work cre-
atively with machine learning, augmented reality, etc.
They should help explore what these things are and feel
like. Maybe there will be different tools that help you do
that, add-ons or plug-ins. I’m really fascinated with that10
and I want to focus on the creative applications of ma-
chine learning systems that help us understand the body
and respond to gestures, those seem really interesting. I
just have a feeling, I cannot really predict what future
tools may look like, besides, I think it’s a task for the15
next generation. I created openFrameworks when I was
young and I feel that younger people might have a better
sense of what these tools should be and how they should
be created.
D.C. Do you think we could define creative coding as20
a global movement?
694 ‡ B.A ??g

B.A
Z.L. I don’t know about numbers, but <Processing><G>68
does publish their download numbers, and you can see
that millions of people use these tools. I think it’s having
an impact all over the place, whether it’s in the field of25
motion graphics or generative design systems, even in
education. I do think it’s a tool like any other.
695 ‡ B.B 69g

B.B
One of the things I’m really afraid of is people
feeling like programming is so important, that everybody
needs to learn how to code, and that if we teach every-30
body to code, it will solve all the world’s problems. A
better way to think about it is as a tool. I think it’s
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Zach Lieberman A.N 285
.N
ZACH LIEBERMAN
certainly an important tool in terms of the impact com-
puting has on our lives, but it’s not the only tool that
you can use to create, it’s just one tool among others.
I think the best thing for students is to gain a familiarity
with a wide range of tools. When you know a tool ex- 5
```
ists, you may not even need to know how to use it well;
```
just knowing the tool is out there is really helpful for
your mind when you are thinking about the problems
you want to solve. I always feel like we should never
frame it as everybody should learn to code, everybody 10
who does graphic design will need a background in com-
puter science, but rather by saying it’s a tool, and we
need to help students understand what it’s good for and
for what it could be used. They need to have a wide
range of tools they can use, so that they can work in a 15
fluid manner.

Luuse

Interviewed by
Rob van Leijsen
<Luuse>59 is a collective based in Brussels whose
aim is to develop alternative methods of editing
and publishing. They strive to encourage a thought-
ful, curious and conscious approach to the relation-
ship between production tools, designers and
users. To this end, the group’s activities span the
fields of pedagogy, research and commissioned
works. Luuse’s activities are aimed at promoting
open systems and free culture through the
development of physical and digital tools and
environments, both practical and theoretical. The
reciprocal transmission of knowledge and creation
of commons, both internally and externally, is a
key component of its raison d'être. The collective
works with a wide variety of groups and
```
individuals: artists, craftspeople, associations,
```
designers, institutions—including schools, colleges
and universities, theoreticians, media institutions,
and the public. Luuse’s methods include the
```
organization of events (workshops, exhibitions,
```
```
conferences) in institutional or non-institutional
```
contexts, the creation of spaces for work and
exchange, and the making of specific tools, as
well as conducting research through either
self-initiated or commissioned work, documenting
their activities to forward their mission. Luuse
is currently made up of the following members:
Antoine Gelgon, Léonard Mabille, Romain
Marula, Étienne Ozeray and Baptiste Tosi.
Interview with Marianne Plano + Léonard Mabille A.O 287
Rob van Leijsen Marianne and Léonard, thank you
for accepting this invitation to speak with us. I would
like to ask you to briefly introduce yourselves and tell us
more about your design practices.
698 Marianne Plano Luuse is made up of a group of six 5
designers. The first four founding members met at art
school, in Valence, France. They came together to work
as a collective on graphic design projects. Later, they
added another member, and the five of them moved to
Brussels in 2015 to create a collective creative space, 10
called Atelier Bek. After two years of sharing a large
workspace with others, they decided to set up a studio
of their own, with a focus on graphic design. Luuse came
into being out of that decision, and I joined them a year
later, in 2017, as their sixth member. 15
699 Most people see us as programmers, but we have
a very diverse practice. Some of us work in the field of
type design, others are more focused on publications,
or book design. A substantial part of our practice is
focused on programming, but making websites is not by 20
any means the sole purpose of the studio. We have a
diverse operation.
700700 Léonard Mabille For instance, we teach in art schools
throughout Belgium. I myself teach type and edito-
rial design. Antoine [Gelgon] also teaches typography. 25
Étienne [Ozeray] and Romain [Marula] teach web design,
and Baptiste [Tosi] teaches creative softwares and coding.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.O 288 Part A: Interview O
INTERVIEWS
A
R.v.L. Did Luuse start out working on commissioned
projects, or was there a phase where you worked on
self-initiated projects before being picked up by clients?
702L.M. We did both. We decided to have a real graphic
design studio structure when we received a big commis-5
sion in 2017 for Villa Noailles, an art center in the south
of France. This was the beginning of our graphic design
commissions under the name Luuse, but before that we
had smaller commissions and self-initiated projects. Villa
Noailles was the first project on which we all worked10
together. It was also when we began developing in-house
tools for design work, applied in a real context.
R.v.L. Tell us more about the collaboration with
Villa Noailles.
704 ‡ B.A 38g
Visual · Identity 16
B.A
L.M. For one year, we worked on Villa Noailles’ visual15
identity. We designed their website, posters, books, etc.
Villa Noailles organizes a lot of events throughout the
year, notably a fashion and photography festival, as well
as exhibitions on design and architecture. One of the
first PR items we created was a small website, a landing20
page for the call for applications for the fashion festival.
We then decided to build the identity progressively. We
developed smaller modules as part of the identity, basing
the typography on a font by an American marine engineer
from the 1960s. Antoine built a type tool that generated25
a custom type family from that open-source lettering
program.
705705M.P. It was important for us that parts of the identity,
like flyers and posters, be generated with web to print
technology. So we designed several scripts and tools to30
create the visuals for Villa Noailles.

The advantage of creating tools lies in being able
to work with multiple formats. When you code, logically
you split content, software and style, so you have three
parts that can be output in different forms and sizes, i.e.
for websites, posters, flyers, etc. 25

Recently, we have been working with a British
publishing house called Divided Publishing. We built
a custom tool, based on <paged.js> <G>63, to generate the
layout of their books. The difficult part was that the
<texts>60in the books had to be justified. So we had 30
to find a way to handle this automatically. Luckily we
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.O 290 Part A: Interview O
INTERVIEWS
A
found a script from a Danish programmer that enabled
justification in <JavaScript><G>41 and we combined that with
our <paged.js><G>63 tool, and, in the end, this worked out.
We designed two publications with this tool, but, for us,
this project reached the limits of what we could do with5
our custom tools and our level of programming skills.
Fig.41
Detail from a publication by

R.v.L. Did the publishing house manage the
content themselves?
713M.P. At first, that was the idea. Initially, the book was
to be generated from text files uploaded by the publisher.10
However, while working on the project we realized that
it wasn’t going to be possible, and we had to go in and
clean up the files after them. We had to debug the code
and adjust functionalities constantly in order to achieve
a correct output. It is hard to imagine a book as an15
automatic thing, because of all the inherent exceptions,
like micro-typography, to name one example.

Textuality · is · something · I · can't · quite ·grasp. · If · I'm · reading · an · ebook, · a · long · poem, · or · even · a · short ·
essay, · I · read · the · text · and · take · it · all · in, · but · I · can't · really · grasp ·what · it · is · exactly. · It's · very · confusing. · I · see · text · as · being · made ·

R.v.L. So did you brush up the export with
conventional software afterwards?
715B.A 55 †

B.A
M.P. No, that was not necessary. We managed to
finish the books entirely with the tool we created. In the
end, we could handle orphans, widows and justification. 5
There were some tiny bugs that remained, like extra
white spaces at the ends of sentences. We had to be
extra careful when going through the <PDF> <G>65s to make
sure the bugs weren’t visible. We could have resolved
them but we simply lacked the money and time and 10
we realized it wasn’t worth the effort. We also said to
```
ourselves: “What’s the point of making a tool that you
```
are only using yourself, which is unreliable as well?” If
you want to create a dependable tool, you need a lot of
time and help from other people. Still, the whole project 15
has been a great experience.
R.v.L. Do you usually get enough economic support
from clients to be able to invest time in custom-made
tools for their commissions?
717B.B 60 †
gBuilding · Tools 21
gWorking · Hours 24
gClients 25
B.B
M.P. No, never. At some point, we thought about 20
how much time we were spending on building tools, but
why should the client have to finance that? We could do
the work much faster with an already existing tool and
without billing all those extra working hours. I am not
sure it is possible to finance your research with a client 25
who is simply expecting a perfect end product.
718B.B 61 †
gCustom · Tools 29
gClients 2
B.B
In the case of the publisher, our client was our
beta tester. They had to be OK with this. When you
are building a custom tool you have to be prepared for
bugs and errors. Also, there are contexts where bugs 30
and errors are the most interesting part, and a lot of
creativity can originate from them, say in the design
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.O 292 Part A: Interview O
INTERVIEWS

of a poster, but a book is more straightforward. When
you send the client <PDF><G>65 s containing errors added by
your own tool, even after proofreading, it’s not a proud
moment. Fortunately, they were very understanding and
supportive with us.5
R.v.L. So the publisher agreed to the working method
but not its financial implications?
720M.P. Well, they came to us because we were using free
software and because of our political position regarding
design, not for a custom <web-to-print><G>87 tool. Still, they10
agreed to the experiment.
R.v.L. How does your approach change your practice
as graphic designers?
722L.M. For me, it changed a lot. If I look back at the
Villa Noailles project, it was really something different15
for me to be dealing with coding software, putting in
data, refreshing the web browser to see the changes I
applied, then printing the webpage to export a <PDF><G>65 .
R.v.L. Is it difficult to choose from among the various20
outputs you generate with your tools? How does the
selection process work between the six of you?
724M.P. We have this website called screenshots.luuse.io
where we archive screenshots of our process, and whatever
we think is interesting as we work. It’s a way for us25
to record the outputs over the course of our projects,
and visuals that won’t necessarily make it to the final
selection. It is also very practical when working with
clients, because we keep track of all the steps. Otherwise,
we trust the team that is in charge of a specific project30
to make the right choices, and the others don’t question
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

LUUSE

that. We also have weekly in-house meetings with the
entire studio to discuss ongoing projects.
725 There was this moment when I realized that
most of the work I do is programming, and you wonder
where the limit lies between being a programmer and a 5
graphic designer, but there is a significant difference. You
don’t get the same commissions. If you use programming
to create a poster and your code is a bit messy, it does
not matter as long as the outcome is compelling, but if
you are creating a website, the code has to work properly. 10
There is real pressure. Here is where the question arises,
when you realize that you are no longer designing with
code, but rather working to optimize your code. This
question must be raised, because our time is limited: is
your focus on becoming a better programmer or creating 15
better forms?
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

A
R.v.L. How similar is your work to that of
a professional programmer?
727M.P. Not at all similar. You might wish to continue
to program everything yourself, without the help of pro-
fessional programmers, but, at that point, you do have5
to ask yourself if that’s really something worth pursuing.
Nevertheless, when you collaborate as a programmer
with people from other fields, you feel a bit like a wizard
that masters a skill they don’t have. The people we work
with recognize the value of it.10
R.v.L. Do your clients still approach you first and
foremost as a graphic design studio?
729M.P. It depends. The more we evolve in this direction,

really have to get our portfolio website out.15

up with your questions before and solved them already.

TA NCRÈ-
DE

www.t-o.studio
Tancrède
Ottiger
Interviewed by
Demian Conrad
and
Rob van Leijsen
<Tancrède Ottiger>62 founded the TO Studio in 2015. TO is
a Swiss-based design practice involved in all fields of print and
interactive design for cultural institutions, businesses and indivi-
duals. The studio is a collaborative practice that includes many
players in the field of design, notably developers, photographers,
industrial designers, architects and filmmakers. The studio handles
works in all media, from print to digital design: books, publications,
identities, web design and scenography. The studio designs both
retail and bespoke typefaces for clients such as Frame or Marie
Claire. Key innovative projects include the interactive map created
for the Ateliers de Renens and the generating identity tool for Les
Teintureries drama school.
Interview with Tancrède Ottiger A.P 299
Demian Conrad Today we are in Lausanne, visiting
Tancrède Ottiger. Thank you for participating in our
project and welcoming us to your office. We would like
to start with a brief introduction about your practice.
748 Tancrède Ottiger Hello. It is a pleasure to have you 5
here. To begin with, I studied at ECAL [University of
Art and Design, Lausanne], and earned a Bachelor’s de-
gree in Graphic Design. After graduation, I immediately
began working as an independent graphic designer and,
at the same time I also worked as a teaching assistant 10
at ECAL for two years. It was a comfortable situation
that gave me the necessary financial security to allow
me to begin exploring the field of graphic design. For
the last three years I have been fully focused on my free-
lance projects. I work alone but I share a workspace with 15
other independent designers. I find that one of the most
interesting things about the practice of graphic design is
the collaboration with professionals from other fields.
D.C. Can you tell us more about the project you
did for Les Teintureries? What is the basic concept and 20
what was your inspiration?
750 T.O. Les Teintureries is a drama school. It’s quite
small, with only three classes of about twelve students
each. It’s comparable to an art school. They approached
me about redefining the school’s visual identity. I asked 25
Romain Cazier, an interaction designer who is also a good
friend, to collaborate with me. So, from the outset, a de-
veloper was included in the design process. I started out
with the basic idea of using colors because of the history
of the school building, which, as the name indicates, used 30
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.P 300 Part A: Interview P
INTERVIEWS
A
to be a dyehouse. The essential idea was to attribute col-
ors to the students attending Les Teintureries. The next
question was: “How would we determine each person’s
color?” Romain and I decided to link a color scheme to
each person’s initials, since they were all different. Each5
of the parameters needed to have a sufficient degree of
variation to generate a variety of colors.
Fig.44
Visual identity,
Les Teinturiers,
Tancrède Ottiger, 2018.
751The banner is a key element, not just of the
website but of the identity as well in the sense that
it functions as a modifiable logo. It is made up of the10
school’s name, followed by a sequence of colors deter-
mined by a color palette associated with the letters of
the alphabet. It appears in different contexts: the intro-
duction to the website, the newsletter, social networks,
videos, flyers and posters.15
752I worked with Romain to create a tool for the
school so they could create visual content on their own
that would nevertheless remain consistent with their
brand identity. It was a logical approach to have them
to print their own marketing materials since there were20
just too many flyers for them to outsource each time. In
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Tancrède Ottiger A.P 301
.P
TANCRÈDE OTTIGER
addition, this enabled the client to truly appropriate the
design themselves.
Rob van Leijsen Did this tool have any unexpected
outcomes or surprises?
754 T.O. Yes. You are right to bring that up. Surprises are 5
a big part of this working method. Just recently, when
we were working on some animation for Les Teintureries,
syntax errors in the code generated an interesting ac-
cident. The resulting bug has the potential to provide
a lead for further visual explorations. I think you can 10
always bounce back from unexpected discoveries. The
unexpected errors that the tool can potentially produce
are often a rich source of inspiration.
D.C. How did you develop the project with Romain
Cazier? What is your work relationship like? Were there 15
any tensions?
756 T.O. As in every collaboration, it depends on the peo-
ple working together. Romain and I have been friends
since the beginning of our studies. In addition to my
graphic design education, I had a strong interest in cod- 20
ing, and vice versa for Romain, who has always been
interested in graphic design. We helped each other a lot
at ECAL. This created a context in which mutual trust
was established and a real exchange of ideas could take
place, as it did during the project for Les Teintureries. 25
Since Romain has a strong sensibility for grids and ty-
pography, he was willing to spend a lot of time searching
for technical solutions to achieve the visual system we im-
plemented. There is no hierarchy between us, we worked
together seamlessly on the project from the start. 30
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.P 302 Part A: Interview P
INTERVIEWS
A
R.v.L. Was the client aware of this collaboration?
758T.O. Of course. When I work with a developer, I
bring them in at the very beginning to see whether they
share my graphical vision, even if it may not be very
clear at that stage, to see how we can experiment within5
the context of the project. However, developers don’t
automatically join me in client meetings since, with each
project, I first have to work on an extensive visual identity
trajectory that I have to work out on my own. Then,
as soon as we enter the digital phase, the developer I’m10
working with is integrated into the project and connects
with the client.
D.C. Is the online tool that your client uses to

760T.O. It was made with <paper.js><G>64, 41 , a <JavaScript>15

students and teachers, with profile sheets for everyone
that includes contact information and a biography. Their
newsletter is also generated and managed from this plat-20
form. It was quite a gamble because we advised them to
drop all the existing software they were using for internal
communications and centralize it all. The website even
manages the teaching staff’s working hours.
761It was a big proposition for us, and the client25
was positive at first, but they wanted to move slowly
with implementation to see how it could work out. As
a result, we embarked on a few months of tryouts and

R.v.L. Could you comment on another project we
would like to know more about, the wayfinding system
for Les Ateliers 5
de Renens?
763 T.O. I worked on this project before Les Teintureries.
It was the first really big project Romain and I worked on
together. The project began as a competition to create
the identity of Les Ateliers, which are working spaces 10
managed by the city of Renens that are rented out. The
competition brief only mentioned the creation of a logo
and its placement on the building. When my proposition
was selected, I also proposed flags for the exterior, but
otherwise there was a really basic signage system that 15
was already in place.
Fig.45
Visual identity, Les Ateliers
de Renens, Tancrède Ottiger,
2018.
764 So, after this initial identity phase, the client
asked that we also design a wayfinding system. It was an
old building in which people could easily get lost. Also,
it had a total of seventy occupants with a constant and 20
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.P 304 Part A: Interview P
INTERVIEWS
A
rapid turnover. Some stayed just two months, others for
years. Their initial plan was to just place the usual letter-
ing in the hallways and on doors to do the job. However,
once I finished my study of the building, I informed them
that the project was far more complex than they had5
initially thought, and that it would cost much more.
I made a very detailed quote of what we would need and
how much it would cost. My main contact from the city
of Renens trusted me and defended my proposal. They
had other proposals from agencies who specialized in sig-10
nage systems, but they went with our idea. Romain and
I proposed to create a signage system that comprised dig-
ital and physical components. The physical part would
remain static and unchanged. It would be constituted of
frames made by Adrien Rovero, an industrial designer15
from Lausanne. It was based upon three basic shapes:
a circle to indicate a workshop or office, a triangle to
provide directions, and a rectangle which identifies the
occupant of a space. We insert colored paper into the
rectangles—it’s cheap, but has a strong graphic impact20
and is easily modified when tenants changed. The dig-
ital component is a completely customized map of the
building which displays on screens in the building as well
as on the website. Visitors can either use the available
touchscreens to navigate or use their smartphones to25
make their way through the building.
765The website hosts the map, along with a back-
end tool which the owners of Les Ateliers can use to
manage the building. For instance, they can access a list
of all the tenants in each attributed space. When new30
tenants sign their contract and enter the space, they also
receive access to this online platform, which they can
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Tancrède Ottiger A.P 305
.P
TANCRÈDE OTTIGER
use to enter and manage their personal information. The
data includes the name and profession of the tenant, as
well as other practical information, like their opening
hours. This is then printed and placed in the door frame
and transferred to the website as well. When tenants are 5
required to use the signage system of the building, they
simultaneously provide information for the website at
the same time. That was the main idea behind
this system.
766 Since so many people were going to use this 10
website, we created a complete guideline tutorial on
Vimeo which is directly accessible from their website. At
first we thought the system and tutorial would be clear
enough to enable people to create their own accounts,
but I still get emails from new tenants asking me 15
for help.
R.v.L. So, you still have to provide technical support?
768 T.O. Yes, but I don’t yet know why it doesn’t work
for some people. It is amazing to see how some people
can use your tools in unpredictable ways. 20
D.C. What type of coding expertise do you have

770 T.O. My knowledge is rather artisanal, because I’m a

sense of fun when I began playing with <HTML> <G>37and 30
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

<CSS><G>17 . You could get results rapidly. I think I came

it all by yourself. However, the fact that I know how

TANCRÈDE OTTIGER
coding languages work enables me to interact with the
developers I work with at a far deeper level.
772B.A 28 †
gGraphic · Design 3
gGraphic · Design · Projects
gDesign · Projects
gWork · with · People 10
B.A
All of my graphic design projects began with
my questioning the technical boundaries of what the
developer could accomplish. Perhaps a part of our job 5
is also to encourage your collaborator to embark on a
quest with you. Also, it must be said that programming
is a veritable black hole in terms of time. It is impossible
to get paid for the actual hours required for most jobs.
So it’s essential to work with people that share your 10
commitment and feel the same way, otherwise I wouldn’t
be comfortable.
D.C. How do you think creative coding affects or
alters your visual language, and how do you think it will
impact the field of graphic design in the near future? 15
774 T.O. Well, for me programming certainly affected the
way I work since, as I said, I collaborate with a developer
on most of my projects.
775 Code-generated design is visible to people within
our profession, but I don’t think the average person on 20
the street perceives it. Also, code-based design doesn’t
have to have an esthetic. The tool itself is not an esthetic
per se, but rather a way of achieving organic identities
or importing huge amounts of data. Tools facilitate a
modular thinking process that was not possible before. 25
That, in my view, is the most interesting aspect of code-
based design.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.P 308 Part A: Interview P
INTERVIEWS
A
D.C. How do you monetize creative coding? Are
clients paying the correct amounts for these services?
777 ‡ B.A 25g
Client · Meetings 16
gGraphic · Design 13
gWilling · to · Invest 6
gDesign · Work 11
gClients 16
gBit · of · Coding 14
B.A
T.O. When I make an offer, I always integrate the
development from the outset. I usually draft an initial
budget and then I meet with the client to see if they are5
willing to invest that amount. Romain and I have devel-
oped templates for quotes over the years that include
many parameters because our projects were becoming
so complex that a basic offer was not sufficient anymore.
The more precise you are at the beginning, the more10
time you can spend on the design work once the project
is up and running. Usually the offers are pretty balanced
between the development and graphic design aspects.
The fact that I have done quite a bit of coding puts me
in the position of being able to handle all the project15
management and client meetings.
778When a client asks a specific question about
the feasibility of a web function, I can usually respond
directly. With some jobs, the project management can
become a massive task. This extra work usually balances20
out the hours between development and design.
R.v.L. Have you noticed that projects that seemed to
purely concern graphic design initially could slowly
evolve into ones that incorporate a large development
component?25
780T.O. Yes, that does happen. Several programmers
I work with all say that there is a growing demand for
development and coding skills in this area. Personally, as
a graphic designer, this is not a problem for me, since it
inspires my desire to learn more and constantly upgrade30
my skillset. I’m still totally happy to do type design and
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Tancrède Ottiger A.P 309
.P
TANCRÈDE OTTIGER
book design and so on. I don’t want to stop that practice,
but I do love working on digital projects.
D.C. Do you think that graphic designers will be
replaced by technology in the future? What would be
the role of the designer if a substantial part of the 5
job is automated?
782B.C 96 †
gGraphic · Design 25
gDesign · Process 18
gThink · that · Graphic · Designers
gDon · t · Think
B.C
T.O. As a designer, automation interests me in two
ways. The first is modularity, the ability to go further
with code, beyond what static design methods can offer.
Secondly, the fact that portions of the design process 10
that are not the most interesting can be taken over by
technology, enabling the designer to focus on the more im-
portant decisions. Still, I don’t think that computers can
create fully automated posters without the involvement
of a designer. Technically speaking it could be done, but 15
the main difference resides in the fact that the computer
is not capable of playing off the accidents and benefiting
from them. Automation in design processes needs to be
scripted, and scripts need to be written. Someone, a
human, has to write the script. Additionally, artificial 20
intelligence, at least the little I know about it, is only
good at one thing at a time, and, when you are designing
a poster, a book, or whatever, you need a lot more than
one single skill. Consequently, I don’t think that graphic
designers are out of the game yet. 25
D.C. Tancrède, thank you so much for spending
this time with us.
784 T.O. Thank you.

I · am · a · creative, · enthusiastic · and ·independent · minded · teacher · who · feels · passionately · about · art, ·
design · and · creativity · in · education. · I · was · lucky · enough · to · win · a ·place · on · the · BBC · Future's · Made · By · Future · programme · and · so ·
got · to · work · with · some · great · minds, · exploring · interesting ·ideas · about · 21st · century · education. · I · have · had · the · privilege · of ·
being · involved · in · setting · up · some · innovative · creative · learning ·projects · with · the · likes · of: · National · Trust, · Arts · Council, · Creative ·
Learning · Organisation · and · many · more. · I · believe · in · the · power · of ·creativity · at · all · levels · and · want · to · support · teachers, · leaders ·
and · parents · in · their · efforts · to · provide · inspiring · and · creative ·learning · opportunities · for · young · people.
TED-Ed · is · an · award-winning, · non-profit · partnership · with · the · goal ·of · dramatically · improving · academic · and · social · achievement ·
worldwide · by · bringing · the · transformational · power · of ·technology · to · education. · Through · the · power · of · TED-Ed ·
tools, · millions · of · teachers, · students · and · parents · in · millions · of ·classrooms · from · all · walks · of · life · are · taking · a · fresh, · modern ·
approach · to · learning · that · is · bringing · results · and · excitement · to ·students · everywhere. · Educators · from · around · the · world · are ·
creating · and · sharing · their · ideas · at · TED-Ed.org · – · all · part · of · a ·common · movement · making · great · learning · achievable · for · everyone.
In · 2011, · in · partnership · with · the · BBC, · TED-Ed · unveiled · their · original ·series, · "Made · by · Future." · Using · the · same · platform, · TED-Ed · also ·
introduced · new · resources · and · expanded · its · focus · to ·address · today's · pressing · issues · at · the · interface · of · technology ·
and · education. · In · the · first · quarter · of · 2012, · as · a · consequence · of ·this · evolution, · TED-Ed · was · renamed · "TED-Ed · Out · of · School ·
Hour" · and · began · publishing · regularly · online, · rather · than · once · a ·month...
My · name · is · Emilie · Pillet · and · I · teach · design · and · technology · at ·St · Andrew's · Primary · School · in · Glasgow. · I · hold · a · First · Diploma ·
in · Design · and · Technology · from · Strathclyde · and · have · also ·studied · Fine · Art · and · Graphic · Design · at · Glasgow · School · of ·
Art. · I · started · teaching · five · years · ago · and · have · experience ·teaching · GCSE · Art · to · Year · 7–8 · classes · as · well · as · primary. · I · am ·
passionate · about · designing · teaching · and · learning · resources · and ·enjoy · collaborating · with · colleagues · and · learning · from · them.
Are · there · any · key · issues · which · concern · educators · about · this?One · of · the · things · that · worries · me · is · in · the · BBC · report. · The · idea ·
of · being · able · to'self-assess'. · The · emphasis · on · testing, · on · a · drive ·to · provide · an · answer · rather · than · exploring · an · area.
What · is · being · done · to · counter · these · concerns?In · the · current · media · climate · we · need · to · give · as · much · power ·
to · students · as · possible. · It · is · a · really · good · step · when ·students · are · given · opportunities · to · challenge · what · the · teachers ·
ask · them · to · do · and · explore · areas · of · debate. · This · is · very ·much · a · part · of · my · teaching · style · and · I · am · always · working · to ·
create · new · learning · opportunities · for · students · to · explore · their ·creative · learning.

<Emilie Pillet>66 is a Swiss graphic designer who currently lives

```
in Graphic Design from ECAL (École Cantonale d’Art de Lausanne).
```

E.P. I have a book which covers the basics, but the

you build on something that already exists, you have

an inspiration for my web design. I think that there are
so many things you can learn from the narration and10
hierarchy of print that you can translate into your work.

801E.P. There are definitely some methods that I try to

you try something one way, try it the opposite way as

job, you are in a different context where there are bigger
constraints and less time. Maybe that’s one thing ECAL20
taught us well: to be able to quickly adapt to a context.

807E.P. I think it depends on what type of website you5
are creating. When I design a website with Romain, the
discussion with the client is more on a graphic design
level—i.e. choosing this font or that column width—rather
than the back-end structure of the website or the interac-
tions. Clients don’t really go into that. They do not truly10
understand the way code works. So they gauge the price
accordingly because they pay for a service that seems
like a huge amount of work to them. It doesn’t sound
nice, but I guess that’s the reality.

that clients are willing to pay for a well-coded website,

of examples of coding and programming by graphic
designers in the public domain or on social media?
809E.P. When it comes to collaborating with coders,20
obviously yes, but I don’t think it’s that common yet to

Maybe in the future it will become more common as the
barriers diminish.
R.v.L. Is it your wish to start your own practice25

on · a · website · set · up · as · a · public · resource · for · software ·developers, · his · work · has · been · the · subject · of · intense · scrutiny ·
on · both · sides · of · the · Internet. · As · a · professional · software ·designer, · Casey · Reas · designs · tools · and · techniques · for · software ·
development. · But · on · the · web-based · project · management ·platform · called · Asana, · he · writes · and · maintains · the · design ·
guidelines · for · projects. · The · latter · is · a · job · that · requires · intense ·focus · and · rigorous · attention · to · detail.
Reas · holds · two · professional · degrees: · a · B.S. · in · computer ·science · from · Cal · Poly · Pomona · and · an · M.S. · in · computer · science ·
from · San · José · State · University. · Over · the · course · of · his · career, ·he's · designed · computer · apps, · worked · on · the · systems ·
architecture · for · a · couple · of · dot-coms · and · most · recently ·helped · launch · Google+ · into · the · world. · He's · worked · with ·
startups · and · large · corporations · — · and · he · still · does.The · Casey · Reas · Experience
In · fact, · Reas · left · the · Googleplex · and · moved · to · Amsterdam ·for · a · while · last · year · to · work · on · the · new · version · of · Gmail. ·
And · as · you'd · expect · from · a · former · Cal · Poly · Pomona ·computer · science · major, · he's · well · versed · in · all · things · code.

```
go · to · Reas' · homepage, · sign · up · for · an · account, · and · then · simply ·open · a · project · in · G+. · (That's · a · lot · of · text · to · write, · and · a ·
```

bunch · of · clicking · to · do · as · well. · I've · provided · a · link · to · G+ ·right · at · the · top · of · this · article.

```
well-paying · company · fail · at · so · fundamental · a · task, · but · then · again ·you'd · think · wrong) · is · so · that · everyone · can · have · a · common ·
```

experience · when · working · on · any · Google · project. · Reas' · process ·starts · with · his · own · research, · then · moves · through · a · series · of ·
drafts. · Reusing · a · lot · of · code · that's · been · written · before ·means · less · time · spent · writing · new · stuff.

A.Q 320 Part A: Interview Q
INTERVIEWS
A.R
CASEY
REAS
Casey
Reas
Interviewed by
Demian Conrad
and
Rob van Leijsen
```
<Casey Reas>67 (aka Casey Edwin Barker
```
```
Reas, b. 1972, Troy, Ohio) lives and works
```
in Los Angeles. He holds a Masters degree from
the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in
Media Arts and Sciences and a Bachelor's degree
from the College of Design, Architecture, Art,
and Planning at the University of Cincinnati.
In 2001, Reas created Processing, an open-source
programming language and environment for
the visual arts, in collaboration with Ben Fry.
His software, prints, and installations have been
featured in numerous solo and group exhibitions
at museums and galleries in the United States,
Europe, and Asia. Reas’ work ranges from small
works on paper to urban-scale installations, and
he balances solo work in the studio with collabora-
tions with architects and musicians. His work is
also part of both private and public collections,
including the Centre Georges Pompidou in Paris,
and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art.
Reas is currently a professor at the University of
California, Los Angeles.
Interview with Casey Reas A.R 321
Demian Conrad Hi Casey, thanks for participating in
this project. To begin with, we’d like to know how you
started using creative coding in your design practice.
816 Casey Reas Well, Ben Fry, my partner and co-creator
of the <Processing> <G>68project, and I both had degrees 5
in graphic design. I had studied at the University of
```
Cincinnati (Ohio), and he had studied at Carnegie Mel-
```
```
lon University (Pennsylvania). I attended design school
```
in the early to mid-1990s, which was a time of change.
We were moving away from a hand-based, craft system to 10
using software. I learned a lot of the original techniques
of drawing type by hand, and how to develop these little
systems manually. However, we were also learning to use
the really early versions of some of the design softwares
that have now become the standard, such as <Illustra- 15
tor> <G>4, 6and <Photoshop>. Consequently, I would say that
I had a bit of a hybrid education that combined the more
traditional manual methods and software techniques.
817B.D 120 †
gLearn · Code 27
gVisual · Arts 4
gStarted · to · Think 25
B.D
When I graduated, the World Wide Web was
becoming an international phenomenon. In 1995, I began 20
applying what I had learned about publication design and
typography to the Web. It was the first time I felt I was
working in a way that felt compelling—writing <HTML> <G>37
and <CSS> <G>17—and for me this was the bridge. Once
I started to think about onscreen type and typographic 25
systems, I decided I wanted to do more of that, and that
was when I began to learn code. I really hadn’t done
that before. At the time, designers who were coding were
generally using programs like <Macromedia Director> <G>51.
It was like a multimedia system, where you would bring 30
in media elements, like images, and then apply code to
those. Ben and I were both interested in working more
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.R 322 Part A: Interview R
INTERVIEWS
A
directly with code as a medium. That led to our working
with languages like <C++><G>16 and <Java><G>40 , which had
remained primarily confined to computer science, and
were not adapted for the visual arts.
818 ‡ B.D 132g
Design · Education 6
gArtists · and · Designers 10
B.D
The concept behind <Processing><G>68 was to adapt5
design education and design ideas to a method of thinking
and making using computer science, to create a coding
system we felt was more approachable and accessible for
people who are primarily visual. We had visual artists
and designers, and even, to a lesser degree, architects, in10
mind as potential users.
819 ‡ B.D 120g
Learn · Code 12
gLearn · to · Code 16
B.D
When you learn coding, you need to learn it
within a domain, and that domain traditionally consists
of working with text and mathematics. Consequently,
the idea of <Processing><G>68 was to change the domain in15
which you learn to code, and let you explore code within
a visual framework.
820 ‡ B.D 132g
Design · Education 18
B.D
The design education that I received in Cincin-
nati in the 1990s was very much rooted in the Bauhaus,
and what had happened in Basel in the 1970s, as well as20
at Yale, which had also been heavily influenced by Basel.
My teachers had been trained in the same manner.
821 ‡ B.B 86g
Sorts · of · Things 13
gDesign · Education 3
gLearn · to · Code
gWanted · to · Expand · and · Extend 12
gCoding · Environment 5
gProcessing · Project 14

gProcessing · Project · Grew
gWorking · with · Processing
gStarted · Working · with · Processing
B.B
When we started working with <Processing><G>68 ,
I was influenced by these teaching methods as well as the
design books of the 1960s and 1970s. We tried to think25
<GG>68 WHAT·ARE · < VISUAL·ARTS >?
The · answers · they · often · give · – · that ·visual · arts · mean · any · creative · work · involving · a · medium · of · human ·
sight, · or · anything · involving · human · eye · and · hand · – · are · not ·exactly · on · target · for · the · moderns · who · consider · the · arts ·
today.But · there · are · some · broad · principles: · Any · visual · art · – · painting, ·
sculpture, · architecture, · photography, · film, · whatever · – · must · be · a ·representation, · an · imitation · or · model · of · someone · or ·
something · of · the · world. · It · must · look · and · feel · like · the · world ·and · be · a · representation · of · a · reality.
And · in · another · respect, · the · broad · definition · is · also · useful · in ·the · definition · of · visual · art · and · the · arts · as · modern · peoples · use ·
the · words: · It's · something · that's · done · by · someone · else · – ·and · with · our · eyes · – · not · something · that's · done · with · our ·
hands.

Interview with Casey Reas A.R 323
.R
CASEY REAS
about a way of learning to code where you could practice
and study following those foundational traditional design
education techniques. The <Processing> <G>68project grew
directly out of a coding initiative written by John Maeda
called Design By Numbers. It was a coding environment 5
that was very approachable for visual designers, but it
had a lot of constraints. You could only make programs
that were 100×100 pixels, and black and white was
the only option. Ben and I had assisted John with that
project, we had taught workshops with it, and we were 10
very enthusiastic about its potential, but we wanted to
expand and extend it, in order to be able to work with
color, in full screen, and all those sorts of things. That
```
was essentially the inception of the <Processing> <G>68project;
```
it was extending everything we thought was amazing 15
about Design By Numbers, but bringing it more into a
complete, visual environment.
Rob van Leijsen Can you tell us a bit more about the
environment and the situation in which you and Ben
developed this platform? Since you were already graphic 20
design graduates at that point, had the educational
environment provided an incentive to build this or not?
823 C.R. I think we both graduated around 1996 and
worked as designers for a number of years, before going
to graduate school. I worked in New York at the time, 25
at a small company doing work on the Web, and Ben
worked in California. I think he might have worked for
Netscape for a while, then we both met for the first
time at the <MIT Media Lab> <G>48. Ben started there in
1998, and I started in 1999. We both went there to 30
study with John Maeda, who was a professor there,
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.R 324 Part A: Interview R
INTERVIEWS
A
and had founded a research group called the Aesthetics
and Computation Group [ACG]. The Aesthetics and
Computation Group occupied the space in the Media
Lab that had previously been used by Muriel Cooper’s
Visual Language Workshop, before her untimely death,5
and also built on a lot of its ideas. Consequently, it was
imbued with the feeling of carrying on some of that legacy
and a sense of exploration. It was there that Ben and
I started working together with John Maeda on Design
By Numbers.10

It was also the birthplace of <Processing><G>68 .
I think we made our initial sketches in June of 2001,
which was at the very end of my time there, but Ben
continued on as a PhD student during <Processing><G>68 ’s
early years. When I graduated, I moved to Northern Italy15
and began teaching at the Interaction Design Institute
in Ivrea, north of Turin. I also started doing workshops
around Europe, while Ben was still at MIT. We devel-
oped the software together over the course of many years,
and released the first official version in 2005, I think, but20
it had been released informally as of 2001 through a lot
of workshops, and to anyone who asked us.
825I would say the <Processing><G>68 project grew abso-
lutely and directly out of the culture of the <MIT Media
Lab><G>48 , and then the <community>69 around <Process-25
ing><G>68 and the people using it just continued to expand
from there.

example, you typically needed twenty to forty lines of

827 A really important early idea of the project was
sketching with code. In addition, as a designer, when
you start working, you often do so without knowing 10
the destination. You do research, and develop ideas,
and you work out ways to get you where you want to
be. We wanted that process to be possible with code.
The original idea behind the <Processing> <G>68language was
to make it more accessible for sketching, and to think 15
through writing small sketches in code.

A.R 326 Part A: Interview R
INTERVIEWS
A
I was working in Boston as an intern at a design studio,
and I attended a design symposium at the Media Lab,
probably in 1993. It was there that I saw David Small
and Yin Yin Wong present demos that had been created
in the Visual Language Workshop.5
831That was my lightning moment, when I saw
what I wanted my future to be. I saw what I was doing
in design, what I knew about design, and the direction in
which I wanted to be moving, so my original intent was
to apply to study with Muriel Cooper. Unfortunately,10
she passed away before I completed my undergraduate
work, but then I had the luck of meeting John Maeda,
and I was able to study with him.
832The Lab was not a large place. There’s a line
of continuity from when it was first originated as the15
Architecture Machine Group, before becoming the Media
Lab in 1985, and a lot of people who knew and loved
Muriel Cooper were still at the lab at that time. I think
that her legacy lived on through the individuals that she
had worked with and had mentored.20
R.v.L. Earlier on, you mentioned the workshops you
did with the Media Lab, but also abroad. Did you adapt
your teaching methods between the two? How important
was the feedback of your students in the development
of the software?25
834 ‡ B.B 68g
Developed · the · Software 27
B.B
C.R. It was really important. We developed the soft-
ware through teaching workshops and we always made
adjustments each time we held one. Those modifications
we made to the software were very important. I think
the subsequent adaptations in the way we taught were30
even more critical, namely in the method we used and
the order in which we presented things.

motion. For us, that was really just the basics, it was
a technique that we were excited about, and had been 5
using for such a long time, so we didn’t realize that there

for students from design backgrounds, mathematics is

837For me, it’s always about the digital media that’s
produced and created. The primary objective is not the

ingly, the curriculum changed rapidly over the first few

R.v.L. So, right from the start, the open-source
platform was really a key element in the development of
the software?
839 ‡ B.B 80g
Important · Thing 20
gWanted · to · Expand · and · Extend 12
gArtists · and · Designers 15
gSource · Code 25
B.B
C.R. Yes, one of the important early decisions was
to make what we call “libraries.” Ben and I realized10
that we were in a bottleneck and getting in the way of
how people wanted to expand and extend <Processing><G>68 .
Ben and I primarily focused on visual media. We don’t
really have an expertise in audio or other areas of media
in which other artists and designers like to work. So,15
we developed a library system that allowed people to
extend <Processing><G>68 in their own way. Now, there’s well
over 100 libraries which have extended <Processing><G>68
into other domains in which people have expertise. An
important thing about the library system is that people20
can create them in their entirety, author and maintain
them, then give them back to the community. It’s a way
of getting engaged, without needing to delve into the
complete source code. They are isolated pieces of source
code that individuals can develop and share with other25
people. The libraries, I think, have been the primary way
in which people contribute to the community, and also
the means by which the project has expanded beyond
its roots.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
