.R
CASEY REAS
R.v.L. Would you call it a kind of co-authorship?
841 C.R. Well, I think it’s a co-authorship in terms of the
larger frame of the project. I think the library authors
really are the autonomous or complete authors of those
contributions of theirs. 5
D.C. You mentioned in an interview that the project
was half engineering, and half community building.
Those are two areas of expertise that a designer doesn’t
learn in school, and that they currently have to pick
up by themselves. How did you acquire this expertise, 10
and how did you develop it? What was the most
difficult aspect of this for you as a designer?
843 C.R. I think one thing that’s important to clarify is
that Ben Fry, my collaborator, is the primary software
engineer of the project. He is better at those things than 15
I am. He actually was, I think, a double major, or a
computer science minor, while I really didn’t have any
technical expertise in coding until I started learning, after
I earned my undergraduate degree.
844 However, I think we developed a lot of our skills 20
and knowledge by learning as we went along. In terms of
community building, we put a lot of energy into that, but
I don’t think that we were necessarily skilled at it. People
had a desire to collaborate, and to come together and
share knowledge. We put some really minimal resources 25
in place, and people built on that with a lot of energy.
I think the original forum was a place where people truly
gathered, before Facebook, Twitter and such.
845B.B 79 †
gOpen-source · Projects 29
B.B
One idea behind being an open-source project
was that people really shared. Someone would ask ques- 30

and write some code. That’s still happening on the forum,

A.R 330 Part A: Interview R
INTERVIEWS
A
but at that time, I would say it was a small and pretty

846As the <Processing><G>68 project expanded and be-
came more international, that single close-knit commu-
nity spread out. Now, there are a lot of more localized5
communities being formed and developed. We started
the first <Processing><G>68 Community Days a few years ago,
and the second year, we made it international. There
were almost 100 events all around the world, across ev-
ery continent. Local organizers made their own days for10
their own communities. I would say that the period from
around 2002 to 2006 was one when it felt almost like
a unified international community created around the

thing different.15

Another thing that’s been really unexpected
with <Processing><G>68 is that it was originally written as
an university-level and art school-level introduction to
coding for artists and designers, but lately it’s being used
more and more in high schools and middle schools, at20
least in the United States. It’s oftentimes used in other
areas as well, like in a mathematics class, for making
visuals for math, or in a physics class, for small physics
demos. So <Processing><G>68 has found its way into areas
beyond our original expectations.25

When the project first began in 2001, it was
really heavily integrated into the World Wide Web, and
<Java><G>40 was able to play in all the browsers through
<Java><G>40 applets. You would basically just be working in
the <Processing><G>68 editor, you would click export, and30
it would then build a <Java><G>40, 37 applet and an <HTML>
file. You could put that on any server, and then display
your work through the Web. That was the aspect of it
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

.R
CASEY REAS
that allowed us to grow, and for other people to find

communicate through the forums. I think, to a large
extent, that’s a thing of the past. Still, it was the way
the project found a community, and an audience in the
beginning. Of course, the decline of <Java> <G>40applets on

that’s why it’s a separate and very energetic community.

see every kind of visual style.10

Even if somebody wants to move into typography and

In that sense, the class is very much a general
introduction, and it has become a lot less technical over 10
the years. It includes less coding, but encourages the
students to be original and inventive with the small
amount of coding that they do learn. The class is very
media-focused, so our students have a wide range of aims
and aspirations. Some students are interested in video, 15
animation, photography, typography, etc., so the class
I teach on coding touches upon all of those subjects over
the course of the term. For instance, we do a project that
focuses on looping photographic images, almost like an
old zoetrope, but integrating interactivity. We also do a 20
project that allows them to load different typefaces and
set them in a way that involves interactivity.

858I know all the students can do it, they’re all

and make possible when used as a medium for design.
It was actually a goal of mine to show what was possi-
ble. When I was at design school, a program for digital

cause the graphic design professors disdained computers.5
However, I think that the program only existed for a

C.R. I think that the idea of students having exposure15
to code, and an opportunity to explore it is exciting. One
thing I’ve noticed is that sometimes, the students who
don’t expect to enjoy it are paradoxically the ones who
actually create the best work. Sometimes it can just open
up a range of possibilities that weren’t there before they20
had the opportunity to work with code. Another way I
always frame it, is that, even if you’re not going to be a
coder, even if it’s not going to be essential to your work
as a designer, having some degree of literacy in this area
facilitates your collaborations with other people.25
868I think the pragmatic truth is that, as a designer,
you’re often working in teams, or working on projects
```
for the Web; with UI and UX, you’re often working with
```
people who are implementing that, and this enables you
to have an understanding of what’s involved in moving30
your design into a piece of software for an app or for a
website. The degree of literacy acquired from having some
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

CASEY REAS
exposure to code can be really helpful in collaborations.

gWorking · with · Code

B.C
As design moves onto the Web more and more,
and continues to evolve within a space of interfaces, ap- 5
plications and software, it becomes essential for designers
to work with code, to gain some exposure and a level of
introduction to coding, along with the skills necessary
to work with those areas of design.
R.v.L. Designers today who are working 10
independently, doing web-based work, are very aware
of their limits, the need for collaboration, and
understand what programming has to offer them.
You can’t make it without any collaboration, and even
if you specialize in web projects, you cannot substitute 15
the extent of a professional programmer’s expertise.
871 C.R. Everybody needs help with coding, from time
to time, even the most advanced coders. The more you
learn about coding, the more you get a sense of where
your own personal boundaries might be, and when it’s 20
worthwhile to spend months learning something new, or
when it’s better to find the right person to commission
to handle that. I still need assistance with my own work.
Sometimes I’ll hire somebody to write a shader, for
example, because shader coding is not something that’s 25
a part of my expertise.

<Processing><G>68 was created. I think it can be difficult to

My entire design education basically consisted of10

lenge, at least for me, but a lot of these high-level visual
environments in animation also have scripting languages
embedded in them, which is something that’s always
been a part of design tools.

You take an environment like <Processing> <G>68,
which is just a <text editor>71, versus a really exten-
sive interface that has scripting capabilities. I think this
goes all the way back to the precursors of multimedia
environments, to things like Hypercard, which were envi- 5
```
ronments where you would script on the elements; this,
```
I believe, led to <Macromedia Director> <G>51, which was a
whole generation’s primary environment for code work.
In my view, the contemporary version of that is the
<Unity> <G>81software, largely used within gaming programs. 10
That’s one area, another is node-based coding platforms,
things like Max, and there’s a lot of people who start
teaching code with those kinds of node-based flow con-
trol programs. Then there’s the paradigm represented
by <Processing> <G>68’s pure text code. 15

<Jeroen Barendse>74’s vast career includes
```
work in interactive design (UI/UX), data
```
visualization, information systems, software tools,
interactive installations, media architecture,
immersive experiences, interface design, visual
identities, generative video, creative coding,
exhibition design, graphic design systems, hybrid
spaces and platforms, machine learning and AI,
code and design workshops, art direction, and film
production. Barendse is a founding partner of
```
LUST (est. 1996), a group which, in 2010, went on
```
to spawn LUSTlab, a research lab for emerging
technologies. In 2014, LUST had the solo exhibi-
tion Type/Dynamics at the Stedelijk Museum in
Amsterdam. In 2017, Barendse went on to co-
found RNDR at The Hague, bringing together a
group of computer scientists, designers, art
directors and developers around a core project,
OpenRNDR, an open-source framework for
creative coding that was awarded the Dutch
Design Award in 2019. Since 2001, Barendse has
also been a professor at the Academy of Arts,
Arnhem, teaching Graphic Design, Typography
and Interactive Design. He also leads workshops
and has lectured all over the world. His publi-
cations include an atlas on the cartography of the
```
North Sea, Form+Code (with Casey Reas and
```
```
Chandler McWilliams), which has been translated
```
into five languages, as well as several research
papers in the field of data visualization. Barendse
was the 2017 recipient of the Piet Zwart Prize
Design Oeuvre Award.

Interview with Jeroen Barendse A.S 345
Demian Conrad Hi Jeroen, thanks for talking to
us today. Could you tell us more about your first steps
in creative coding?
886B.C 110 †
gYears · Ago 16

B.C
Jeroen Barendse Basically, I was trained as a graphic
designer, in the early 1990s, when the Internet had just 5
started up. I didn’t study media design or coding in
school since there were no such programs at the time. As
soon as I set up LUST, my first studio, we immediately
began exploring the possibilities of new media, and I’ve
been interested in developing that ever since. We’ve been 10
developing generative identities since the early 2000s. Of
course, it was way too early so they were never imple-
mented—that’s the story of our life. We were before our
time with a lot of things, but now, over the last couple
of years, I’m seeing ideas that we had developed twenty 15
years ago becoming relatively common practice. Because
we were still very much grounded in graphic design, with
only a few coders in the team, we began to progressively
integrate media and programming in our work as of 2010,
with the founding of LUSTlab, an internal research lab. 20
We did a lot of projects that explored new ideas of inter-
action, using coding to create forms or sound and data
to inform interaction. A lot came out of it, including
things we still work on every day.
887B.B 66 †
gOpen-source · Software 2
gCreative · Coding 26
gYears · Ago 5
gBegan · Developing 25
B.B
We began developing our open-source framework 25
```
for creative coding, OpenRNDR (which was called RNDR
```
```
at the time), because we could not find the right tools to
```
enable us to make what we wanted to at the time. We
had always really admired what Casey Reas and Ben Fry
had done with <Processing> <G>68, especially the community 30
they created around the software. Even back during the

after LUST folded, the first thing we decided was to take
that idea further. About two years ago, we finally open-5
sourced it. Of course, you can put it on <GitHub><G>31 and
say it’s open source, but there’s a lot more to it, such as
creating detailed <documentation>75. Documentation is

to evolve. In order to keep this manageable, we also10

<documentation>75, so we didn’t have to do all that

B.C
We’re really trying to think more about provid-
ing tools that somebody can use to create stuff. I still 5
find it a very interesting space to be in and to explore
further, because we’re developing projects where we’re
not trying to achieve a specific end product. I find it
quite refreshing, it opens up a lot of possibilities. I’m also
teaching interaction design at Arnhem University of the 10
Arts and I see that more and more students are thinking
that way as well. A lot of them are not really thinking
about the final result, but more about the process or
the approach.
890 Of course, it also has a downside. For example, 15
I like good typography and there are no typography
classes in the interaction design section. Usually the
typography is pretty crappy, but you can’t have every-
thing. On the other hand, we are trying to develop a
new method for integrating coding or programming with 20
design. It’s a new way of approaching code and design.
We’re trying to merge the two sides of the brain, the
left and the right, and evolve a new sort of methodology.
I really think that, in the Netherlands, creative coding
is at last becoming an integral part of design. I see lots 25
of developments in graphic design courses here, where
coding is now an integral part of the curriculum as early
as the first year. I think it’s getting bigger and bigger.
891B.B 91 †
gMachine · Learning 31
B.B
We also have artificial intelligence and machine
learning classes, and we’ve just been asked to join a 30
project in which machine learning can become part of
the curriculum. We’re thinking about how it can be
incorporated within design-related practice.

print. The constraints of the project were such that it
was impossible to do it manually in the few days we had
from when we received the 1,500-page text to when we 20
had to send the file to the printer. We simply had to
automatize it, at the cost of a certain level of quality of
the result. For instance, we couldn’t get optimal hyphen-
ation. Still, you couldn’t tell the book was generated, so
I’m confident about working that way. You can do a lot 25
with programming, but if fine typographic work is really

J.B. We couldn’t find tools that allowed us to do the
work that we wanted. There was <Processing> <G>68, which 10
at the time was too unstable for us and also not flexible
enough, and then you had openFrameworks, which could
do a lot, but was too complex for the way we work. Like
the philosophy behind creative coding, we wanted a bit
more flexibility as well. This led to the idea of creating 15
something ourselves. We started with something very
basic, then added new functions with each project as
needed. We are still doing that. For example, last year we
did a big project centered around video processing, which
was still somewhat undeveloped on the platform. Now 20
all of the functionalities we developed on that project
are part of OpenRNDR, such as being able to skip to a
specific frame in an extended video.

906 J.B. Basically, we ended up paying for 99% of Open-
RNDR’s development. However, when it came to docu-
mentation, we were able to get grants from the creative
industries funds in The Netherlands. This enabled us
to dedicate time to specific things. As for the rest, we 30
looked into making it sort of a business model, but that’s

<GG>77 WHO·IS ·< YEHWAN·SONG >?
What · has · happened? · In · this · brief · time ·Yehwan · Song · has · risen · to · public · prominence · like · a · phoenix ·
from · the · ashes. · The · first · time · I · got · to · watch · his · gameplay, · he ·was · already · very · known, · being · famous · for · his · work · on · the ·
popular · competitive · game · ` · League · of · Legends' · since · the · game's ·release · in · 2009. · When · I · looked · into · Song's · professional ·
history, · I · discovered · that · he · was · no · newcomer. · In · fact, · Song ·had · done · much · more · than · just · contribute · to · ` · League · of ·
Legends'.Song · initially · gained · fame · as · a · music · and · movie · composer. · From ·
2002 · to · 2009 · he · mostly · composed · theme · music · for · films · and ·TV · shows · such · as · ` · Dae · Jang · Geum' · and · 'Winter · Sonata' · starring ·
Ryu · Dong-geun · and · Cha · Tae-hyun · respectively. · By · 2007 · however, · he ·began · making · the · transition · to · video · game · titles, · and · that ·
changed · everything, · in · a · good · way. · From · then · and ·throughout · the · rest · of · the · 2010's, · Song · would · compose · over ·
150 · pieces · of · music · for · ` · League · of · Legends', · a · number · which ·seems · almost · unassailable · now. · The · reason · for · this · sudden ·
success · is · because · of · an · unlikely · set · of · circumstances · which · led ·to · Song · being · a · game · developer.
```
After · Song · started · making · music · for · ` · League · of · Legends' · (which ·I'll · go · into · shortly), · the · game · received · a · huge · amount · of ·
```
attention · and · acclaim. · So · much · so · that · many · of · the · gaming ·community · began · praising · the · game · for · being · the · best · example ·
of · a · video · game · genre · that · should · be · in · its · infancy. · A · genre ·with · a · long · history · that · has · been · ignored · by · the · mainstream ·
for · one · reason · or · another, · or · for · no · reason · at · all. · These ·people · were · also · impressed · by · Song's · compositions · for · ` ·
```
League · of · Legends'. · The · game · itself · is · a · mix · of · a · real-time ·strategy · and · a · third-person · shooter, · called · ` · MOBAs'; ·
```
Multiplayer · Online · battle · Arenas. · It · is · one · of · the · top · games · in ·the · genre, · and · has · a · huge · following · worldwide. · The · reason ·

for · that · is · very · simple; · its · games · play · unlike · any · other · video ·game · out · there, · and · it · is · not · surprising · how · popular · they · have ·

become · in · a · very · short · period · since · their · release · in · 2009.

A.T

WA N
SONG
www.yhsong.com
Yehwan
Song
Interviewed by
Rob van Leijsen
<Yehwan Song>77 is an artist and designer who questions
standardized design and interface conventions that lack awareness
of content and oversimplify the user experience. She constructs
outside-the-frame web interfaces and tools for websites in order
to challenge the notion of user-friendliness and bring back
content awareness. She pursues diversity instead of consistency,
and respects variety in web design above efficiency.
Interview with Yehwan Song A.T 355
Rob van Leijsen Thank you very much for being with
us today, and for accepting our invitation. We’re really
happy to have the benefit of your insight. I would like to
start by asking you to introduce yourself and tell us a
bit about your design practice. 5
913B.A 48 †
gWebsites · Look 9
gWeb · Design 8
gMaking · Websites
B.A
Yehwan Song I’m an art director, designer and devel-
oper currently based in Seoul, South Korea. My focus is
on web design. I feel kind of frustrated when I see that
most websites look pretty similar, even though they have
very different content. So I try to change this, and make 10
websites more content-focused. I really want to change
the web as a platform, and try to push users to get the
information they want, and understand that information.
R.v.L. How did you get into web design,
and programming in general? 15
915B.D 120 †
gLearn · Code 17
B.D
Y.S. Oh, that’s a hard question. There wasn’t really
one point where I started to learn code and begin to deal
with websites, there were multiple factors. My father
really loved coding, and was fascinated by code, trying
to understand it and how machines work. Whenever 20
he bought a new radio, for example, he first tried to
disassemble it and understand how it worked. He’s that
kind of person.
916 I was born in 1995, which makes me a member
of generation Z: I represent a generation that got exposed 25
to digital devices early on, so for me, it’s just a natural
environment. I was exposed to websites and digital el-
ements on the same level that former generations were
exposed to publications and printed matter.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.T 356 Part A: Interview T
INTERVIEWS
A
R.v.L. Did you learn code from your father,
or did you take programming courses at art school?
918 ‡ B.136g
Ten-week · Program 11
B.D
Y.S. I learned to code from YouTube tutorials, be-
cause I wanted to make some stuff and didn’t know
how to, so I just looked it up. It’s surprising how many5
resources there are on YouTube, especially for web pro-
gramming. Consequently, when I started out, I didn’t
have a tutor or any person who taught me to code line by
line. After a while, I wanted to understand it better, espe-
cially the structure and history of coding. I then enrolled10
in a ten-week program at the School for Poetic Com-
putation, in New York. It’s made up of a combination
of students coming from both art school and computer
science backgrounds. I started to understand how to use
code-based tools there.15
R.v.L. Do you know of any institutions where one can
learn code and web design in Seoul, or is it something
that is not part of art education?
920Y.S. In Korea, there is a lot of activity regarding
design, and graphic design in particular. There are defi-20
nitely environments where you can experiment with code,
and there are coding classes in graphic design programs
these days. At the same time, it’s a whole different world
compared to Europe or the US. Coding is approached
from a mathematical angle here, and some students have25
a hard time following those classes.
R.v.L. Is learning code a separate discipline from
design in Korea?
922 ‡ B.B 83g
Programming · Languages 9
B.B
Y.S. Yes. The way Americans deal with code is much
more fluent because programming languages derive from30
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Yehwan Song A.T 357
.T
YEHWAN SONG
Fig.54
Detail from website,
Dipping Toes, Yehwan Song, 2020.
English. For a Korean, that requires an extra effort,
besides just learning to understand the structure of pro-
gramming. The language in which we code is not related
to our native language, and on top of that, it’s a scien-
tific language. In the US, I noticed that they read code 5
kind of easily, because it’s essentially written in their
language. They can read a function, whereas for Koreans
understanding the terms in the first place is not so easy.
Therefore, programming languages are not that actively
understood in Korea, and I see many limitations in terms 10
of art school students learning them.
R.v.L. That’s a very logical assessment. If you don’t
have any language barriers, you can focus straightaway
on the technical aspects of coding. Could you explain
more about the web projects you worked on? 15
924B.A 50 †
gPersonal · Website 17
B.A
Y.S. Whenever I have a web project, I always try
to make something different. My personal website, for
example, uses <Google > <G>34Spreadsheet as a database.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.T 358 Part A: Interview T
INTERVIEWS
A
I don’t like the process of coding the back end of a
website, and creating a database myself. I want to fo-
cus on the front end, and <Google ><G>34 Spreadsheet can
be used as a tool to make a website lighter and more
understandable, even for non-developers.5
925 ‡ B.A 51g
Making · Websites
B.A
Most of the websites I create are micro-websites,
a kind of flat website without a database. I make them
very direct: if you press a button, it reacts immediately.
Buttons do not have a bunch of layers, or animations, to
make things cool. That’s part of my practice, to make10
websites as light as possible.
R.v.L. Does this preference for lightweight websites
have to do with the ecological impact of the web? Do
you participate in the low-tech trend?
927Y.S. It depends. What I’m mainly trying to do is to15
make things obvious and easy to understand. I don’t
want to create black boxes and abstract websites, where
it’s hard to see what’s going on.
R.v.L. How do you apply that to client-based projects?
929Y.S. I first try to understand the client’s needs. It is20
not a matter of creating the perfect website. The environ-
ments, browsers and devices we use are always changing.
So if you want to keep a website up and running, it
requires maintenance. I try to get clients to let go of
that, even when a website is broken. You are better off25
creating a new website than putting all your energy into
fixing it, and trying to keep its design the same forever.
930 ‡ B.A 49g
Functional · Website 30
gClients 5
B.A
So far, especially in Korea, this approach has
been accepted to some degree, because I come up with a
hybrid between a highly functional website and a visual30
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

YEHWAN SONG

portal, almost akin to a poster. I like the temporality of
the poster medium. We use posters for a certain amount
of time only, and do not reuse them. So I look at it as
a temporary medium, and I think that’s what websites
should be too. At any rate, I try to push my clients in 5
that direction.
R.v.L. Does your approach create misunderstandings
with clients? For example, do they have to adapt their

932B.A 49 †

Y.S. I try to put myself in a position where I am able 10

of a client project. To be honest, I try to find clients
who understand my way of thinking and approach to
web design. Usually they are already familiar with my
practice and support me, as a result there haven’t been 15
problems with that yet.

934Y.S. I would say that a website running for longer

draw up a contract with me to maintain their website,

multiple libraries or frameworks, but I sometimes use
<React><G>71 , if I really need to build a big database, or
even use some machine learning. Otherwise, aside from
<JavaScript><G>41, 37, 17 , I use <HTML> and <CSS>.
R.v.L. Do you also use code to generate other visual15

938Y.S. I was focusing on static posters and motion

and go beyond basic computer-generated imagery that
isn’t really esthetic.
R.v.L. Do you think you will get back to designing

940 ‡ B.A 26g

gWeb · Projects 26
gClients 7
B.A
Y.S. I think I will. I still love doing animation, and25
it’s not that different or separate from my web projects.
When I design a website, I need to create other visual ma-
terial, such as icons, logos and animations, so I don’t feel
like I have stopped doing static design work. All the more
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Yehwan Song A.T 361

YEHWAN SONG
so these days, in the middle of the coronavirus pandemic,
all the exhibitions happening in Korea are going online.
An exhibition requires general promotional material in
addition to a website. Therefore, I’m doing quite a bit of

I have heard that some traditional graphic designers have

more money and time. 10
R.v.L. What is your experience in terms of the
relations between South Korea, the US and Europe
when it comes to collaborations with other
designers and working with clients?
942 Y.S. I actually work with clients from the US, Ger- 15
many and Korea, and their characteristics are very dif-
ferent. In Europe, for example, they give me more time
to think and reflect, while in the US and Korea, there is
more urgency, and the job has to be finished by the next
day. Still, I love those cultural differences and they are 20
reflected in the resulting websites, because they are also
experienced in different ways.

gPersonal · Website 28
gClients 1

Y.S. I think it really helps designers and students to

these social media platforms still allow you to show your
work globally. Especially for designers here in Korea, 30

who have a limited reach in terms of clients outside

946 ‡ B.C 102g

A.U 364 Part A: Interview U

A.U
SA NDER

STA N
HAA NAPPEL
STUDIO
DUMBAR
www.studiodumbar.com
Stan Haanappel, senior visual and motion
designer at Studio Dumbar, graduated from the
Willem de Kooning Academy. He is the 2017
winner of the Fontanel Award for Dutch Design
Talent. He specializes in new technologies, digital
crafts and typography, and unique approaches
in design communication. His latest explorations
cover wifi allergies, unsecured security cameras,
digital amnesia, and the creation of a mobile phone
scroll robot.
Sander Sturing has been an interaction designer
and creative coder at Studio Dumbar for the last
five years. After completing his studies in
Information and Interactive Media Design at the
Arnhem Art Academy, Sturing started working as
both a developer and designer, mainly for the Web.
Currently, Sander has been focused on creative
coding, using code as a design tool to create custom
software for other designers to use and the creation
of computational and generative designs.
Studio Dumbar
Stan
Haanappel
Sander
Sturing
Interviewed by
Demian Conrad
```
<Studio Dumbar>78 (part of Dept) is an international agency
```
with a Dutch heritage, specializing in visual branding and motion.
Studio Dumbar attracts talented individuals from around the
world. Their portfolio is equally diverse, encompassing work for
a variety of clients both large and small, from business and govern-
ment to cultural and non-profit. Projects range from the visual
identity of the Dutch Police, the Adidas Futurenatural identity,
the non-profit Alzheimer Nederland, and the Van Gogh Museum
identity refresh to initiating and designing Demo, the largest
design in motion festival in the world.
Interview with Stan Haanappel + Sander Sturing A.U 365
Demian Conrad Hi Sander and Stan, thank you for
speaking about your practice with me. Can you start
with a short introduction about who you are?
950 Stan Haanappel I’m Stan Haanappel, a visual designer
at Studio Dumbar. I have worked here for almost four 5
years. I was an intern here for three months when I was
in art school in Rotterdam, and that’s when I decided
that this where I wanted to work. I think the project
that you’re working on right now is really interesting,
because we are in the middle of this exciting change at 10
the studio. Sander and I have worked on a few projects
that are really pertinent to this subject, which we shall
go into later.
951951 Sander Sturing I’m Sander Sturing, I have been work-
ing at Studio Dumbar for around five years, and prior to 15
that, I worked for the same amount of time at another
studio in The Hague. I started coding when I was at art
school, using programming on a really low level and it
was not really my goal to make it my main profession.
Ten years ago, as a technical designer, the only thing 20
you could work on at a studio was websites, so I started
out doing a bit of UX design and coding websites. I’m
still doing that now, but, over the last couple of years,
it’s shifted more and more towards what we now call
creative coding, or really creating specific tools or scripts 25
to help us with design.
952B.A 28 †
gWorking · Method 32
gGraphic · Design 6
gCouple · of · Years 29
gMotion · Design 7
gSpecific · Projects 31
gDesign · Tools
gClients 10
gDon · t · Think
B.A
At first my task consisted of making an existing
design easier to use, or quickly create a lot of variations of
it, but now, for the last couple of years, we have actually
been designing with code. We’ll go into some specific 30
projects later, but to give you an idea of our working
method, we sketch a lot, we have always done that. We
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.U 366 Part A: Interview U
INTERVIEWS
A
don’t think about just three or four specific directions,
but explore all the directions we can think of. Then,
together with the client, we see if there’s something they
like, or they dislike, so we can develop the sketches. We
used to sketch on paper, with very traditional graphic5
design tools, but, over the last years, as we have shifted
towards motion design, we began using that, along with
scripting, in the early phases of our projects. This way,
we can demonstrate the upside of using these technologies
to our clients. That’s a big change for us, but it’s really10
interesting and raises a lot of questions.
D.C. How did you begin working with creative
coding at the studio?
954S.H. When I was in art school, people started working
with <Processing><G>68 . This was a really exciting time: you15
could make wild things, like interactive installations and
such, but at that point it wasn’t really happening on
a large scale for corporate visual identities. The whole
time Sander and I have been working together, we were
continuously experimenting back and forth. I am more20
on the visual side of things, and I know what is possible
with code, but I will never be a coder. I have already
abandoned the idea that I would ever learn those skills,
because it’s just not my thing.
955 ‡ B.C 97g
Design · Tools 28
gDesign · Process 4
B.C
So we were already sketching stuff, just waiting25
for the right project to actually begin to use this approach
on a job. I think the first big commercial project where
we actually created a design tool was for Amsterdam
Sinfonietta. There was actually a sketch for creating a
gradient halftone effect, and then we replaced the dots30
with typographic elements. We created that sketch as an
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Stan Haanappel + Sander Sturing A.U 367
.U
STUDIO DUMBAR
<Illustrator> <G>4plug-in, and we thought about how cool it
would be if it could actually move, and react to a physical
or sound input. While we were working on the design
process for stills, like brochure covers, etc., Stan and
I began experimenting with making this halftone grid 5
with typography in <Processing> <G>68. At that point, the
whole identity system came into being. All of a sudden,
we had a tool that could create something we were not
able to do in <After Effects> <G>3, or with the usual tools,
one that created something special and unique.

Detail, Amsterdam Sinfonietta identity,
Studio Dumbar, 2018.
10
956 This also opened the door for everyone to ex-
periment in the studio. It was such a cool thing to see
Sander, our one creative coder, open that door for all
the designers working with us. Everyone was eager to
test it. They would come up to him and say, “OK, give 15
me this software and let’s see what I can do with it.” It
has been one of the coolest things about the past years,
because everyone saw this new environment open up for
us. The identity we were working on also took shape.
All of a sudden, we had something that could shift, and 20
from the same input, it could have an endless number of
possibilities.

A

gClients 12
gWorking · with · Processing
B.B
We continued working with <Processing><G>68 , but
since it’s a sketching program, we had to take it a step
further software-wise, since we also had ideas about work-
ing directly with the orchestra to do a sort of live, VJ
set with them. Sander managed to redo the program5
in openFrameworks in his spare time, and all of a sud-
den, we were able to do way heavier stuff, like render
actual live video, instead of having to export the frames,
treat them and compile them in <After Effects><G>3 . This
was a cultural project, but it also opened up possibili-10
ties in terms of what we could do to personalize other
client projects.
958958S.S. Of course the fact that this project opened
these doors was of interest, but also, since it was the first
project we did that way, it taught us a lot about how15
this type of process should go, and how we could benefit
from the technology. Like Stan said, the initial way we
produced the content was quite a hassle, because we were
developing the process at the same time as the project.
The project was already defined on the visual side at the20
point when we decided to include the creative coding
part, and that opened our eyes as to how we could use
coding to actually design it.
959 ‡ B.C 109g
Working · with · Code 25
gDesign · Software 3
gWork · with · Designers 25
gSketching · with · Code 30
B.C
As a programmer, it is good to work with de-
signers who are open to working with code, since I need25
their input. I wouldn’t like to be working all by myself.
We mostly work together, along with Stan, because, as
he said, he knows what’s possible with code, he’s really
enthusiastic and sees a lot of possibilities. Now sketching
with code has become part of our initial process, not just30
to find nice shapes, but to work with it right along with
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Stan Haanappel + Sander Sturing A.U 369
.U
STUDIO DUMBAR
the other tools we have. We don’t go into a project think-
ing we need to include code, but we use it in the same
way we use <Illustrator> <G>4or any other design software:
it’s not just this weird techy thing anymore.
D.C. How do you approach creative coding in 5
regards to the projects of the studio?
961 S.H. We only use creative coding when it adds real
value to the project, so it really depends on the project
and also on the sketches that we have already done for it.
For example, the identity for the Design in Motion Festi- 10
val [DEMO], came from one of our designer colleagues
who was using Autodesk SketchBook on his phone in
the train on his way to work. He used an image trans-
formation tool that allowed him to warp an image with
his finger from the center, while keeping the sides intact. 15
It was really interesting visually, so we all started to
experiment with images of letters. It was fascinating,
the movement was super fluid, but we had no clue as
to what was happening. Everybody tried to replicate
it using <After Effects> <G>3as well as other software, but 20
we couldn’t figure it out. There was no way to recreate
this effect, and that made it even more attractive. At

realized that the magic behind this transformation was
only possible when you broke the image down into a grid 25
of small pieces, and then stretched or squished them.

968968S.S. What I thought was interesting, specifically for
the Cumulus Park project, was how we sold it to the
client. We usually try to apply the identity we’re design-
ing to different items. We generally use still sketches,
which we show on billboards so the client can see what5
it might look like. However, since Cumulus Park has all
these public spaces around Amsterdam, we thought it
would be nice to imagine a common feature involving
the identity. It took the form of a clock, which they then
actually asked us to make. We already had most of the10
code, so we were able to do it quite easily. That is one
instance in which these skills have opened up new doors
for us with clients.
969969S.H. What we are also seeing is that sometimes it’s
easier to create an animation with code than it is in After15
Effects.
D.C. What kind of impact has this increased use of

evolved a lot, since a project does not simply involve
one graphic designer, but more a sort of team including20
animators and programmers.

S.S. I was always doing a bit of coding, but it mostly
wasn’t part of our work. A few years ago, we started
focusing on motion design. We already had a motion
designer in the studio and were incorporating a bit of25
animation in our projects at that point, but it had never
been our main focus. We perceived a growing need for
more dynamic elements from the clients and brands we
worked with, so we started talking about it in the studio.
Our creative director, Liza [Enebeis], always says static30
is no longer an option, and we truly believe that. Now,
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Stan Haanappel + Sander Sturing A.U 373
.U
STUDIO DUMBAR
you have to design for screens of all sizes and animate
things more than ever. The studio is now based around
that idea, with two recent hires being motion designers
who also work with 3D and VR, for example.
972 Creative coding is the extra element we can use 5
to push things even further, to do things that are not
possible with the software we are already using, such
as creating something interactive. It’s really all about
broadening our skill set. We have a team made up of equal
shares of graphic and motion designers, with some people, 10
like Stan, who can also program with <After Effects> <G>3
or Cinema 4D. The skill sets of the new designers we
hire are much broader: this can result in new ways of
working, and new projects. Creative coding seems like a
logical next step for a studio like ours. 15
D.C. As a graphic design studio, do you think the

six years ago is that motion design is not something we
introduce after the project begins. Now we include it in
the initial conception phase of every new project. This
does not mean that every project has to have some ani- 25
mated element, it really must be relevant to the project
at hand. In any case, clients usually get really excited
when they see something move. Screens and animations
are all around us, and clients perceive the opportunity
they represent for their brands. Moving elements have 30
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>

enormous power when it comes to capturing the atten-

B.A
The DEMO Festival was a direct result of the5
increase in the number of screens around cities. For the
most part, we saw poorly animated advertisements on
them, even though there are a lot of super-talented de-
signers with incredible motion design skills who don’t
have a platform to show them outside of Instagram. We10
came up with the idea of organizing this festival in order
to give them a chance to actually show their work in a
physical space. As a result, for a period of twenty-four
hours, the screens of the Amsterdam Central Station
displayed their animations, many of which were made15
with creative coding. A lot of people are currently exper-
imenting with this, and I think that will only increase in
the future.

skills, and studios are actively seeking them out. Graphic
designers no longer focus solely on static and printed
```
content anymore; a lot of them seek to diversify the range

of tools with which they work.25

S.H. I think that students should make the decision
themselves. We will always need specialists, and it’s not 5
good when everyone becomes a Jack of all trades. For
example, our team includes a typography specialist. He is
from the TypeMedia program at KABK [Royal Academy
of Arts, The Hague], and what he does is impossible for
the rest of us. We really need that kind of focus and 10
mastery of one discipline, be it in typography, motion
design or creative coding.
979 On the other hand, having some knowledge of
the fields associated with yours really helps in terms
of collaborating with the people with whom you work. 15
I have a basic understanding of code, and an idea of what
is possible to accomplish with it, and I think that is a
huge benefit in my work with Sander.
980 I know some art schools in The Netherlands have
started experimenting with teaching creative coding, and 20
trying to get students interested in these technological
aspects. Overall, I would say that we need “all-arounders”
as well as specialists, so schools should provide students
with the possibility of choosing which path they want
to follow. 25

some freedom to discover what they are into, and the

Much of the time, studios are behind what’s
happening in schools. I graduated ten years ago, and5
then there was a gap between those who could develop
or design websites. That gap has almost closed over the
last few years: now it’s pretty normal for designers to
be able to code a website, or at least to know what’s
possible with the web.10
983Like Stan said, what the DEMO Festival demon-
strated is mostly the gap between what exists in term
of commercial animation on advertising screens, which

what designers are doing on Instagram. These young15
designers create wild stuff, but big brands are not yet
making use of their skills. A lot of studios are not really
ready to fully integrate these talents, and often they are
still in art school.
984Interns have an important role at Studio Dum-20
bar, because we learn a lot from them. We try to hire
people who don’t do the same things we do, who have
new and different skills and ways of seeing. That actually
has to come from the students themselves. The school
should give them the possibility of exploring what they25
really like, and the studios have yet to embrace this.

STUDIO DUMBAR
986 S.H. I don’t think that our jobs will ever be done by

have an underlying concept, a sort of emotional value
that only humans can conceive, hopefully. Of course, you
can get a really cool hipster logo with your name under 5
it online in two seconds, but the whole conceptual aspect

S.S. We have already tried to include AI in a project,
but we didn’t have the technical skills and the amount of
time necessary to make it work the way we wanted. How- 10
ever, change is coming faster and faster, it will continue
to grow, and I think we need to roll with it. In the end,
technology gives us more tools and ways to design. If
you go with it, you can use this power and have it work
```
for you. Automation is one example; instead of spending 15
```
time doing repetitive tasks, you can let the machine do
it for you, and focus on the creative part. We sometimes
create a tool not because we can’t do something in <Af-
ter Effects> <G>3, but because it is faster to iterate through
programming. We stay in charge of what the program 20
does, and of choosing the right outcome. That will always
remain the task of the designer. The risk is of course to
automate too much, and lose the human input along the
way.
988988 S.H. What I’m scared of is data becoming the most 25
important thing. It already happens with A/B tests,
where the clicks of users get to dictate which color is
best, for example. I fear that AIs will tell us how to
design the most successful brand based solely on data.
I think we’ll have to fight for design to not to be selected 30
by algorithms in the future. I don’t want to work with a
machine telling me what will be successful.
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.U 378 Part A: Interview U
INTERVIEWS
A
989989S.S. I don’t think that’s going to be a real design
problem. Like you said, if <Google ><G>34 is testing which
color is getting one percent more clicks, that’s what
they require, and I don’t think you can call that design.
I’m fine with that, we designers can do other, more5
interesting things.
990In the near future, what will be changing is that
VR, AR and such will become increasingly mainstream.
The next step is to figure out how to brand something,
or create an identity for VR. Another thing would be10
designing for non-visual things, such as voice interfaces
and the like, where you can’t apply a logo or a color.
The field is changing, but it always has been, only now
it’s at a much faster pace. Our job as designers is to try
to communicate with the user as to which senses the15
experience affects. That role remains, we simply adapt
it to different media.
D.C. Thanks for these insights. Is there anything
with which you wish to conclude?

Interviewed by
Demian Conrad
```
<Samuel Weidmann>79 (b. 1984) studied
```
visual communication at the Zurich University of
```
the Arts (ZHdK) and the Artez in Arnhem, and
```
graduated in 2011 after internships at Marie Lusa
and Lehni-Trüb. In 2012, Weidmann founded
the eponymous design studio Samuel Weidmann,
which has been active in the multidisciplinary
field of visual communication. The focus of
his practice lies in the symbiosis of technology
and design and the intersection of web design,
animation and code, the development of inter-
media identities as well as related artistic
directions. His commissioned work includes clients
both cultural and commercial and he also works
on self-initiated projects. Weidmann is also a
visiting lecturer in digital publishing, web design
and graphic animation at the Design and
Visual Communication Department at the Zurich
University of the Arts.
Interview with Samuel Weidmann A.V 381
Demian Conrad Hi Samuel, thanks for accepting
our invitation to discuss your practice.
Could you start by introducing yourself and your
work as a graphic designer?
995 Samuel Weidmann Thank you for the invitation! I am 5
a graphic designer based in Zurich. I work in the mul-
tidisciplinary field of graphic design—the focus of my
practice lies in the symbiosis of technology and design
and intersects web design, animation and coding, as
well as the development of related intermedia identities. 10
I do not limit myself to this area, but this is what best
characterizes my practice. I work for clients in both the
commercial and cultural fields and also do self-initiated
projects. As of 2021, I am a visiting lecturer in digital
publishing, web design and graphic animation at the 15
Department of Design, and Visual Communication at
ZHdK [Zurich University of the Arts]. Previously, I had
been an assistant in new media since 2017.
D.C. Can give us some details of your Grafik Kiosk
project? How did it start? How was it developed, from 20
the general idea to the more technical aspects?
997B.A 22 †
gGraphic · Design 29
gVisual · Communication
gThesis · Project 22
gField · of · Graphic · Design 29
B.A
S.W. Grafik Kiosk was my thesis project with Jonas
Hegi for our Bachelor’s Degree in Visual Communication
at ZHdK, back in 2011. While I was in school, I had
already became interested in future issues relating to 25
the profession of graphic designer. This continued during
my internship at Lehni-Trüb, where they were already
thinking about process automation in the field of graphic
design, such as templates and <PDF> <G>65to book. Jonas
was an intern at NORM, and he was interested in design 30
systems. At some point, we both met Raphael Koch,
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.V 382 Part A: Interview V
INTERVIEWS
A
who, along with Urs Hofer and Gina Bucher, created
a software called Rokfor for generative design through
templates. We were quite interested in those types of
processes, from a machine that allowed you to design
and print your business card at the Zurich train station,5
to software built for design.
Fig.58
Grafik Kiosk is a digital program that intuitively generates
posters in different formats using user generated content.
Jonas Hegi, Samuel Weidmann, ZHdK, 2011.
998 ‡ B.A 29g
Graphic · Design 13
gExperimental · Project 8
B.A
For our final BA project, we teamed up to do
an experimental project, without caring about whether
it would work or not. Basically, we decided to create a
machine that could replace us as graphic designers. We10
thought it was kind of funny, since we were reaching the
end of our studies, to develop a machine that worked as
a graphic designer. We had no idea on how or where to
start, because there were so many questions.
999 ‡ B.C 97g
Good · Thing 8
gTime · Constraints 6
gCreative · Process 20
gDesign · Process 11
B.C
Eventually we just started thinking about what15
design is and what it takes to do it. Mainly, it was about
figuring out how to express design. We defined param-
eters for placement, density, contrast, and hierarchy in
order to have a set of ways to talk about design and figure
out how to turn a creative process into an algorithm. Our20
goal from the beginning was not to be template-based.
Right up until five days before the exhibition, we still
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Samuel Weidmann A.V 383
.V
SAMUEL WEIDMANN
had no idea if the project would even work because there
were so many questions that remained to be answered.
To this day there still are, because, while the idea and
the concept behind it works quite well, the application
has some shortcomings that could not be fixed due to 5
```
time constraints; but, most importantly, in the end the
```
output all looks more or less the same. I guess we failed
in a way, but it’s still kind of a good thing. Actually, we
didn’t think we would reflect that much on design, about
our position, or what’s important or not in the design 10
process, but it led us to begin to figure out what kind of
values we have as designers.
1000 We didn’t do the coding ourselves. We found two
interaction designers, Mario von Rickenbach and Philipp
Läubli, who did the developing for us, because we only 15
had three months to do the whole project. Four years
ago [in 2017], the Museum für Gestaltung [Zurich Design
Museum] asked us if they could exhibit the project in
their “What’s Next” corner. It was funny, because the
project was already old news for us at that point, but 20
was going to be shown as innovative.
D.C. Could you dive a bit more into the details of
the project? Particularly regarding its interface, since it
does not use the usual mouse and pointer paradigms for
designing posters used in Illustrator, for example. 25
1002 S.W. The interface is based on some simple parame-
ters, and for each of these parameters, we tried to figure
out their most extreme values. We thought about a way
to describe these combinations, and how they looked to
us, and came up with words for these values, like light, 30
heavy, funny or colorful. Using the application, you first
<GLOSSARY >KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
A.V 384 Part A: Interview V
INTERVIEWS
A
type the information you want on the poster, and then
set the different values, for example 50% funny, 0% heavy.
Finally, you can generate the poster using these values.
Our goal was also to transform the names of these values
into unfamiliar words in design, to make you think about5
the meaning of these words. You often hear clients asking
designers to make a poster more “fresh,” “on point,” or
“strict.” These words have different meanings for every-
body, and what do they mean exactly when applied to
design? Actually the most interesting point to reflect10
about was what exactly is design. What would a value
of 100% funny look like, and how would an algorithm
translate that, or is it even possible? We talked a lot
about visual connections, visual codes and meaning in
relation to context.15
1003We also thought about fonts, the visual possi-
bilities they offer, and the possibility of choosing one, or
being restricted to a specific one. We drew a font family,
Kiosk Grotesk, inspired by A4 format proportions. We
did both a light and heavy cut, as well as rounded, wob-20
bly, and abstract versions.
1004In my opinion, the most interesting part of the
project was the thinking about design. It raised a lot of
```
questions: what should design be? Can it be replaced by
```
algorithms? What happens when it is? That is the reason25
why you couldn’t use the application at the Museum für
Gestaltung and you could only watch videos of someone
using it. We didn’t want people to focus on the results,
but rather on the process and reflection behind it.
<GLOSSARY > KEY SENTENCESML-BASEDCLUSTERING<GENERATED GLOSSARY>
Interview with Samuel Weidmann A.V 385
.V
SAMUEL WEIDMANN
D.C. Grafik Kiosk was a students’ final thesis
project. Do you think you would approach it differently
today, based on the knowledge you have acquired since
then, and the technologies that are available now?
1006 S.W. Of course. We had to deliver something in a very 5
short time frame, there was only so much we could work
on and address. What I would do today is specify certain
aspects even more, and work towards a more neutral
end result, to avoid the questions around authorship
in design. I would also try to code it myself, since if 10
you have the idea, but lack the skill to develop it, there
always is a gap between what you have in mind and the
final outcome.
D.C. Thank you Samuel for your time
and reflections. 15
1008 S.W. You’re welcome.

```
HyperCard. ActionScript was initiallydesigned for controlling simple 2D vector
```
animations made in Adobe Flash (former-ly Macromedia Flash). Initially focused on
```
animation, early versions of Flash contentoffered few interactivity features and thus
had very limited scripting capability. Laterversions added functionality, allowing for
```
the creation of web-based games and richweb applications with streaming media (such
```
```
as video and audio). Today, ActionScript issuitable for desktop and mobile development
```
through Adobe Air, and can be used in somedatabase applications, as well as in basic
robotics, such as the Make Controller Kit.• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/
ActionScript

video, motion pictures and print. Adobewas founded in December 1982 by John

for audiovisual content creation, editingand publishing.

ing, compositing, and animation. It alsofunctions as a very basic non-linear editor,

lishing and page layout designing softwareapplication produced by Adobe Inc. Graphic
designers and production artists are the prin-cipal users, creating and laying out periodical

SWF formats to create e-books and digitalpublications, including digital magazines,

editor developed and published by AdobeInc. It was originally created in 1988 by
Thomas and John Knoll. Since then, thesoftware has become the industry standard
not only in raster graphics editing, but in
digital art as a whole. Photoshop can editand compose raster images in multiple layers
and supports masks, alpha compositing andseveral color models. Photoshop uses its
own PSD and PSB file formats to support
these features.

Macromedia Shockwave) is a discontinuedmultimedia platform for building inter-

active multimedia applications and videogames. Developers originate content

in a web browser on any computer with
the Shockwave Player plug-in installed.

6 WHAT IS <ADOBE>?Adobe is an American software company specializing in
develop-ing and marketing of a variety of graphics design tools,software for desktop, and mobile devices. The company is
one of the pioneers in the software industry that is now beinghailed as a global provider of Web-based services and
applications.Adobe has established a strong portfolio, with its signature
offerings being the desktop and mobile productivity applications,along with its video and animation products. The company has
since started a new generation of products with the introductionof Creative Cloud and Creative Suite.
8 WHO IS <ILLUSTRATOR>?An Illustrator is a graphics application. It’s made up of
tools, palettes, objects, and layers, that allow you to draw,paint, and design. Unlike a Photoshop, Illustrator is much more
affordable and most of the work of designing for printis done in pre-prepared styles, allowing you to change just a
little. There are thousands ofIllustrator designs available to
inspire you and here at We’rIllustrators we provide custom
designs and services to a smallgroup of clients.

8 AlgorithmIn mathematics and computer
science, an algorithm is a finite sequenceof well-defined, computer-implementable
instructions, typically to solve a class ofspecific problems or to perform a com-
putation. Algorithms are used as specifi-cations for performing calculations, data
processing, automated reasoning, and othertasks. Starting from an initial state and
initial input, the algorithm’s instructionsdescribe a computation that, when executed,
proceeds through a finite number ofwell-defined successive states, eventually
producing “output” and terminating at afinal ending state.
Algorithms are essential to the waycomputers process data. Many computer
programs contain algorithms that detailthe specific instructions a computer should
perform—in a specific order—to carry out
a specified task.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/
Algorithm
9 AppleApple Inc. is an American multi-
national technology company that spe-cializes in consumer electronics, computer
software, and online services. Apple wasfounded by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and
Ronald Wayne in 1976 to develop and sellWozniak’s Apple I personal computer. It
was incorporated by Jobs and Wozniak as
Apple Computer, Inc. in 1977, and sales of
its computers, including the Apple II, grewquickly. They went public in 1980 to instant
financial success. Over the next few years,Apple shipped new computers featuring
innovative graphical user interfaces, suchas the original Macintosh.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/
Apple_Inc.

11 ArduinoArduino is an open-source electronics
platform based on easy-to-use hardwareand software. Arduino boards are able to
read inputs—light on a sensor, a finger on abutton, or a Twitter message—and turn it
into an output: activating a motor, turningon an LED, publishing something online.
You can tell your board what to do by send-ing a set of instructions to the microcon-
troller on the board. To do so you use the
```
Arduino programming language (based on
```
```
Wiring), and the Arduino Software (IDE),
```
based on Processing.All Arduino boards are completely
open-source, empowering users to buildthem independently and eventually adapt
them to their particular needs. The software,too, is open-source, and it is growing through
the contributions of users worldwide.• arduino.cc/en/Guide/
Introduction
```
12 Artificial Intelligence (AI)Artificial intelligence (AI) is
```
intelligence demonstrated by machines, asopposed to the natural intelligence dis-
played by humans and animals. LeadingAI textbooks define the field as the study
of “intelligent agents”: any system that per-ceives its environment and takes actions
that maximize its chance of achieving itsgoals. Some popular accounts use the term
artificial intelligence to describe machinesthat mimic “cognitive” functions that
humans associate with the human mind,such as learning and problem solving. AI
```
applications include advanced Web searchengines, recommendation systems (used
```
```
by YouTube, Amazon and Netflix),understanding human speech (such as Siri
```
```
or Alexa), self-driving cars (e.g. Tesla), andcompeting at the highest level in strategic
```
```
game systems (such as chess and Go).
```
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/
Artificial_intelligence

13 Augmented Reality (AR)Augmented reality is computer-
```
generated content overlaid on a real worldenvironment. AR hardware comes in many
forms, including devices that you can carryand devices you wear, such as headsets,
and glasses. Common applications of ARtechnology include video games, television,
and personal navigation.
• techterms.com/definition/
augmented_reality

14 BASICBASIC (Beginners’ All-Purpose
```
```
Symbolic Instruction Code) is a family ofgeneral-purpose, high-level programming
```
languages whose design philosophy empha-sizes ease of use. The original version was
designed by John G. Kemeny and ThomasE. Kurtz and released at Dartmouth College
in 1964. They wanted to enable students infields other than science and mathematics to
use computers. At the time, nearly all com-puter use required writing custom software,
which was something only scientists andmathematicians tended to learn.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BASIC
21 WHAT IS <APPLE>?Is it a software company? Or at least a consumer electronics
company? Or is it something more special as it has been recentlyreported? I think the answer is far from trivial, so it is worth
looking at the history and try to get a better understandingof what is it exactly that Apple is. In my view it would be fair
```
to say that there are two major moments for Apple, that havesignificantly shaped that company – the birth of Apple (which
```
```
happened by some stretch of definition in the early 1970s) andthe birth of the iMac in 1998. In other words, when did Apple
```
as it exists today come into existence? As a simple question,the answer is of course never. The company was always alive, as
Steve Jobs well understood his own words, in 1985. He said,“we don’t know how it will turn out, but we have to trust in
the creative process”, and this certainly reflected the way he wasthinking about the company.

16 C++C++ is a general-purpose pro-
gramming language created by Bjarne
Stroustrup in 1982 as an extension ofthe C programming language, or “C with
Classes.” The language has expandedsignificantly over time, and modern C++
now has object-oriented, generic, andfunctional features in addition to facilities
for low-level memory manipulation. It isalmost always implemented as a compiled
language, and many vendors provide C++compilers, including the Free Software
Foundation, LLVM, Microsoft, Intel,Oracle, and IBM, so it is available on many
platforms. C++ was designed with anorientation toward system programming
and embedded, resource-constrained soft-
ware and large systems, with performance,efficiency, and flexibility of use as its design
highlights.

C%2B%2B

17 CSSCascading Style Sheets (CSS) is a
```
style-sheet language used for describing thepresentation of a document written in a
markup language such as HTML. CSS is a
cornerstone technology of the World WideWeb, alongside HTML and JavaScript.
CSS is designed to enable the separationof presentation and content, including lay-
out, colors, and fonts. The term cascading
derives from the specified priority schemeto determine which style rule applies if
more than one rule matches a particularelement. This cascading priority scheme
is predictable.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CSS
18 Commodore C64The Commodore C64 was a flag-
ship personal computer product of the
Commodore company, released in 1982.It was largely recognized as the highest-
selling personal computer model of alltime, with between 10 and 17 million units
```
sold (according to available estimates).The Commodore C64 was an 8-bit home
```
computer with 64 kB of RAM. It ran on a
Commodore BASIC operating system andhad a VIC-II graphics card, an external 170
K floppy drive, ports for two joysticks, and
a cartridge port.In its time, the Commodore C64
stood out from its competitors in terms ofboth sound and graphics, with multicolored
sprites and three-channel sound that pro-
vided what was, for that era, cutting-edgetechnology. The ability to play Commodore
games on the system was only part of the
appeal, with a variety of business uses also
built into the early computing system.

18140/commodore-64-c64

```
(known as the VC-20 in Germany and theVIC-1001 in Japan) is an 8-bit home

computer that was sold by CommodoreBusiness Machines. The VIC-20 was an-
nounced in 1980, roughly three years after
Commodore’s first personal computer, thePET. The VIC-20 was the first computer
of any description to sell one million units.

20 DatabaseA database is an organized collec-
tion of structured information, or data,typically stored electronically in a computer
```
system. A database is usually controlled bya database management system (DBMS).
```
Together, the data and the DBMS, alongwith the applications that are associated
with them, are referred to as a databasesystem, often shortened to just database.
Data within the most common typesof databases in operation today is typically
modeled in rows and columns in a series of
tables to make processing and data query-ing efficient. The data can then be easily
accessed, managed, modified, updated,controlled, and organized. Most databases
```
use structured query language (SQL) forwriting and querying data.
```
• oracle.com/database/
what-is-database
21 DrawBotDrawBot is a powerful, free applica-
tion for MacOSX that invites you to writesimple Python scripts to generate two-
dimensional graphics. The built-in graphicsprimitives support rectangles, ovals,
```
(Bézier) paths, polygons, text objects and
```
transparency.
DrawBot is an ideal tool for teach-ing the basics of programming. Students
get colorful graphic treats while becom-ing familiar with variables, conditional
statements, functions, etc. Results can besaved in a selection of different file formats,
including high resolution, scaleable PDF,SVG, movie, PNG, JPEG and TIFF.
DrawBot is written in Python. The
DrawBot project started in 2003 as a pro-gram named DesignRobots, written for a
Python workshop at the TypoTechnica con-ference. Since then the application evolved
into a Cocoa application with a powerfulAPI and image export functionality. It has
proven itself as a key part of the curriculumat the Royal Academy in The Hague, and
is developed by Just van Rossum, Erik van
Blokland and Frederik Berlaen.
• drawbot.com/index.html

proprietary model as well as an open-sourcemodel, allowing the licensor to simultane-
ously leverage the advantages of both typesof licenses.

software-security/
software-licensing-decisions-

23 EPUB
EPUB is an e-book file format that
uses the “.epub” file extension. The term is
short for electronic publication and is sup-
ported by many e-readers, and compatiblesoftware is available for most smartphones,
tablets, and computers. The EPUB formatis implemented as an archive file consist-
ing of XHTML files carrying the content,along with images and other supporting
files. EPUB is the most widely supportedvendor-independent XML-based e-book
format. EPUB is a technical standard
published by the International DigitalPublishing Forum and supported by almost
all hardware readers.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EPUB

24 e-Reader
An e-reader, also called an e-bookreader or e-book device, is a mobile electronic
device that is designed primarily for thepurpose of reading digital e-books and
```
periodicals. Any device that can displaytext on a screen may act as an e-reader;
```
however, specialized e-reader devices mayoptimize portability, readability, and battery
life for this purpose. Their main advantagesover printed books are portability. An
e-reader is capable of holding thousands ofbooks while weighing less than one book.
Many e-readers use the Internet throughWi-Fi and the built-in software can provide
a link to a digital library or an e-bookretailer, allowing the user to buy, borrow,
and receive digital e-books.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/E-reader
25 FlashAdobe Flash is a multimedia
software platform used for production ofanimations, rich web applications, desktop
applications, mobile apps, mobile games,and embedded web browser video players.
Flash displays text, vector graphics, andraster graphics to provide animations,
video games, and applications. It allowedstreaming of audio and video, and can
capture mouse, keyboard, microphone, andcamera input.
Flash was initially used to createfully interactive websites, but this approach
was phased out with the introduction ofHTML5. Instead, Flash found a niche as the
dominant platform for online multimediacontent, particularly for browser games.
Due to numerous security flaws, the useof Flash declined as Adobe transitioned to
the Adobe Air platform. The Flash Playerwas deprecated in 2017 and officially
discontinued at the end of 2020.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/
Adobe_Flash
26 Fontographer
Fontographer, developed by JamesR. Von Ehr for the Mac and released in
January 1986, was the first commerciallyavailable Bézier curve editing software for
a personal computer. High quality fontsin PostScript format could be developed
for a fraction of the cost of other existingmethods, leading to the democratization of
type design. For the first time, numerousself-taught type designers without sub-
stantial capital investment could producefonts for professional use. Fontographer
2.0 was released eight months later, in the
fall of 1986. In 1989, Fontographer 3.0 was
released, featuring an auto-trace tool andautomatic generation of hints for PostScript
printer fonts.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/
Fontographer
27 For Loop
```
In computer science, a for-loop (or
```
```
simply for loop) is a control flow statement
```
for specifying iteration, which allows codeto be executed repeatedly. A for-loop has
two parts: a header specifying the iteration,and a body which is executed once per
iteration. The header often declares anexplicit loop counter or loop variable, which
allows the body to know which iteration isbeing executed. For-loops are typically used
when the number of iterations is knownbefore entering the loop. For-loops can be
thought of as a shorthand for while-loops,
which increment and test a loop variable.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/
For_loop
28 Fortran
Fortran is a general-purpose, com-piled imperative programming language
that is especially suited to numeric com-putation and scientific computing. It was
originally developed by John Backus andIBM in the 1950s for scientific and engineer-
ing applications, and subsequently cameto dominate scientific computing. It has
been in use for over six decades in compu-tationally intensive areas such as numerical
weather prediction, finite element analysis,computational fluid dynamics, geophysics,
computational physics, crystallography andcomputational chemistry.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fortran
29 Generative Adversarial
```
Network (GAN)A generative adversarial network is a
```
class of machine learning frameworks. Twoneural networks contest with each other
```
in a game (in the form of a zero-sum game,where one agent’s gain is another agent’s
```
```
loss). Given a training set, this techniquelearns to generate new data with the same
```
statistics as the training set. For example, aGAN trained on photographs can generate
new photographs that look at least super-ficially authentic to human observers,
having many realistic characteristics.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/
Generative_adversarial_
network
30 GitGit is a software that tracks changes
in any set of files. It is generally used forcoordinating work among programmers
who are collaboratively developing sourcecode during software development. Its goals
include speed, data integrity, and supportfor distributed, non-linear workflows
```
(thousands of parallel branches running ondifferent systems). Git was created by Linus
```
Torvalds in 2005 for development of theLinux kernel, with other kernel developers
contributing to its initial development.Since 2005, Junio Hamano has been the
core maintainer. As with most otherdistributed version control systems, and
unlike most client-server systems, every Gitdirectory on every computer is a full-fledged
repository with complete history and fullversion-tracking abilities, independent
of network access or a central server.
Git is free and open-source softwaredistributed under GNU General Public
License Version 2.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Git

net hosting for software development andversion control using Git. It offers the

37 HTMLHyperText Markup Language,
better known as HTML, is the standardmarkup language for documents designed
to be displayed in a web browser. It can
```
be supported by technologies such asCascading Style Sheets (CSS) and scripting
```
languages such as JavaScript. Web browsersreceive HTML documents from a web
server or from local storage and render thedocuments into multimedia web pages.
HTML describes the structure of a webpage semantically and originally included
cues for the appearance of the document.HTML can embed programs written in
a scripting language such as JavaScript,which affects the behavior and content of
web pages. Inclusion of CSS defines the lookand layout of content.
In 1980, physicist Tim Berners-Lee,a contractor at CERN, proposed and proto-
typed ENQUIRE, a system for CERNresearchers to use and share documents. In
1989, Berners-Lee wrote a memo propos-ing an Internet-based hypertext system.
Berners-Lee specified HTML and wrote thebrowser and server software in late 1990.
• en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTML
17 WHAT IS <GOOGLE>?If you could be any one, which one would you be?
The number one Google search option is “Who is Google?” andthe same question has been asked over and over again in various
situations. However, there might never be an answer.There are so many companies out there today and in the future
that will be a Google. This question was asked byThe Economist in 2000: Who will be the next search engine?
The winner was... not Google. It was Ask Jeeves. Since then thecompany, founded in 1995 and owned by IAC, made its mark
on the web but was bought several times and merged intoAsk Inc. in 2007. There were reports that Jeeves was planning a
comeback. But Google came to the rescue to make it happen.It acquired Ask.com for $167 million US dollars. According to
The Economist the question has been answered.
